pres1.title: Operationalising Community Engagement as an Implementation Strategy for HIV-Associated Fungal Infection Research: The IMPRINT Multicountry Model
pres1.abstract: Background: HIV-associated fungal infections such as cryptococcal meningitis remain a significant cause of AIDS-related deaths in low- and middle-income countries. Despite the availability of effective diagnostics and treatments, their impact is limited by barriers including delayed presentation, low treatment literacy, and insufficient involvement of patient perspectives. The IMPRINT consortium uses structured community engagement to enhance the acceptability and relevance of interventions for HIV-associated fungal infections. By addressing behavioural and health system factors affecting cryptococcal infection management, IMPRINT bridges the gap between host and pathogen interaction. Methods: IMPRINT applied a standardised community engagement approach in seven countries across Africa and South East Asia, forming Community Advisory Boards (CABs) as governance bodies. Multilingual facilitation and treatment literacy tools supported CAB activities, which included protocol reviews, feedback sessions, and qualitative data collection. Knowledge gains were measured with pre- and post-training questionnaires, and engagement outcomes were tracked through protocol amendments and implementation changes. Regional workshops further strengthened capacity in governance and planning. Results: CAB members showed improved understanding of cryptococcosis, research processes, and patient rights. Community feedback led to protocol updates, including better consent processes and tailored screening and treatment. Outputs included advocacy briefs and stakeholder dialogues supporting access to diagnostics and therapies. Capacity strengthening improved CAB workplans and coordination, enhancing research acceptability and feasibility. Conclusions: IMPRINT’s measurable engagement strategy proves effective in multinational HIV-associated fungal infection research. Integrating patient perspectives improves intervention relevance and delivery, supporting responsive research and implementation in high-burden regions.

pres2.title: Low CSF glucose is associated with increased CNS inflammation in adult patients with HIV-associated cryptococcal meningitis: a prospective cohort study.
pres2.abstract: Background: Low cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) glucose is a prognostic marker in several meningitis etiologies including tuberculosis, but its role in HIV-associated cryptococcal meningitis is less defined. We investigated if CSF glucose is associated with cryptococcal outcomes. Methods We conducted a prospective cohort study of 1,103 adults with HIV-associated cryptococcal meningitis in Uganda from January 2019 to May 2025. We grouped participants by CSF glucose into tertiles: low <45 mg/dL n=311, medium 45-74 mg/dL n=414, and high >75 mg/dL n=361. We assessed for baseline associations with CSF parameters and outcomes. Results Participants with low CSF glucose were more likely to present with altered mental status (GCS<15; P=0.001), higher CSF white cells (P<0.0001), and higher CSF protein (P<0.0001). Sixty percent (60%) of participants with low glucose had CSF white cell counts >5, compared to 45% and 28% among those with medium and high glucose, respectively (P<0.001). Flow cytometry (n=189) revealed that the low CSF glucose group had 4-fold more CSF CD4 cells (xÃÖ=624 vs 160 cells/mL, P=0.024), more PD-1+ CD4 exhaustion (67% vs 49%, P<0.001), and lower monocyte activation (HLA-DR+, CD163+) compared to CSF glucose >45 mg/dL. CSF glucose showed a weak inverse correlation with baseline fungal burden (r=-0.10; P=0.0016), but glucose was not associated with in-hospital mortality, CSF early fungicidal activity, or CSF sterility. Conclusion Low CSF glucose was associated with severe clinical disease and CNS inflammatory response. A variety of mechanisms likely contribute to low CSF glucose, including increased metabolic activity in the brain due to infiltrating leukocytes and microbes.

pres3.title: Validation of the FungiXpert Cryptococcal Capsular Polysaccharide K-Set Lateral Flow Assay
pres3.abstract: Background: Cryptococcal antigen (CrAg) testing is important for the screening and early diagnosis of cryptococcal meningitis. However, there is currently only one CrAg testing kit with FDA approval—the Immy CrAg lateral flow assay (LFA). Here we analyze the specificity and sensitivity of the FungiXpert Cryptococcal Capsular Polysaccharide K-Set LFA by Genobio Pharmaceutical. Methods: We retrospectively tested 200 cryopreserved serum samples collected from 200 participants between 2020 and 2025 in Uganda. Individuals had HIV seropositivity and suspected meningitis and were enrolled in the ACACIA clinical study. FungiXpert CrAg LFA testing was conducted in accordance with manufacturer’s instructions and read by three blinded observers. Results: When using the IMMY CrAg LFA test as a reference standard, the FungiXpert CrAg LFA had a sensitivity of 95.9% [95% confidence interval (CI) 92.1-98.2%] and a specificity of 75.0% [95% CI 19.4-99.4%]. When compared to the results of the IMMY Semi-Quantitative CrAg LFA, all false negatives occurred in samples classified as low titer. Adjusting for the cryptococcal antigenemia prevalence in Uganda of 5.7%, the FungiXpert CrAg LFA had a 18.8% positive predictive value and 99.7% negative predictive value in serum. Conclusion: This is the first independent evaluation of the FungiXpert CrAg LFA kit. We found that the FungiXpert kit had a high sensitivity and suboptimal specificity compared to the IMMY CrAg LFA. Reduced performance in low-titer samples highlights the need for further evaluation during early or asymptomatic infection and other clinical contexts.

pres4.title: Mortality among individuals with subclinical cryptococcal meningitis identified through cryptococcal antigen (CrAg) screening programs in South Africa and Tanzania
pres4.abstract: BACKGROUND: Up to 40% of blood cryptococcal antigen (CrAg)-positive individuals have subclinical cryptococcal meningitis (CM), yet clinical outcomes for this understudied subgroup remain unclear. While current guidelines recommend intensive treatment for all individuals with CM, evidence is needed to inform risk-stratified management strategies. Methods In South Africa and Tanzania, we prospectively followed-up 2 cohorts of adults with CD4 counts <100 cells/¬µl: 1) subclinical CM (CSF CrAg-positive) and 2) symptomatic CM. Baseline characteristics and outcomes over 6 months were recorded. Results From November 2022 to October 2025, 62 adults with subclinical CM and 364 with symptomatic CM were enrolled. Median age was 39 years. Seven days of amphotericin B plus flucytosine was the most commonly administered induction treatment in both cohorts (71% and 67%, respectively). Subclinical CM cohort: 68% (42/62) were ART-experienced. Median baseline opening pressure (OP) was 15cmH2O (IQR: 11-27, n=32). Two-week, 10-week and 6-month mortality was 8.1% (95%CI: 2.7-17.8, 5/62), 35.5% (95%CI: 23.7-48.7, 22/62) and 40.3% (95%CI: 28.1-53.6, 25/62), respectively. Symptomatic CM cohort: 58% (201/344) were ART-experienced. Median baseline OP was 28cmH2O (IQR: 18-40, n=78). Two-week, 10-week and 6-month mortality was 21.7% (95%CI: 17.6-26.3, 79/364), 40.9% (95%CI: 35.8-46.2, 149/364) and 44.5% (95%CI: 39.3-49.8, 162/364), respectively. Discussion The majority of opening pressures were normal at baseline among the subclinical CM patients, with fewer CM-related deaths than in the symptomatic cohort, as indicated by a lower 2-week mortality. However, 10-week and 6-month mortality was similar between the cohorts. Alternative risk-stratified management approaches may be considered for these patient groups.

pres5.title: Predictors of 90-Day Mortality Across Immune Groups in Adults with Cryptococcal Meningoencephalitis
pres5.abstract: Background and Aims Mortality predictors in cryptococcal meningoencephalitis (CM) across HIV, solid organ transplant (SOT), and non-HIV, non-transplant (NHNT) adults are unclear. We compared clinical characteristics and 90-day mortality by immune status. Methods We identified adults (18 years) with CM in TriNetX (2003–2024) using ICD-10-CM codes or cerebrospinal fluid confirmation. Analyses were restricted to amphotericin B–treated patients. Patients were categorized as HIV, SOT, or NHNT. Clinical characteristics, immunosuppressive exposures, and outcomes were compared by 90-day mortality, with time-to-event analyses using Kaplan–Meier and Cox models. Results Among 766 patients, 26% had HIV, 22% had prior SOT, and 52% were NHNT. Ninety-day mortality was 19% overall (23% in NHNT, 20% in SOT, and 13% in HIV). In HIV-associated CM, non-survivors more often had hematologic malignancy (14% vs 2%) and liver disease (18% vs 3%). In SOT recipients, non-survivors had higher rates of hematologic malignancy (14% vs 3%), aplastic anemia (48% vs 10%), and ICU admission (66% vs 25%). Among NHNT patients, non-survivors were older (63 vs 56 years), more likely to have neoplastic disease (39% vs 23%), and require ICU care (55% vs 28%). Immunosuppressive exposures were similar across groups. NHNT status was associated with increased 90-day mortality (HR 1.98) in unadjusted analysis, but attenuated after adjustment for age, comorbidity, and glucocorticoids, with age remaining associated with death (HR 1.02/year). Kaplan–Meier curves demonstrated early, persistent separation by immune status. Conclusions Higher mortality in NHNT adults reflects host fragility rather than immune status, supporting severity-based risk stratification.

pres6.title: Clinical Phenotypes and Immunosuppressive Medication Exposures in Pulmonary Versus Extrapulmonary Cryptococcosis Among Non-HIV, Non-Transplant Adults: A U.S. Claims Analysis
pres6.abstract: Background and Aims Cryptococcosis in non-HIV, non-transplant (NHNT) adults is increasingly recognized yet clinically heterogeneous. We characterized clinical features of NHNT cryptococcosis and evaluated associations between disease phenotype, comorbidities, immunosuppressive medication exposures, and outcomes. Methods NHNT adults (18years) with ICD-10-CM cryptococcosis codes in Merative MarketScan (2016–2023) were classified as having pulmonary or extrapulmonary disease. Immunosuppressant, systemic glucocorticoid, and antineoplastic (WHO ATC classification) exposures were assessed 180 days before diagnosis. Outcomes included ICU admission, duration of hospitalization, and discharge disposition. Results Among 722 NHNT adults, 52% had extrapulmonary cryptococcosis. Extrapulmonary disease occurred in younger patients with greater multimorbidity than pulmonary disease (81% vs 62%, p<0.001). Only 5% lacked comorbidities, compared with 12% of pulmonary cases. Autoimmune and immune-mediated diseases were more frequent in extrapulmonary disease (20% vs 16%), while chronic lung disease was less common (9% vs 21%). Immunosuppressant exposure was higher in extrapulmonary cases (26% vs 11%, p<0.001), whereas antineoplastic exposure did not differ (10% vs 13%). Glucocorticoid exposure was similar (57% vs 60%, p=0.560), predominantly prednisone. Cumulative prednisone-equivalent dose was similar between extrapulmonary and pulmonary cases (median 600 vs 230mg, p=0.072), but extrapulmonary cases had longer prednisone exposure (median 60 vs 27days, p=0.025). Extrapulmonary cryptococcosis was associated with higher ICU admission (43% vs 18%), longer hospitalization (median 16 vs 7days), and fe wer home-based discharges (59% vs 21%) (all p<0.05). Conclusions Extrapulmonary cryptococcosis in NHNT adults is associated with greater immunosuppressive medication exposure, multimorbidity, and healthcare utilization, illustrating the need for improved awareness of this evolving at-risk population.

pres7.title: 15 years on: how the changing HIV treatment landscape has impacted cryptococcal meningitis rates in Cape Town
pres7.abstract: Background: Cryptococcal meningitis (CM) is a life-threatening opportunistic infection, typically associated with advanced HIV. This study compares the epidemiology of CM in a high HIV-prevalence setting in South Africa with a study in a similar population 15 years ago. Methods: Retrospective clinical and laboratory information was collected for all adults who underwent a lumbar puncture at Mitchells Plain District Hospital between January 2022 and December 2023. Clinical episodes of CM were identified and analysed using descriptive statistics. Results were compared to a 2010 cross-sectional analysis by Jarvis et. al in a geographically-similar population. Results: 2022 clinical presentations were captured. 262 episodes of microbiologically-confirmed meningitis were recorded of which CM accounted for 34% (88/262); compared to 63% in Jarvis et. al (2010). Most CM episodes (95%) were confirmed by CrAg LFA compared to only 40% in 2010. In this study, 99% (87/88) of CM cases were HIV-positive. Although 84% were ART-experienced, only 23% (19/83) were reportedly adherent to ART at presentation. CM mortality was 37% (30/87) overall. Although reflex serum CrAg screening was in place, only 39% (34/88) had a serum CrAg result prior to admission of which 68% (23/34) were serum CrAg positive. Conclusion: CM occurred predominantly in patients with advanced HIV with a median CD4 count of 34 cells/uL. While prior ART-exposure in CM was high, most patients had interrupted ART prior to CM. In comparison to the Jarvis et al. (2010) cohort, availability of CrAg CSF testing has improved with almost all CM cases confirmed with CrAg LFA.

pres8.title: Comparison of Bruker Biotyper Sirius, VITEK MS PRIME, and ASTA MicroIDSys MALDI-TOF MS Systems for Cryptococcus Species Identification Across Extraction Protocols
pres8.abstract: Background and aims: Accurate and rapid identification of Cryptococcus spp. is critical for patient care. Matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry (MALDI-TOF MS) enables yeast identification; however, its performance varies with instrument and database coverage. We compared the performance of three MALDI-TOF MS systems—Bruker Biotyper Sirius (Sirius; Bruker Daltonics), bioM?©rieux VITEK MS PRIME (PRIME; bioM?©rieux), and ASTA MicroIDSys (ASTA; ASTA Inc.)—in detecting Cryptococcus species across extraction protocols. Methods: In total, 51 C. neoformans, two C. deuterogattii, five Vanrija humicola, and one Naganishia liquefaciens clinical isolates were analyzed. Each isolate was first tested after on-plate formic acid extraction; isolates failing identification were retested after repeat on-plate and in-tube extractions. Results were categorized as correct, incomplete, incorrect, or no identification against ribosomal DNA sequencing results. Results: After initial on-plate extraction, correct identification rates were 86.4% for Sirius, 88.1% for PRIME, and 69.5% for ASTA. After repeat on-plate extraction, the accuracy of ASTA increased to 81.4% (P = 0.016). Additional in-tube extraction did not significantly improve identification results across platforms, although PRIME outperformed ASTA (P = 0.012). All platforms reliably classified C. neoformans (correct identification rates: 94%–96%) after additional on-plate extraction; discrepancies occurred mainly in uncommon species, possibly due to gaps in database coverage. Conclusions: Sirius and PRIME demonstrated robust performance for C. neoformans identification and C. gattii complex differentiation, outperforming ASTA for relatively rare species, owing to differences in database coverage. Single on-plate extraction is sufficient in clinical practice; however, continued database expansion is essential to maximize MALDI-TOF MS performance.

pres9.title: Advanced HIV disease complicated by cryptococcal meningitis, disseminated tuberculosis, severe drug-induced liver injury, and heart failure.
pres9.abstract: Background: Advanced HIV disease (AHD) remains a major cause of morbidity and mortality in sub-Saharan Africa, frequently complicated by overlapping opportunistic infections and treatment-related toxicities. Managing concurrent cryptococcal meningitis and tuberculosis in profoundly immunosuppressed patients is particularly challenging, with high risk of sepsis, drug toxicities, and death. Case Presentation: We report a 35-year-old female, one year postpartum, with a 20-year history of HIV infection who defaulted antiretroviral therapy for one year and presented to Mulago National Referral Hospital, Uganda, with fever, cough, chronic diarrhea, weight loss, genital ulcer herpes, and confusion. She was diagnosed with cryptococcal meningitis and disseminated tuberculosis, with profound immunosuppression (CD4 20 cells/¬µL). She was initiated on amphotericin B-based induction therapy, flucytosine, high-dose fluconazole, and anti-tuberculosis treatment. Her hospital course was com plicated by acute respiratory deterioration secondary to severe pneumonia, septic shock, cardiogenic shock with new-onset dilated cardiomyopathy and heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (EF 35-40%), amphotericin-associated ototoxicity, and severe drug-induced liver injury (DILI). Hepatotoxic drugs, which included anti-TBs were temporarily withheld, then successfully reintroduced using a stepwise re-challenge DILI protocol with close biochemical monitoring. Heart failure was managed with guideline-directed therapy. Antiretroviral therapy was restarted after stabilization. The patient was discharged after 51 days and remained clinically stable and adherent to therapy at 18-week follow-up. Conclusion: This case highlights the extreme clinical complexity of AHD management in resource-limited settings and demonstrates that survival is achievable despite multiple life-threatening complications.

pres10.title: Delayed presentation to care among Ugandan adults with HIV-associated cryptococcal meningitis: a prospective cohort study
pres10.abstract: Background: Mortality from cryptococcal meningitis remains high between 10%-25% at two weeks within clinical trials. We sought to determine whether participants with cryptococcal meningitis who present late to care have worse clinical outcomes compared with participants who do not present late to care. Methods: We analyzed data from a cohort of hospitalized adults with HIV-associated cryptococcal meningitis. Delayed presentation to care was defined as having a headache for >21 days at the time of hospitalization. We used logistic regression to investigate the association with delayed care-seeking, and its effect on length of hospital stay. Cox proportional hazards ratio was conducted to assess the association between delayed care seeking and in-hospital mortality. Results: Among 1,032 participants with cryptococcal meningitis, the median CD4 cell count was 28 cells/¬µL and the median duration of headache prior to hospitalization was 14 days (IQR: 7-29). Overall, 26% of part icipants presented to hospital with >21 days of headache. When comparing those with >21 days of headache versus those with <21 days of headache, participants who were on antiretroviral therapy were more likely to delay seeking care (AOR= 1.43, p-value= 0.016). There was no significant difference in hospital duration (p=0.329) and two-week mortality (p=0.088) between those who presented late to care versus those who did not. Conclusions: Late presentation to care is common, with 26% of participants presenting with >21 days of headache. There was no difference in two-week mortality and length of hospitalization between participants with >21 days of headache versus <21 days of headache.

pres11.title: Cost of cryptococcal meningitis hospitalization among adults with advanced HIV disease in Uganda
pres11.abstract: Background: Cryptococcal meningitis is a leading cause of hospitalization and death among people with advanced HIV disease. We estimated the cost of cryptococcal meningitis hospitalization among adults with advanced HIV in Uganda. Methods: We conducted a micro-costing analysis among adults with advanced HIV hospitalized for cryptococcal meningitis within a trial (NCT05085171) conducted in Kampala, Uganda (May 2022–January 2025). Participant-specific resource use (length of stay, antifungal medications, and treatments for co-occurring conditions) was extracted from case report forms. Standard inpatient resource use (personnel, supplies, diagnostics, and lumbar punctures) was estimated using hospital-level data and national treatment guidelines. Costs were summarized in 2025 US Dollars (USD). Results: Among 1,429 outpatients with advanced HIV, 22 (1.5%) were hospitalized for cryptococcal meningitis during six-month follow-up. Median length of stay was 11 days (IQR 7–15). Median total cost of hospitalization was 969 USD (IQR 647–1366). Antifungal medications accounted for the largest proportion of total costs. Specifically, 47% of hospitalization costs were attributed to flucytosine, and 15% were attributed to liposomal amphotericin B. Personnel salaries (15%), inpatient bed costs (7.4%), and monitoring labs (6.9%) contributed smaller shares of total cost. Conclusions: Cryptococcal meningitis hospitalization imposes a substantial financial burden on the Ugandan healthcare system, with costs dominated by flucytosine. The average cost of a cryptococcal meningitis hospitalization is comparable to screening over 270 people for cryptococcal antigenemia, highlighting the potential for early screening and pre-emptive treatment to reduce mortality and healthcare system costs among people with advanced HIV.

pres12.title: Antifungal susceptibility profile of Cryptococcus spp. isolated from patients with neurocryptococcosis in Brazil.
pres12.abstract: Cryptococcosis is frequently associated with meningoencephalitis in immunocompromised individuals and high mortality. Due to the limited availability of treatment and increasing clinical resistance, understanding the susceptibility profile of circulating strains is essential. The aim of this work was to evaluate the susceptibility profile of Cryptococcus isolated from patients in five Brazilian States. A total of 252 isolates were evaluated, 193 identified as C. neoformans and 59 as C. gattii. Susceptibility testing revealed intermediate resistance to flucytosine (5-FC) in two isolates, and resistance to fluconazole (FCZ) in six isolates. MIC evaluated from sequential samples from the same patient showed differences in susceptibility, suggesting a transient resistance or coinfection with different strains. Three C. gattii isolates showed simultaneous resistance to 5-FC and FCZ. For amphotericin B, no elevated MIC values were observed. Exposure of a susceptible isolate to 5- FC induced the expression of FCY2, responsible for drug internalization, while FCY1 and FUR1, involved in 5-FC intracellular activation, were downregulated. FCZ exposure upregulated the efflux pump genes AFR1 and MDR1, whereas ERG11, involved in ergosterol synthesis, remained highly expressed, possibly as a compensatory mechanism to maintain ergosterol production. Two resistant isolates showed high expression of AFR1 and MDR1, in the absence of drugs. These findings indicate that surveillance of resistance is essential to evaluate treatment of cryptococcosis and clinical susceptibility testing should be monitored during treatment to detect a resistance phenotype. Further studies will provide insights into the differences in sequential isolates from the same patient and the underlying resistance mechanisms.

pres13.title: Birth and clinical outcomes of pregnant women with Cryptococcal Antigenemia in outpatient clinics in Uganda
pres13.abstract: Introduction: Clinical trials for the treatment and prophylaxis for cryptococcal meningitis have historically excluded pregnant persons. As a result, clinical and pregnancy outcomes for pregnant persons with HIV-associated cryptococcal infection are unknown. We evaluated clinical and pregnancy outcomes among pregnant participants with cryptococcal antigenemia. Methods: We prospectively enrolled adults with advanced HIV disease (defined as CD4≤200 cells/¬µL) within the ENCORE clinical trial (NCT05085171). Enrollment is ongoing at 10 of the 26 participating outpatient HIV clinics in Uganda. We present an analysis of baseline characteristics, opportunistic infections, birth outcomes, and clinical outcomes among pregnant participants. Results: Between May 2022 and April 2025, 114 pregnant participants with advanced HIV disease were enrolled, of whom 45% were antiretroviral therapy na?Øve, median CD4 cell count was 124 cells/¬µL, and 42% were in their second trimester. Pregnant participants were less likely to be CrAg positive (2% vs. 7%, p=0.023) and less likely to be TB LAM positive (11% vs. 28%, p<0.001) compared with non-pregnant female participants. One CrAg-positive pregnant participant was enrolled at eight weeks gestation, received fluconazole for 24 weeks and delivered at term. The other participant was CrAg SQ 3+ at 16 weeks gestation, received 5 doses of weekly Amphotericin B deoxycholate (1mg/kg) without fluconazole, and delivered at term. Conclusions: Among pregnant participants with advanced HIV disease, prevalence of cryptococcal antigenemia was 2%, significantly lower than that of non-pregnant participants. Antifungal therapy was used with favourable maternal outcomes.

pres14.title: Baseline CMV Viraemia and Neurocognitive Performance Among Cryptococcal Meningitis Survivors
pres14.abstract: Background: CMV viraemia is highly prevalent among adults with advanced HIV disease and is associated with increased mortality. We sought to investigate the association between baseline CMV viraemia and neurocognitive performance among Ugandan adults with HIV-associated cryptococcal meningitis. Methods: We enrolled a prospective cohort of Ugandans with cryptococcal meningitis between August 2021 and April 2024, and quantified baseline CMV viremia via polymerase chain reaction on cryopreserved plasma. We assessed neurocognitive function among survivors at 3 and 12 months using a composite quantitative neurocognitive performance z-score (QNPZ). Results: Among 223 survivors diagnosed with HIV-associated cryptococcal meningitis, 26% (57/223) had baseline CMV viraemia. At enrollment, individuals with CMV viraemia presented with significantly lower CD4 count 16 [IQR 6,47] vs. 25 [IQR 9,70] cells/mm3, lower cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) white blood cell count 4 [IQR4,65] vs. 45 [IQR 4,130] cells/mm3, and lower CSF protein 52 [IQR 29,100] vs. 100 [IQR 40,138] mg/dL. There was no difference in overall neurocognitive performance (QNPZ-8) at 3 or 12 months between participants with vs. without baseline CMV viraemia. Individuals with CMV viraemia had similar QNPZ-8 scores compared with those without CMV viraemia at 3 months -0.9 [IQR -01.2, 0.5] vs -0.9 [IQR -1.3, -0.4], p=0.62and 12 months -0.5[ IQR -0.7, 0.0] vs. -0.6 [IQR -0.9, -0.1], p=0.22. Conclusion: In HIV-associated cryptococcal meningitis, individuals with baseline CMV viraemia demonstrated reduced baseline CSF inflammatory responses; however, this did not translate into differences in neurocognitive performance at 3 or 12 months post cryptococcal meningitis diagnosis among survivors.

pres15.title: Diagnostic Challenge of Opportunistic Infections in Advanced HIV Disease: A Case of Disseminated Cryptococcosis.
pres15.abstract: Background: Cryptococcosis, a deadly opportunistic fungal infection, can present as disseminated disease, including Cryptococcal Meningitis (CM) in immunocompromised patients. However, disseminated disease may present with non-specific signs and symptoms, evading standard screening algorithms, including serum cryptococcal antigen (CrAg) testing, yielding false-negative results due to the prozone effect. Methods: We report a case of disseminated cryptococcosis with soft tissue and pulmonary involvement in advanced HIV disease (AHD) patients diagnosed at postmortem. Results: A 30-year-old female, newly diagnosed with HIV, not on ART with CD4 of 04 cells/¬µL, presented with a 2-month history of easy fatiguability associated with chest pain, non-productive cough, and dyspnea. On general examination, she was sick-looking with respiratory distress, wasted, and moderate pallor, without focal neurological deficits. Laboratory investigations revealed Grade 4 thrombocytopenia (32 ?ó10+/L) and liver transaminitis. Urine TB lipoarabinomannan and serum cryptococcal antigen were negative. The chest radiograph showed bilateral hilar adenopathy. She was managed presumptively for Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia with high-dose dapsone. Despite supportive care, she passed on after 10 days’ hospitalization. Upon autopsy, gross examination of the lungs revealed heavy, consolidated lungs and subarachnoid clouding of the meninges, showing pulmonary parenchymal disease and meningitis. Microscopic examination of Periodic Acid Schiff stained sections revealed extensive involvement by cryptococcosis of the brain, lungs, spleen, kidneys, adrenal glands, heart, and pancreas. Conclusion: The case highlights diagnostic limitations of serum CrAg screening tests in AHD with non-specific systemic illness. Disseminated cryptococcosis may remain clinically unrecognized until death. Strengthening diagnostic algorithms for opportunistic infections in AHD is crucial.

pres16.title: Disseminated HIV-Associated Cryptococcosis with Pulmonary Embolism in Pregnancy: A case report
pres16.abstract: Case Presentation Cryptococcal meningitis (CM) is a leading cause of mortality in pregnant patients with Advanced HIV disease. Despite the updated WHO treatment guidelines for CM following the AMBITION trial, there is still a gap in treatment of CM in special populations like pregnant women due to the well-established teratogenic effects antifungal medication. We present a detailed case of a newly diagnosed HIV-1 pregnant patient with cryptococcal meningitis, pulmonary embolism. A 28-year-old female, Gravida 2 para1+0 at 27 weeks of gestation with advanced HIV disease (CD4 count of 5 cells/¬µL). She was hospitalized with concerns of a persistent headache associated with neck stiffness. She also reported history of difficulty in breathing and low-grade fevers. She complained of generalized body weakness and an ulcer in the left axilla. The patient reported to have attended antenatal care once during the current pregnancy. She was in an HIV sero-discordant relationship. Discussion The management of CM in pregnancy is complex as there are no standard treatment guidelines. Patient was treated using intravenous liposomal amphotericin B at 3 mg/kg for 10 days was instituted. The Lumbar Puncture was performed had an opening pressure of 170mmH20 and the CSF analysis confirmed the presence of 3,000 Colony Forming Units (CFUs). On Day 7, the culture results revealed no growth. Baseline CD4 count was 5 cells/ ¬µL, Despite adequate therapy, the patient died. An autopsy revealed a mildly enlarged liver, congested right lung, gallstones. Histopathology revealed focal cystic areas in the kidneys containing blastoconidia of Cryptococcus

pres17.title: AUTOPSY FINDINGS IN PERSONS DIAGNOSED WITH HIV-ASSOCIATED CRYPTOCOCCAL MENINGITIS.
pres17.abstract: Introduction: Data on autopsy findings among persons with cryptococcal meningitis (CM) remain limited, particularly in advanced HIV disease. Methods: We conducted autopsies among consented participants with CM enrolled in a prospective cohort in Kampala, Uganda from January to September 2025. Autopsy findings were correlated with perimortem clinical records from hospitalization. Results: Twenty-four autopsies were reviewed; 58% (14/24) were female. Median (IQR) age was 40 years (30–46), and median CD4 count was 5 cells/¬µL (0–25). One cadaver had a gravid uterus (20 cm) with a single deceased fetus, and one was 18 days postpartum. All cadavers demonstrated brain pathology, including hazy meninges (n=24) consistent with meningitis, features of elevated intracranial pressure (n=18) with edema, narrow sulci and flattened gyri; parenchymal necrosis (n=4), herniation (n=3), and hemorrhage (n=2). All also had pulmonary pathology, including edema (n=24), consolidations (n=17), pleural effusions (n=2), collapse (n=1), cavities (n=1), and granulomas (n=1), although only 37.5% (9/24) had a perimortem diagnosis of respiratory distress or aspiration pneumonia. Lymphadenopathy was present in 33% (8/24), with cryptococcal cysts identified histologically in one case. Renal pathology was common (17/24), including cryptococcal cysts (n=9), focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (n=7) , and caseating granulomas (n=1). Although 29% (7/24) had antemortem diagnoses of tuberculosis, only one case was histopathologically confirmed. Aspergillosis and Kaposi’s sarcoma were identified in one case each. Conclusions: Beyond extensive CNS involvement, asymptomatic pulmonary, renal, and lymphatic pathology is common in HIV-associated cryptococcal meningitis. Antemortem presumptive tuberculosis diagnoses frequently lacked histopathologic confirmation. Safe feeding practices are needed to reduce aspiration.

pres18.title: Evaluation of laboratory checklists for protocol-scheduled testing in HIV-associated meningitis clinical studies
pres18.abstract: Background and aims: Clinical studies are vital in improving outcomes for individuals with HIV-associated meningitis, but often face protocol deviations during biospecimen collection, such as missed or poorly documented samples. This study evaluated the effectiveness of a laboratory checklist in improving adherence to protocol-specified testing in an ongoing HIV-associated meningitis clinical study. Methods: A mixed-methods design was used at Mulago and Kiruddu Hospitals, Uganda. Staff surveys assessed checklist usability and workflow integration, while laboratory records were reviewed to compare adherence to sample collection schedules at days 0, 7, and 14 before and after checklist implementation. Results: Baseline adherence was 100%. Blood sample collection generally improved from 27% to 52% (p=0.003). Complete blood count (CBC) on day 14 increased from 27% to 50% (p=0.008). Baseline CD4 testing rose from 88% to 98% (p=0.021). Cerebrospinal fluid collection remained low (Day 7: 51% to 38%; Day 14: 19% to 22%). Among 21 staff surveyed, over half had 4–6 years of experience. Most reported prior checklist use (71%), formal training (62%), and confidence in checklist use (52%). Although 76% agreed that checklists reduce protocol deviations, challenges included limited time and inconsistent checklist availability (43%), with heavy workload being the main barrier (67%). Suggested improvements included workflow integration (81%), staff participation in development (76%), and regular training (62%). Conclusions: The laboratory checklist improved adherence to protocol-defined blood-based tests, particularly at follow-up visits. Sustainable use requires workflow integration, staff engagement, consistent availability, and ongoing training.

pres19.title: Challenges and missing links to optimize nursing care for persons with HIV-associated meningitis in a resource-constrained setting
pres19.abstract: Background and aims: Nursing care is a vital component of managing persons with HIV-associated meningitis. We sought to describe the challenges faced during the nursing care of these patients, highlighting the gaps and recommendations to optimize nursing care in a resource-constrained setting. Methods: We included and interviewed ten nurses as key informants who were routinely involved in providing care for persons with HIV-related meningitis during nine clinical studies conducted between 2010 and 2025. Results: The human resource and infrastructure limitations include understaffing, limited knowledge, insufficient supplies, lack of space for counselling, limited bed space in hospital wards, limited number of physiotherapists for rehabilitation, and poor patient tracking post-discharge. The clinical and treatment-related complications include delayed diagnosis/treatment, raised intracranial pressure, Immune Reconstitution Inflammatory Syndrome, relapse, poor infection prevention and control, pill burden and multiple drug side effects. The socio-economic limitations include limited patient education, lack of patients’ social support, myths/misconceptions about medical procedures, religious beliefs, HIV stigma and non-disclosure of HIV status, patient/caregiver challenges and poverty of patients. The major missing links include the need to recruit more nurses, enhance training programs for nurses, empower nurses to gain expanded skillsets, ensure resource availability, provide more bed space , improve infection prevention and control, promote community awareness on HIV-associated meningitis, reinforce adherence to treatment guidelines, training caretakers about medication management and patient hygiene, and mentor nurses in research. Conclusions: Patients with HIV-associated meningitis require extra nursing care, including community engagement. Addressing the missing links will help to improve treatment outcomes.

pres20.title: Incidence, predictors, and outcomes of re-hospitalisation among adults with HIV-associated cryptococcal meningitis in Uganda: A retrospective cohort study
pres20.abstract: Background and aims: While advances in Cryptococcal meningitis (CM) treatment have improved survival, post-discharge outcomes such as rehospitalisation remain poorly understood. We aimed to determine the incidence, predictors, causes, and outcomes of re-hospitalisation by 10 weeks among adults with HIV/CM. Methods: This was a retrospective cohort study analysing data from four clinical trials conducted between 2017 and 2024 at Mulago, Mbarara, and Kiruddu Hospitals in Uganda. Adults with HIV/CM discharged alive after initial hospitalisation were followed for 10 weeks. Results: Of 1055 patients enrolled in the four clinical trials, 787 were discharged alive. All participants were adults with a median age of 35 years (IQR=30,40). All participants had HIV with a median CD4 cell count of 21 cells/¬µL (IQR=8,58). The majority were male (59%), and 45% were ART-experienced. 209/787 (26.6%) experienced either rehospitalisation or death within 10 weeks. Seventy-six died without rehospitalisation, 25 died after rehospitalisation, 108 were alive by 10 weeks, and 578 had no event. GCS, duration on ART, Haemoglobin, CD4, sodium, CSF opening pressure >200, CSF protein, and CSF WBC were significantly different across the four groups. The median time from initial discharge to rehospitalisation was 20 days (IQR= 10, 36). The major cause of rehospitalisation was CM complications. Only normal GCS was significantly associated with rehospitalisation. However, age, GCS, CD4, haemoglobin and sodium were significantly associated with death. Conclusions: Nearly 3 in every 10 adults with HIV/CM experience rehospitalisation or death within 10 weeks following discharge, with the major cause of rehospitalisation being CM complications.

pres21.title: Missing links to optimise cryptococcosis diagnosis and outcomes in resource-constrained settings
pres21.abstract: Background and aims: Early diagnosis of Cryptococcal meningitis (CM) is crucial in improving treatment outcomes. Despite advances and the availability of modern and point-of-care diagnostics for cryptococcosis, gaps still exist in resource-constrained settings (RCS), leading to unfavourable treatment outcomes. We aimed to review the current outstanding issues or missing links that need to be filled to optimise the diagnosis of cryptococcosis in RCS to improve treatment outcomes. Methods: We highlight the missing links to optimise diagnosis and outcomes, including some practical recommendations. Results: The major missing links to optimise treatment outcomes among CM patients involve troubleshooting the limitations of fungal culture, addressing delayed diagnosis, developing more versatile diagnostics and improving access to the CrAg LFA, antifungal susceptibility testing and therapeutic drug monitoring. However, from RCS, we find that most facilities have a light microscope since it is still an essential tool in diagnosis. Trained human resource capacity in the identification of cryptococcal meningitis is still key. Procurement issues also affect the availability of tests, unless RCS are funded by outside sources. There remain issues of inequality and equity of resource allocation between the private vs government-run laboratories. The high cost of tests is still a limiting factor to access in RCS. Implementation of these tests in the RCS is also hindered by competing diseases and health systems of these settings, which in turn affect the diagnosis of CM. Conclusions: Major issues still exist in the diagnosis of cryptococcosis in RCS. Addressing them could help to improve treatment outcomes.

pres22.title: Headache as a predictor of meningitis in ambulatory patients with symptomatic HIV-associated cryptococcal antigenemia
pres22.abstract: Background: People with HIV-associated cryptococcal antigenemia are at high risk for meningitis and death. In resource-limited settings, lumbar puncture to evaluate for meningitis is infrequently performed in asymptomatic individuals; therefore, symptomatic individuals are prioritized. We evaluated the predictive value of meningeal symptoms for cryptococcal meningitis in people with cryptococcal antigenemia. Methods: We conducted a secondary analysis from three randomized clinical trials in Uganda. We included adults with meningeal symptoms who had cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) cryptococcal antigen (CrAg) testing performed. Logistic regression and classification trees were used to determine the associations between meningeal symptoms and meningitis. Area under the Receiver Operating Characteristic curve (AUROC) and misclassification rate were used to evaluate model performance. Results: Among 344 participants, median CrAg titer was 1:1280 (IQR 1:100,1:2560). Overall, 285 (83%) participants presented with headache and 205 (60%) had meningitis. Presence of headache was associated with meningitis (adjusted odds ratio 11.7, 95% Confidence Interval [CI] 5.23–28.50). The AUROC of headache alone to predict meningitis was 0.65 (95%CI 0.61–0.69), with 95% (195/205) sensitivity and 35% (49/139) specificity. The AUROC improved to 0.86 (95%CI 0.82–0.90) when stiff neck, photophobia, confusion, sex, and CrAg titer >1:160 were added to the model. In the final classification tree, headache combined with CrAg titer >1:160 had the highest probability (79%) of meningitis. Conclusions: Headache and high CrAg titer are the most reliable predictors for meningitis. In the absence of plasma CrAg titration, symptoms of headache, stiff neck, photophobia, and confusion predict meningitis for individuals with cryptococcal antigenemia.

pres23.title: CMV viremia is associated with impaired CSF fungal clearance amongst 509 Ugandan adults with HIV-associated cryptococcal meningitis
pres23.abstract: Background: Cryptococcal meningitis is the leading cause of HIV-associated meningitis with 10-week mortality of 25-30%. Cytomegalovirus (CMV) viremia occurs in 30-50% of adults with advanced HIV disease and is associated with increased mortality in cryptococcal meningitis through unclear mechanisms. We aimed to determine whether CMV viremia impairs CSF fungal clearance. Methods: We measured CMV DNA levels by quantitative PCR in baseline cryopreserved plasma from Ugandans with cryptococcal meningitis enrolled in clinical from 2010–2024. Amphotericin-based induction therapy evolved over time according to international standards. Early fungicidal activity (EFA) was calculated as the negative slope change in quantitative CSF fungal burden (log≥ CFU/mL/day), estimated by linear regression. We analyzed odds of achieving sterility and all-cause mortality through 18 weeks. Results: Of 509 Ugandan participants with EFA available, we found CMV viremia in 37% (187/509). CMV viremia was significantly associated with lower CD4 counts, lower hemoglobin, fewer CSF white cells, and higher baseline fungal burden (p<0.01 for all). Participants with CMV viremia (median EFA=0.31 [IQR, 0.17–0.44]) were less likely to sterilize CSF by Day 18 after antifungal therapy (36% vs 54%; p<0.001) as compared to participants without CMV viremia (median EFA=0.35 [IQR, 0.22–0.51]). The combination of CMV viremia and EFA<0.20 dramatically reduced the odds of achieving Day 18 CSF sterility (OR=0.01 [95%CI, 0.00–0.05]). Participants with CMV viremia had higher mortality at 18 weeks (43% vs 26%; p<0.001). Discussion: CMV viremia is associated with impaired CSF fungal clearance, providing a possible mechanism for why CMV viremia is associated with excess mortality in cryptococcosis.

pres24.title: Baseline hematologic abnormalities and survival in HIV-associated cryptococcal meningitis: a prospective cohort study
pres24.abstract: Background Cryptococcosis is the leading cause of meningitis among people living with HIV. Hematologic abnormalities, particularly cytopenia, are frequently observed at presentation; however, their prognostic significance remains poorly defined. We evaluated the association between baseline cytopenia and survival in HIV-associated cryptococcal meningitis. Methods We prospectively enrolled participants between 2013 and 2025 at three hospitals in Uganda. Cytopenia was defined according to the Division of AIDS toxicity grading criteria as grade >=2 abnormalities in >=2 hematologic cell lines (white blood cells, hemoglobin, or platelets) at baseline. We compared baseline clinical and laboratory characteristics between participants with and without cytopenia and evaluated the association between baseline cytopenia and 4-week mortality. Results Among 1,729 participants presenting with HIV-associated cryptococcal meningitis, the median age was 35 years (interquartile range [IQR], 30–42); 60% (1,035/1,729) were male, and 51% (883/1,729) were not receiving antiretroviral therapy at presentation. At baseline, 9% (149/1,729) had cytopenia, 12% (n=214) had leukopenia, 25% (n=432) had anemia, and 10% (n=167) had thrombocytopenia. Participants with cytopenia had lower median CD4 cell counts (9 cells/¬µL [IQR: 4-7] vs 23 cells/¬µL [IQR: 8-59], p<0.001), and lower median intracranial pressures (180 mm Hg [IQR: 111-245] vs 240 mm Hg [IQR: 150-350], p<0.001). Presence of cytopenia was associated with higher hazard of death at 4-weeks (adjusted-Hazard Ratio: 1.90; 95%CI, 1.45–2.48). Conclusion Baseline cytopenia is independently associated with substantially increased early mortality. These findings identify cytopenia as a readily available prognostic marker that may help risk-stratify individuals at presentation and inform the need for intensified monitoring.

pres25.title: Frequence and therapeutic impact of fluconazole resistance in Cryptococcus neoformans strains from French surveillance
pres25.abstract: The aim was to analyze clinical characteristics of cryptococcosis cases due to isolates resistant to fluconazole. Between January 2014 and August 2025, 777 cases were reported in France and 753 isolates from 663 patients were sent to the NRCMA for antifungal susceptibility testing and species identification. 26 isolates had fluconazole MIC >16mg/L, corresponding to 3.3% of the patients (22/663). Among these patients, 20 episodes from 20 patients were analysed. Sex ratio was 1.22 and mean age of 54.15. Main risk factors were HIV (7), solid organ transplant (n=7). Only one patient had been preexposed to fluconazole and one to caspofungin, 15 were na?Øve to azoles treatment. Majority of isolates belong to C. neoformans (n=16), followed by AD hybrid (n=3) and C. deneoformans (n=1). Mortality rate at 3 months was 20%. Among the 18 patients with disseminated or cerebral infection, 16 were first treated with amphotericin B plus flucytosine. Two patients died before the beginning of the treatment. The treatment was followed by a switch to fluconazole except for one patient who died before the beginning of fluconazole therapy. Data from cryptococcosis surveillance in France over a ten-year period allow us to conclude that the frequency of isolates considered resistant to fluconazole in vitro is low and is found in the three species of the C. neoformans complex, mainly in patients who have not been previously exposed to azoles. Treatment with fluconazole despite the presence of a strain with a high MIC does not result in treatment failure or significant mortality.

pres26.title: Identification of synergistic compounds in combination with fluconazole from the Prestwick Chemical Library® against Cryptococcus neoformans.
pres26.abstract: The treatment of Cryptococcus neoformans is limited due to its low susceptibility to fluconazole, compared to other fungal species, and intrinsic resistance to echinocandins, resulting in a restricted range of therapeutic options. In this context, drug repurposing emerges as a promising alternative strategy, as it consists of identifying new biological effects of drugs already used for other purposes, offering significant advantages in terms of cost, time, and safety compared to de novo drug development. In this study, a total of 1,520 off-patent, FDA-approved compounds from the Prestwick Chemical Library® were screened to identify agents with synergistic activity in combination with fluconazole against C. neoformans. An initial high-throughput screening allowed the selection of 12 promising compounds, including acetylsalicylic acid, pinaverium bromide, thioridazine hydrochloride, cianidol, risedronic acid monohydrate, tegaserod maleate, alendronate sodium, ibufenac, flurbiprofen axetil, oxiglutatione, atorvastatin, and avermectin B1. Synergistic interactions were subsequently confirmed by checkerboard assays using six C. neoformans clinical isolates. Synergy with fluconazole was validated for pinaverium bromide, thioridazine hydrochloride, alendronate sodium, oxiglutatione, ibufenac, atorvastatin, avermectin B1, tegaserod maleate, and risedronic acid monohydrate. To further assess the translational potential of these combinations, cytotoxicity was evaluated in RAW 264.7 macrophages, revealing toxicity at the highest tested concentrations of pinaverium bromide, tegaserod maleate, and thioridazine hydrochloride. In addition, in vivo safety assessment using Caenorhabditis elegans is currently being conducted to further characterize compound efficacy. Overall, these results highlight drug repurposing as a valuable approach for identifying novel antifungal combinations against C. neoformans.

pres27.title: False Positive IMMY® Serum Cryptococcal Antigen Lateral Flow Assay Results Due to Improper Use of Titration Buffer.
pres27.abstract: Background: False-positive results with the IMMY® cryptococcal antigen (CrAg) lateral flow assay (LFA) may result from procedural or reagent-related errors. Although the CrAg LFA is a reliable diagnostic tool for Cryptococcus, occasional false positives complicate interpretation, highlighting the need to identify their causes to maintain accuracy and avoid unnecessary treatment. Immunological factors such as antigen cross-reactivity, rheumatoid factor, or heterophilic antibodies, and technical errors including incorrect use of titration diluent, improper dilution, or use of the positive control as diluent, may lead to false-positive outcomes. Methods: Two patients who were initially reported as CrAg positive by a peripheral laboratory were referred to Kiruddu Hospital in Kampala, Uganda, for confirmatory testing. Repeat tests were conducted using specimen diluent following IMMY manufacturer’s protocol, as well as incorrectly using titration diluent only. Semi-quantitative CrAg LFA testing and control assays were performed to identify the source of error. Results: Repeat patients’ serum testing with the correct specimen diluent yielded negative results, whereas testing with only the titration diluent produced false positive bands. Serial dilutions confirmed persistent false positivity up to 1:80 using titration diluent, while all dilutions using specimen diluent remained negative at 1:5 titration. Conclusion: Incorrect use of titration diluent instead of specimen diluent can lead to false positive CrAg LFA results due to the absence of the blocking agent that neutralizes heterophilic antibodies. Technical laboratory error may be the cause of false positive results. Removal of titration diluent in situations where CrAg titers are not performed may improve real-world diagnostic performance.

pres28.title: Cryptococcosis drives hospitalisation and death in advanced HIV disease in Uganda
pres28.abstract: Background: Among people with HIV, 30% present with advanced HIV disease (defined as CD4 ≤200 cells/¬µL). Causes of hospitalisation, duration, and characteristics of those hospitalised are poorly described among people with advanced HIV disease. Methods: Among adults with advanced HIV disease enrolled into a cluster-randomised trial evaluating opportunistic infection screening and prophylaxis for people with advanced HIV disease (NCT05085171), we evaluated participants who were hospitalised during study participation. Participants were enrolled across 26 outpatient HIV clinics in Uganda from May 2022 to January 2025. Baseline characteristics, length of hospital stay, final diagnoses, and clinical outcomes were summarised descriptively. Fisher’s Exact Test was used to compare proportions. Results: Among 1429 participants with advanced HIV disease, 83 (6%) were hospitalised, with a median hospital stay of 7 days (IQR 4–14). Hospitalised individuals had a median CD4 cell count of 50 cells/¬µL (IQR 22-91 cells/¬µL), and 68% were ART-naive at enrollment. Overall, 40% (33/83) of hospitalised participants were plasma cryptococcal antigen (CrAg) positive at enrollment compared with 5% of non-hospitalised participants (p<0.0001). Hospitalised participants were more likely to be TB LAM positive (37% vs 27%, p=0.0569) and receiving active TB treatment (57% vs 38%, p=0.0007) compared with non-hospitalised participants. Most common causes of hospitalisation were TB (23/83; 27%) and cryptococcosis (22/83; 26%). Overall, 20% (17/83) died during hospitalisation, and an additional 13% (11/83) died within 30 days post-discharge. Conclusions: CrAg-positive individuals are at substantially higher risk for hospitalisation, and Cryptococcosis is a leading cause of hospitalisation among people with advanced HIV disease.

pres29.title: Serum Cryptococcus antigen testing: Results of an external quality control program 2022 - 2024
pres29.abstract: Cryptococcus antigen (CRAG) testing is essential for cryptococcosis management. Predictive value of semiquantitative titers in persons living with AIDS has been documented. Accuracy of semiquantitative titers in routine testing is difficult to assess. We describe an external quality assessment (EQA) program for accuracy of semiquantitative CRAG testing in non-specialized laboratories. In Germany, laboratories that provide CRAG testing reimbursed from health insurances are required to participate in an EQA program organized by Instand e.V., a German, non-profit interdisciplinary scientific medical society providing EQA programs for medical laboratories. Test samples are prepared using antigen from culture supernatants of C. neoformans H99 spiked into serum. CRAG titers vary between 1:80-1:2560. They are controlled before and after shipment to participants. They receive a positive and negative sample. The acceptable range is derived from the participants mean plus/minus two dilutions. We summarize results of participants using the IMMY lateral flow assay (lfa), the most commonly used assay. Between 68 and 80 (median 73) laboratories participate in the program. Use of lfa increased from 41% to 60% of participants during the observation period. The Median of participants semiquantitative results were within 1-2 dilutions steps from results of the EQA provider irrespective of sample transport. Between 0 and 16% of participants (Median 5.4%) were outside the acceptable range (plus/minus 2 dilutions) in the assessments. Laboratories increasingly use lfa for CRAG testing. Semiquantitative results in routine testing may vary considerably, limiting the use as prediction tool for individual patients.

pres30.title: Phospholipid homeostasis in host-Cryptococcus interactions during pulmonary cryptococcosis
pres30.abstract: Cryptococcus neoformans is the leading cause of life-threatening fungal meningitis. Macrophages are the first line of defense against Cryptococcus infection, yet the molecular mechanisms underlining macrophage-Cryptococcus interaction remains incompletely understood. Our previous study demonstrated that loss of lipid flippase function by deleting CDC50 gene in C. neoformans leads to increased accumulation of exocytoplasmic phosphatidylserine (PS) on the plasma membrane, enhanced phagocytosis and macrophage killing, resulting in avirulence in animal infection models. Notably, this PS-driven phagocytosis occurs independently of the host PS receptor MertK. Our recent findings reveal that cdc50àÜ cells produce significantly more extracellular vesicles (EVs) with elevated surface PS levels compared to wild-type H99. Both cdc50àÜ cells and their EVs exhibited increased production of multiple phospholipid species, including PS and PE, as shown by our lipidomic analysis. Furtherm ore, supplementing H99 culture with cdc50àÜ EVs during macrophage-H99 interaction markedly increases in H99 phagocytosis, indicating that cdc50àÜ EVs can enhance the fungicidal activity of macrophages. Collectively, our data demonstrate that fungal lipid homeostasis is crucial for C. neoformans resistance to macrophage recognition and clearance during early host-pathogen interactions in cryptococcosis. Lipid flippase function emerges as a potential drug target, and we have developed a peptide-based inhibitor that shows potent activity against Cryptococci.

pres31.title: Non-HIV Cryptococcal Disease: A Case Series from a Tertiary Hospital in Peru
pres31.abstract: Background and Aims: Cryptococcal disease is a severe fungal infection mainly associated with HIV but also occurs in non-HIV patients. In Peru, HIV remains the main risk factor; however, early recognition in non-HIV patients is essential. We aimed to describe the clinical characteristics, treatment, and outcomes of non-HIV cryptococcal disease. Methods: Retrospective case series of patients with mycologically confirmed cryptococcosis and non-reactive HIV ELISA (2018–2024). Clinical, laboratory, treatment, and outcome data were collected, and CSF parameters were analyzed according to 30-day mortality. Results: Seventeen patients were included; 88% had cryptococcal meningitis. Mean age was 52 ¬± 14 years, and 53% were male. Immunosuppression was present in 64.7%, mainly corticosteroid use (35.3%); 35.3% had no identified risk factors. Common symptoms were headache (76.5%), fever (70.5%), and altered mental status (57.9%). Median CSF values were leukocytes 55 cells/mm°, proteins 131 mg/dL, glucose 36 mg/dL, and mean opening pressure 33 cmHÇO. Most patients (94.4%) received amphotericin B plus fluconazole. Thirty-day mortality was 41.1%. No significant CSF differences were found by mortality, but effect sizes were large for opening pressure (d = 0.84) and moderate for hypoglycorrhachia (d = 0.57). Conclusions: Non-HIV cryptococcal disease was often associated with corticosteroid use, and one-third of patients lacked immunosuppressive risk factors. Elevated opening pressure and low CSF glucose may be associated with increased mortality.

pres32.title: Developing experimental immunotherapies for offsetting inflammatory brain pathologies in Cryptococcus Meningoencephalitis
pres32.abstract: Severe brain damage is the main source of morbidity and mortality in cryptococcal meningoencephalitis (CM). Despite antifungal drug treatment, inflammation continues to contribute to damage, and the CNS-inflammatory component remains largely unaddressed clinically. Here, we devised and preclinically tested urgently needed immunomodulatory therapies aiming to suppress CNS inflammation without substantial effects on fungal clearance. Anti-CXCR3 antibody (CXCR3Ab), low-dose IL-2 immune complexes (LdIL-2cpl), or anti-FAS-L immunotherapies were applied at the onset of symptoms in mouse models of CM. Body weight loss, neurological and body condition score, and brain/other organ CFUs were assessed. Inflammatory parameters were analyzed by flow cytometry and histology. Some of these therapies were studied in the context of antifungal drugs fluconazole (FLC) and AmBisome (AmBi). CXCR3Ab-immunotherapy improved survival and symptoms in the hyperinflammatory CM model (C.n.52D) by reduc ing exuberant inflammation while only slightly slowing fungal clearance. Combination fluconazole (FLC) and CXCR3Ab therapy improved survival above any individual treatment. In the high-virulence CM model (C.n.H99), CXCR3Ab decreased inflammation level without worsening fungal CFU or survival, and in combination with AmBi, CXCR3Ab-therapy improved mouse survival. The LdIL-2cpl therapy boosted CNS Treg response and reduced inflammation, greatly improving survival in C.n.52D-CM. Alone, it significantly suppressed brain fungal clearance, but not when combined with FLC. Promising results were obtained with Anti-FASL, showing a mild immunosuppressive effect, but drastically reducing symptoms and mortality in C.n.52D-CM. In conclusion, targeted immunotherapies show promise as safe and effective means of suppressing inflammatory damage in CM, achieving optimal effects when combined with conventional antifungal drug therapies.

pres33.title: Changing impact of Cryptococcus on HIV-associated meningitis in northern Uganda
pres33.abstract: BACKGROUND & AIMS In past decades, Cryptococcus was the leading cause of death due to meningitis in sub-Saharan Africa among people living with HIV (PLWH). With improvements in HIV and cryptococcal meningitis (CM) care, we sought to evaluate the current causes of death or neurological impairment in PLWH with meningitis in northern Uganda. METHODS We conducted a retrospective cohort study utilizing data from PLWH admitted to Lira Regional Referral Hospital with meningitis from 2021-2025. Collected data consisted of demographic information, meningitis etiologies, in-hospital mortality and neurological deficits at hospital discharge, including neurocognitive sequelae such as confusion (GCS <14), delirium, aphasia, and behavior changes. RESULTS Among 276 PLWH with confirmed meningitis, 32.2% (n=89) were diagnosed with CM, 28.6% had bacterial meningitis (BM), 17% had tuberculosis meningitis and 8% had viral meningitis (VM). Thus, most PLWH with meningitis had etiologies other than Cryptococcus. Mortality due to CM (32.6%) was lower than for TBM (51.1%) and BM (48.1%). Those with TBM had more neurocognitive dysfunction at the time of hospital discharge (27.7%), compared to CM (15.7%) and BM (15.2%). However, among patients who died in the hospital, 71.4% of those with CM had neurocognitive dysfunction, compared to 38.5% of those with TBM and 25.0% of those with BM. CONCLUSION Among PLWH in Lira, northern Uganda, meningitis due to etiologies other than Cryptococcus occurred more commonly, although CM remains a major cause of death and neurological impairment.

pres34.title: Cryptococcal antigenaemia screening and treatment: observations from the field in South Africa (CAST OFF-SA)
pres34.abstract: Background and aims Early detection and treatment of cryptococcal antigenaemia (CA) among patients with advanced HIV (defined as CD4 count<200 cells/¬µl) prevents deaths due to cryptococcal meningitis. Since 2016, a laboratory-based reflex screening programme in South Africa automatically tests all individuals with CD4 plasma/serum samples <100 cells/¬µl for cryptococcal antigen (CrAg). Although 92% of eligible people have been screened, it is unclear whether cryptococcal meningitis incidence and mortality have reduced. We aimed to investigate barriers to screening implementation and propose opportunities for improvement. Methods We conducted 21 focus group discussions and 112 questionnaires with clinic staff in 4 provinces to explore their knowledge of CA and barriers to care for patients following a positive result. We conducted interviews with 14 investigators of a CA treatment trial (EFFECT, ISRCTN 30579828) to explore its impact on patient care. Results Clinic staff described fragmented, poorly defined pathways to care for patients following a positive reflex CrAg test. Key barriers included lack of standardised result notification systems, unclear clinical ownership of results, difficulty tracing and referring patients for lumbar puncture, and limited confidence in CA management. Consequently, positive results were frequently missed or acted upon only weeks to months later. EFFECT investigators described potential solutions including creation of regional roles dedicated to linking patients to care following a positive reflex CrAg test and using text messaging to improve notification systems. Conclusions Insights from clinic staff and EFFECT investigators could be used to inform interventions that improve the impact of CA screening in South Africa.

pres35.title: Whole genome alignment of Cryptococcus and Kwoniella reveals patterns of conservation
pres35.abstract: Fully characterizing the evolution of pathogenicity in Cryptococcus neoformans is crucial for identifying more effective drug targets. Ortholog analysis of Cryptococcus and Kwoniella have pinpointed genes unique to pathogenic Cryptococcus, but evolution of non-coding regions remain less well understood. A recent GWAS analysis identified SNPs in non-coding regions linked to higher fungal burden in patients infected with C. neoformans, demonstrating the potential for non-coding variation to regulate pathogenicity functions. To investigate C. neoformans at nucleotide-level resolution, we aligned the reference genomes of 31 species with Progressive Cactus and used the Phylogenetic Analysis with Space/Time Models toolkit to find patterns among evolutionarily conserved and accelerated regions in Cryptococcus and Kwoniella. Close to 75% of bases in C. neoformans conserved across the whole tree belonged to coding sequence. Within protein-coding genes, exons varied in degree of cons ervation depending on its position relative to UTRs. Some intergenic regions and ncRNAs were also highly conserved, indicating these non-coding regions serve an important function. Examining sequence unique to pathogens, we found over 30% of non-protein coding bases in C. neoformans were exclusively aligned across all pathogenic species. This suggests newly evolved regulation of pathogenesis-conferring processes. The highest rates of evolutionary acceleration were seen in intergenic regions, 5’ UTRs, and 3’ UTRs (in descending order). Future analyses will re-examine transcriptional data to identify potential genes within conserved intergenic regions and improve exon-intron boundaries in pathogenic Cryptococcus.

pres36.title: Saprobic and pathogenic Cryptococcus species: uncovering hidden diversity across the genus.
pres36.abstract: Species within the genus Cryptococcus range from opportunistic human pathogens to environmentally associated saprobes, yet the genomic and ecological processes underlying this diversity remain poorly understood. Comparative analyses across pathogenic and nonpathogenic lineages are essential for identifying evolutionary transitions linked to pathogenicity and for refining species boundaries. Here, we analyzed seventeen Cryptococcus species representing divergent evolutionary lineages, including six newly identified taxa and lineages previously lacking formal species designation. We integrated phylogenomic inference, divergence metrics, chromosome-scale genome analyses, mating assays, and metabolic phenotyping to clarify species boundaries and patterns of genome evolution within the genus. We formally designated Cryptococcus hyracis as the VGV lineage of the C. gattii species complex and described five new nonpathogenic species recovered from environmental substrates such as fruit, soil, and bark beetle galleries, three of which formed a previously unrecognized clade within Cryptococcus. One of these species, Cryptococcus porticicola, showed a strong association with bark beetles across multiple strains sampled from distinct geographic locations. Genomic analyses of this species revealed intraspecific chromosomal polymorphism, signatures of recombination, and evidence of sexual reproduction. Across Cryptococcus, we observed extensive karyotypic diversity, including lineage-specific rearrangements and repeated reductions in chromosome number, potentially linked to centromere loss or inactivation. Phenotypic Biolog profiling revealed clade-specific substrate preferences suggestive of ecological specialization, while growth at 37 °C was restricted to clinically relevant taxa. Together, these findings expanded the recognized diversity of Cryptococcus and provided new insights into the genomic and ecological forces shaping speciation and pathogenic potential.

pres37.title: Analyzing genomic and phenotypic variation within a large clinical Cryptococcus population to identify determinants of virulence and disease outcome
pres37.abstract: Cryptococcus neoformans is the main pathogen responsible for cryptococcosis, a disease which results in an estimated 112,000 deaths per year. Pathogen-specific characteristics, such as an infectious isolate’s genetic background and expressed phenotypic traits, may influence poor patient outcomes. However, the relative contributions of genetic diversity and phenotypic variation to clinical outcomes remain poorly understood. Here, we leverage clinical, genomic, and in vitro phenotypic data from 590 C. neoformans isolates collected through the AMBITION clinical trial, spanning Uganda, Malawi, South Africa, Botswana, and Zimbabwe. As previously observed in other studies, we find that both fungal burden and the rate of fungal clearance correlate highly with mortality. Whole-genome sequencing and phylogenetic analysis reveal that, while we identify all four major lineages (VNI, VNII, VNBI, and VNBII), 87% of our isolates are within the VNI lineage. Genome-wide association study approaches identify variants linked to fungal burden, corroborating results from previous studies linking natural genetic variation to disease outcomes. In vitro phenotypic profiling of clinical isolates reveals heterogeneous growth in response to stressors which target key virulence traits, including cell wall stress, elevated temperature, and hypoxia. This study represents one of the largest genotypic and phenotypic characterizations of clinical C. neoformans isolates, providing a resource to complement previous and future studies of virulence within this pathogen. By analyzing both genomic and phenotypic diversity, we can illuminate biological mechanisms and pathways underlying pathogenicity, host adaptation, and clinical outcomes.

pres38.title: Cryptococcus neoformans Sequence Type 93 is Associated with Reduced Type-1 Immune Responses in Ugandan Patients with HIV-associated Cryptococcal Meningitis
pres38.abstract: Cryptococcus neoformans causes fatal meningitis commonly among patients with advanced HIV disease in Sub-Saharan Africa. The relationship between C. neoformans genotypes and virulence is incompletely understood. We performed whole-genome sequencing (WGS) on 493 C. neoformans isolates from Ugandan patients with HIV-associated Cryptococcal Meningitis (HIV-CM) enrolled in the ASTRO (NCT01802385) and AMBITION (MREC 1297) clinical trials conducted between 2015 and 2020. Multiple Locus Sequence Types (ST) were extracted from the WGS data. Associations between ST and virulence using clinical, microbiological, and immune-phenotype data were assessed using Chi-square tests for categorical phenotypes and the Wilcoxon rank-sum test for continuous phenotypes. Of the analysed isolates, 42.2% (208/493) were from patients who died, whereas 57.8% (285/493) were from patients who survived. Thirty-four (34) unique STs were identified among the HIV-CM clinical isolates. ST93 and non-ST93 acco unted for 65.1% (n=321) and 32.5% (n=160) of the C. neoformans isolates, respectively, while 2.4%(n=12) of the isolates could not be typed. There was no statistically significant difference in mortality, nor in known CM prognostic factors such as Glasgow coma scale, fungal burden, CD4+ T cell counts, cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) white blood cell count, CSF protein, and CSF glucose between patients infected with ST93 isolates and those infected with non-ST93 isolates. However, patients infected with ST93 isolates expressed significantly lower levels of the Type-1 proinflammatory cytokines. Since Th1 immune responses are essential for fungal clearance from the central nervous system, this finding potentially explains the high prevalence of ST93 among immunosuppressed patients with advanced HIV-CM in Uganda.

pres39.title: From Essential Metabolism to Resistance: Transposable Elements and 5-FAA Escape in Cryptococcus
pres39.abstract: Transposable elements (TEs) can rewire genomes and gene expression, helping pathogens adapt under stress. In Cryptococcus, TE activity has been linked to antifungal resistance through disruption of key genes. Our recent research focuses on how TE mobilisation can also drive resistance to 5-fluoroanthranilic acid (5-FAA), a tryptophan (TRP) pathway antimetabolite. This is an especially interesting system in Cryptococcus because, unlike in Saccharomyces cerevisiae, the TRP biosynthetic pathway is essential. Classic loss-of-function resistance is therefore less plausible, pointing instead to regulatory changes, gene-dosage effects, or structural variation—routes in which TEs could play a key role. To test this, we used RNA interference (RNAi) as a lever, since it is the primary TE-silencing pathway. We compared a wild-type background with an RNAi-deficient strain, which is expected to show elevated TE activity. Using mutation-rate experiments, we observed an increased frequency of spontaneous 5-FAA-resistant colonies in the RNAi mutant. To identify causal variants, we conducted whole-genome sequencing analysis on resistant and non-resistant isolates. Initial findings indicate that de novo TE insertions in both UTRs of TRP5, a core TRP-pathway gene, are common in resistant isolates, suggesting resistance may arise through altered expression rather than gene inactivation. We are now extending the analysis to catalogue and validate additional structural variants associated with resistance. Together, this work aims to define how uncontrolled TE mobilisation can generate resistance to a compound targeting an essential pathway—precisely the type of pathway often prioritised in antifungal drug discovery—in a significant human pathogen.

pres40.title: Subtelomeric Genomic Rearrangement underlies Phenotypic Variation and Amphotericin B Resistance in Clinical isolates of Cryptococcus neoformans
pres40.abstract: Emergence of amphotericin B (AmB) resistance remains uncommon in Cryptococcus but is increasingly recognized and poses a serious clinical threat, as AmB-based regimens remain a cornerstone of induction therapy for cryptococcal meningitis. We analyzed serial clinical isolates of C. neoformans obtained from a patient with treatment-failed cryptococcosis before and after AmB-based treatment. The post-treatment isolate exhibited elevated AmB MICs (2–3 ¬µg/ml) compared with the pre-treatment isolate (0.25–0.38 ¬µg/ml). Phenotypic assays showed that AmB-resistant isolates had reduced growth at elevated temperature and impaired fitness under membrane perturbation, redox/oxidative stress, and iron limitation. Consistent with altered redox homeostasis, fluorescence-based assays revealed increased basal reactive oxygen species in cytosolic and mitochondrial compartments in the resistant isolate. To define the underlying mechanism, we performed RNA-seq comparing genome-sequenced w ild-type and post-treatment isolates. Gene Ontology enrichment identified ergosterol biosynthesis as the most significantly altered pathway, with marked downregulation of ERG2 in post-treatment isolates. Long-read nanopore sequencing uncovered an approximately 20-kb terminal deletion on chromosome 1 encompassing ERG2, followed by de novo telomeric repeat addition consistent with chromosome-end healing. Complementation of AmB-resistant isolates with wild-type ERG2 restored phenotypic defects and AmB susceptibility, confirming ERG2 loss as the primary driver of AmB resistance. These data demonstrate a clinically relevant route to AmB resistance emergence in C. neoformans mediated by chromosome-end loss and telomere addition and highlight ERG2-dependent sterol homeostasis as critical for core physiology and polyene susceptibility.

pres41.title: Molecular and Phenotypic Characterisation of Clinical Cryptococcus Isolates
pres41.abstract: The Cryptococcus neoformans/gattii species complexes can be subdivided into serotypes, molecular types and genetic lineages based on structural, molecular or genetic differences, respectively. Hybrid strains have been reported both within (A/D) and between complexes (B/D). Differences in species and genotypes can influence patient type and antifungal susceptibility. Therefore, defining the molecular epidemiology of cryptococcal species is essential. This study aimed to identify and characterise clinical isolates from hospitals across Spain deposited at the Mycology Reference Laboratory of the Institute of Health Carlos III since 2020. Molecular identification was performed by conventional PCR and Sanger sequencing of the intergenic spacer region (IGS); hybrids (A/D or B/D) were detected using real-time PCR with molecular beacon probes. To date, 48 strains have been analysed. 81.8% were identified as C. neoformans, 57% of which belonged to genotype 1a (57%), followed by genotype 1b (14%) and 1c (6%). 11.3% were C. deneoformans, and 6.8% as A/D hybrids. The remaining 5% of isolates were C. deuterogattii (66.7%) and C. gattii (33.3%). Interestingly, all C. deuterogattii isolates were from the same geographical region (southern Spain). Regarding the origin, most of them were isolated from cerebrospinal fluid (49%) followed by blood samples (25%). Twelve isolates showed high fluconazole MICs (32 mg/L). Preliminary phenotypic analyses revealed capsule variability among clinical isolates. Growth assays showed that some strains exhibited markedly slow growth at multiple temperatures, but no clear correlation with genotype thus far. These early findings provide initial insight into the phenotypic diversity of Cryptococcus species associated with virulence.

pres42.title: ERG11 Mutations Reshape Fluconazole Binding in Cryptococcus
pres42.abstract: Antifungal drug resistance (AFR) complicates the treatment of invasive fungal diseases (IFDs) such as cryptococcosis, particularly in immunocompromised individuals and those in resource-limited settings. Drug resistance often arises from single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) in genes encoding antifungal targets such as ERG11. This study assessed trends in cryptococcal AFR, identified resistance-associated SNPs and investigated their impact on protein folding and ultimately ligand interactions.
