Clinical and environmental cryptococcal isolates, originating from South Africa, were tested for antifungal susceptibility to fluconazole (FLC) using the microbroth dilution assay. The ERG11 gene of ten resistant and six susceptible isolates was sequenced and analysed for SNP identification and AFR correlation. Protein modelling, molecular docking and dynamic simulations assessed the impact of the SNPs’ corresponding protein mutations on lanosterol 14α-demethylase structure, FLC and natural substrate interaction. Environmental isolates exhibited significantly higher AFR than clinical isolates (p < 0.001). Additionally, temporal and regional analyses revealed increased resistance in recent (post-2018) compared to older (pre-2010) isolates, with distinct spatial distributions (p < 0.001). Multiple ERG11 SNPs were identified, including South African-specific variants that alter the substrate-interacting residues and the haem motif. Molecular docking and dynamic simulations confirmed reduced FLC affinity and enhanced lanosterol binding in an extremely resistant variant (FLC minimum inhibitory concentration > 64 ¬µg/mL) compared to a susceptible variant. These findings highlight evolving AFR strategies, structural SNP impacts, emphasising the need for integrated surveillance and antifungal stewardship in high-risk settings.

pres43.title: The cell-type specific neuroimmune response to cryptococcal infection of murine hippocampal brain slices
pres43.abstract: Cryptococcal meningitis (CM) is often associated with severe neurological damage but the neuropathophysiology of this fatal disease is incompletely understood. This study aims to characterise the immunomodulatory transcriptional activity of each cell type in the murine brain to C. neoformans using single-nuclei RNA sequencing (snRNAseq). Hippocampal organotypic brain slices were prepared from six-day old mouse pups and stimulated with either 1 x 108 C. neoformans cells, lipopolysaccharide (LPS) or normal growth media for 24 hours. Brain slices were Dounce homogenised and a single nuclei suspension recovered for sequencing. Libraries were generated by 10X Genomics and sequenced via Illumina. Microglia did not significantly upregulate any major pro- or anti-inflammatory genes in cryptococcal infection, in contrast to the pro-inflammatory gene upregulation with LPS treatment. There is no clear phenotype for cryptococcal-treated microglia, but upregulated genes include phagocytic receptors with anti-inflammatory properties including Colec12 and Lrp1. Astrocytes have more significantly upregulated pro-inflammatory genes in response to Cryptococcus including Stat3, Nos2, Cxcl2, Fos and Nfkb1. Astrocytes’ role at the blood-brain barrier is also affected with upregulated Vegfa and Piezo1. Tnfaip2, critical in tunnelling nanotube biogenesis, is upregulated in both astrocytes and microglia. Excitatory neurons have the highest number of differentially expressed genes in response to Cryptococcus with upregulated Stat3, Nfkb1, Fos and Il4. The glutamate cycle is also affected with downregulated Gls and Grik2. This data suggests that microglia are phagocytosing Cryptococcus leading to a minor anti-inflammatory response. Astrocytes are significantly pro-inflammatory and, along with excitatory neurons, have a significant Stat3-response to Cryptococcus.

pres44.title: Genomic signatures of parasex among two genetically distinct hybrid groups of Cryptococcus neoformans.
pres44.abstract: Hybridisation is an important source of genetic and phenotypic diversity in fungi, yet the mechanisms shaping hybrid genomes in human pathogens remain unclear. Cryptococcus neoformans, a major cause of fungal meningitis, comprises two varieties—C. neoformans var. grubii and var. neoformans—that occasionally hybridise. We aimed to resolve hybrid lineage structure, parental origins, and recombination mechanisms driving genome evolution in this pathogen. We analysed 197 C. neoformans genomes, including 13 newly sequenced isolates, using phylogenomic, population genetic, and long-read assembly approaches. Analyses included mitochondrial inheritance, admixture, loss of heterozygosity (LOH), and structural variation to identify hybridisation signatures. We identified 73 hybrid isolates forming two previously unrecognised groups (H1 and H2) with distinct ecological origins and parental ancestry. H1, comprising exclusively clinical isolates, showed limited admixture but high nu cleotide diversity, consistent with ancient hybridisation and backcrossing. H2, primarily environmental or laboratory-derived, exhibited extensive admixture between VNI and VNIV lineages. Both groups displayed pervasive aneuploidy, large-scale LOH, and mosaic haplotype inheritance consistent with parasexual recombination. Phenotypic assays revealed substantial variability in growth and virulence, highlighting functional impacts of hybrid genome plasticity. Our study provides the first evidence of parasexual recombination in C. neoformans, revealing a composite lifecycle that integrates mitotic and meiotic processes. Parasex emerges as a key mechanism shaping genome evolution, population diversity, and pathogenic potential in this globally important human pathogen.

pres45.title: Gene content diversity in Cryptococcus neoformans
pres45.abstract: Cryptococcus neoformans causes an estimated 147,000 deaths per year worldwide, primarily in immunocompromised patients. C. neoformans consists of 4 different molecular types: VNI, VNII, VNBI, and VNBII. While we understand sequence variation separating these groups, we have a limited understanding of the differences in genetic content that differentiate each isolate. We de novo assembled a pangenome using Illumina sequences of 384 diverse C. neoformans isolates. We curated a set of C. neoformans orthologs that are either core (present in >95% of genomes), accessory (present in >=95% and <1 genomes), or singletons (present in 1 genome). Most genetic content variation in C. neoformans is the result of variation in accessory gene content. Our pangenome revealed that a large portion of accessory genes were located near subtelomeres in the H99 reference genome. We also found numerous accessory genes that were limited to specific molecular types and that many singletons shared cl ose sequence similarity to closely related species of Cryptococcus, consistent with introgression or incomplete lineage sorting events. We are currently using transposon mutagenesis to explore the functional consequences of variation in gene content across the species.

pres46.title: Susceptibility and Genomic Testing of Cryptococcus gattii and Cryptococcus neoformans Clinical Isolates from Mozambique
pres46.abstract: Cryptococcus accounted for 38% of laboratory-confirmed meningitis cases in sentinel surveillance of adults with suspected meningitis in Mozambique (2016-2017). In 2019, fifteen isolates, cultured from adults with HIV-associated cryptococcal meningitis who had not responded to treatment, were referred to the National Institute for Communicable Diseases in South Africa. Isolates were first inoculated onto Niger seed agar and L-canavanine glycine bromothymol blue (CGB) agar and then identified using matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionisation time-of-flight mass spectrometry. Antifungal susceptibility testing was performed using commercial broth microdilution (Sensititre) and gradient diffusion (Etest) methods, and minimum inhibitory concentrations were interpreted using Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute epidemiological cut-off values for VNI or VGI. Whole genome sequencing was undertaken. Nine isolates (60%) were phenotypically identified as Cryptococcus neoformans species-complex and six (40%) as Cryptococcus gattii species-complex. All isolates were categorised as wild-type to azoles, flucytosine and amphotericin B. Fluconazole MICs ranged from 4-8 mg/L; 9/15 (60%) had an MIC of 8 mg/L. All C. neoformans isolates were molecular type VNI, while C. gattii isolates included one VGIV and five VGI. No known resistance-conferring mutations were detected. Despite reported treatment non-response in these clinical cases, all referred isolates had wild-type susceptibility phenotypes. However, fluconazole MICs of 8 mg/L in 60% of isolates could have resulted in suboptimal pharmacodynamic target attainment during periods when fluconazole daily dosing was <800 mg. Treatment non-response in cryptococcal meningitis may also be a consequence of other factors such as suboptimal induction therapy omitting flucytosine or inadequate pressure management.

pres47.title: Mitotic and cytokinetic defects under stress can drive polyploidy in Cryptococcus neoformans
pres47.abstract: Polyploidy is observed in multiple human fungal pathogens and provides a strategy for rapid adaptation to stressful conditions encountered during infection. In Cryptococcus neoformans, polyploidy is a defining feature of Titan cells, which can reach ploidy levels from 2C to 312C. These cells exhibit enhanced survival compared to typical haploid yeast cells in the host. Polyploidy could arise through several mechanisms, but the precise cell-cycle alterations that underlie this process in C. neoformans remain poorly understood. Recently, work in the lab, demonstrated that cells exposed to hypoxia arrest before mitosis as large unbudded cells in G2. C. neoformans cells exposed to prolonged hypoxia either alone or combined with heat stress or nutrient limitation, exhibited mitotic and cytokinetic defects that drove polyploid cell formation. Live-cell imaging revealed several distinct mitotic and cytokinetic failures that subsequently produced polyploid cells with one or more nu clei. These observations reveal that host-relevant stresses can disrupt normal mitotic progression and promote polyploidization through multiple mechanisms. Using molecular approaches coupled with live-cell imaging, we aim to identify how core cell-cycle machinery is modified during polyploidization and how hypoxia contributes to these changes. Because the ability to grow and divide in host-imposed stress is a major virulence factor, understanding how polyploid cells form and proliferate will provide insight into how polyploid Titan cells contribute to disease progression and fungal pathogenicity.

pres48.title: The role of Eisosomal proteins in pathogenesis of Cryptococcus neoformans
pres48.abstract: Cell wall and plasma membrane (PM) contribute to fungal pathogenicity. PM-associated eisosome complex plays key roles in PM organization, stress response, and cell wall synthesis. Eisosomes have been described in the ascomycetes phylum; however, no study has been conducted yet to understand eisosome’s role in Cryptococcus neoformans. In Saccharomyces cerevisiae, Pil1 and Lsp1 proteins are the core components of eisosomes that shape the curvature of the PM based on their conserved AH-BAR domains. Our results revealed two localization patterns for fluorescently tagged Pil1 and Lsp1 homologues in C. neoformans: the canonical punctate localization to the PM and a novel localization to the mother-bud neck. The pil1Δ and lsp1Δ mutants exhibited inverse sensitivity to various stress conditions suggesting an unexpected mechanism of how Pil1 and Lsp1 cooperate in stress response. Cells lacking Lsp1 exhibited fewer and more intense punctate signals of Pil1-GFP as compared to tho se found in the wild-type, suggesting a role of Lsp1 in eisosome formation and/or dynamics. Interestingly, elimination of Lsp1 had similar consequences on the localization of the septin Cdc12-GFP, which forms filamentous punctate assemblies at the PM specifically when cells are exposed to 37°C. As the well-described function of septins is at the mother-bud neck during cytokinesis, our results point to a novel connection between septins and eisosome components that involves PM homeostasis and cytokinesis. Strikingly, the double mutant (pil1Δlsp1Δ) revealed defects in two major virulence factors, polysaccharide capsule and secretion of urease, pointing to a critical contribution of eisosomes to pathogenicity of C. neoformans.

pres49.title: A Conserved Metabolic Network Regulates Titan Cell Formation in Cryptococcus neoformans
pres49.abstract: Cryptococcus neoformans is an opportunistic fungal pathogen that primarily causes pulmonary infections, with the potential to cause life-threatening infections including meningoencephalitis in immunocompromised individuals. Key virulence factors including polysaccharide capsule and melanin, facilitate immune evasion and tissue invasion in the host. Recently, Titan cell formation, has been defined as another important virulence factor of C. neoformans and plays a pivotal role in disease progression. Titan cells are defined as enlarged cells with a diameter of 10 ¬µm and are highly polyploid with a significantly thicker capsule compared to the yeast morphotype. Despite the established role of Titan cells in C. neoformans pathogenesis, the mechanistic details governing their formation remain largely unresolved. The cyclic AMP–protein kinase A (cAMP-PKA) pathway has been shown to be an important regulator of virulence-associated processes, including capsule formation, mela nin biosynthesis and Titan cell formation. However, the upstream signals, critical for the activation of cAMP-PKA pathway in the context of Titan cell formation remain poorly understood. In this study, for the first time, we demonstrate that the central carbon metabolic pathway, glycolysis, is critical for cAMP-dependent Titan cell formation in C. neoformans. Pharmacological and genetic perturbation of glycolysis significantly attenuated Titan cell formation. Remarkably, exogenous addition of cAMP completely reversed the Titan cell defects observed during glycolysis perturbation. These findings establish a novel regulatory axis wherein central carbon metabolism orchestrates morphogenetic switching in C. neoformans by regulating the activity of the well conserved cAMP-PKA pathway, providing new insights into the metabolic control of C. neoformans dimorphism.

pres50.title: Evolution of thermotolerance in a human fungal pathogen through recurrent mutation of a sugar transporter
pres50.abstract: The ability to grow at high temperature, or thermotolerance, is essential for pathogenicity in many microbes, including fungi. Despite the importance of this trait for pathogen success, our understanding of the genetic basis of high temperature growth in pathogenic fungi remains limited. Here, we used experimental evolution to examine thermal adaptation in a temperature-sensitive environmental strain of Cryptococcus neoformans. Through this approach, we identified a number of thermally-adapted isolates which exhibit a striking improvement in growth at higher temperatures. Whole genome sequencing of several isolates revealed recurrent mutation of a hexose transporter, HXT1, across independent replicate populations. Each of the three unique HXT1 mutations identified in these adapted strains are both necessary and sufficient for thermotolerance and closely phenocopy deletion alleles of HXT1. Our preliminary data indicates these mutations may also lead to improved growth of the fungi in macrophages, suggesting a strong link between growth at high temperatures and pathogenesis. Our ongoing work seeks to provide a mechanistic understanding of the role of Hxt1 and nutrient availability, more broadly, in regulating thermotolerance. This includes performing biochemical characterization of glucose uptake functions and metabolite concentrations in mutated Hxt1 variants and strains grown at different temperatures. Collectively, this work provides key insight into the fundamental biology of an important fungal pathogen and the processes by which fungi can and will adapt to high temperature, leading to the emergence of novel pathogenic species. From: Marina Ruzic <ruzic@bc.edu<mailto:ruzic@bc.edu>> Date: Thursday, 18 December 2025 at 20:37 To: 12th International Conference on Cryptococcus & Cryptococcosis <ICCC2026@exeter.ac.uk<mailto:ICCC2026@exeter.ac.uk>> Subject: Abstract submission Hello, I had just submitted my abstract today, but had realized right after I sent it that I made a mistake when listing the affiliation. Would it be best to resubmit the abstract online? I am extremely sorry for the mistake and confusion. Thank you for your help. Best, Marina -- Marina Ruzic (she/her) Graduate Student Hilbert Lab Biology Department Boston College

pres51.title: Identifying Cryptococcus spore-enriched proteins and their roles in spore coat restructuring during germination
pres51.abstract: To colonize new environments, including a mammalian lung, fungal spores must undergo germination into a vegetatively growing cell type. For Cryptococcus, this necessitates significant morphological changes in which a small, ovoid spore becomes larger and rounder as a yeast emerges laterally from the original spore structure. Functions such as spore coat degradation and new cell wall synthesis are likely crucial for spores to undergo the changes required for germination into yeast, yet little is known about the molecular pathways involved in these processes. To determine how protein identities and levels change during germination, we carried out a comprehensive analysis of the spore proteome during germination (at 6 time points over 10 hours). From this dataset, we identified 19 highly spore-enriched carbohydrate active enzymes (CAZymes) that we hypothesize are key players in spore coat restructuring during germination. Among these proteins are a chitin synthase, an endochit inase, an endo-1,3-beta glucanase, and a polysaccharide deacetylase, suggesting that cell wall modification plays a crucial role in the germination process. We are creating strains with targeted knockouts of the genes encoding these proteins to determine how their deletion impacts spore germination. We will evaluate the rates and efficiency of spore germination at both single-cell and population levels using quantitative germination assays (QGAs), and we will identify differences in spore carbohydrate levels and localization using fluorescence microscopy. These studies promise to reveal fundamental aspects of spore biology and identify mechanisms by which germination occurs, opening the door to more targets for prophylactic antigerminant therapies.

pres52.title: Methodological Considerations for Flow Cytometric Analysis of Polyploidy in Cryptococcus neoformans
pres52.abstract: During infection, Cryptococcus neoformans can form large polyploid cells called Titan cells. To better understand how Titan cells form, we have been examining in vitro conditions that can drive polyploidy. A classical method for quantitating cell ploidy is to stain DNA followed by flow cytometric analysis; however, we have discovered that culturing conditions and staining procedures can influence fluorescence measurements in a way that does not appear to accurately reflect DNA content. In some cases, these conditions can produce data that falsely indicate the presence of polyploid cells. Using our substantial experience in analyzing polyploidy in S. cerevisiae, we have designed new flow cytometry protocols to reliably identify true polyploid cells in C. neoformans. We compared the accuracy of our new protocols with published PI/DAPI protocols to assess the benefits and limitations of different DNA dyes. Our results highlight the high sensitivity of DNA fluorescence to norma l and stress culture conditions, as well as to fixing and staining parameters for C. neoformans. Here, we present the comparative analysis and our most optimized DNA staining protocol. We define conditions that contribute to an increase in fluorescence in normal haploid cells and make recommendations for validating polyploidy in the cell population.

pres53.title: Ubiquitin mediated stress adaptation in Cryptococcus neoformans
pres53.abstract: As an opportunistic fungal pathogen, Cryptococcus neoformans survives in widely varied physical environments. This ability to adapt, particularly to high temperature and stressors in human hosts, allows the yeast to survive, proliferate, and cause severe invasive infections. Underlying this stress adaptation, C. neoformans precisely regulates cell structure and metabolism in part by the post-translation modification of ubiquitination. A ubiquitin ligase enzyme, Rsp5, is essential for C. neoformans to grow under mammalian stressors ranging from temperature to pH. This broad range of functions is mediated by pleiotropic interactions between Rsp5 and diverse substrates. However, the mechanisms of Rsp5 regulation and specificity in response to unique stressors is incompletely understood. Here we use a co-immunoprecipitation based proteomics screen to identify novel C. neoformans E3-ligase regulatory proteins under host-stress conditions. The two most highly enriched Rsp5 inter acting proteins are previously uncharacterized in C. neoformans, but structural homologs in other model organisms regulate ubiquitin ligase activity and function. We demonstrated that C. neoformans mutant strains lacking these likely novel Rsp5 adaptor proteins have altered growth at 39°C. Finally, because the homologues of these novel Rsp5 adaptors are involved in free radical damage response, we tested and found that C. neoformans Rsp5-dependent thermotolerance is also mediated partly by free radical stress. These studies provide novel insight into how C. neoformans uses ubiquitination to survive in the hostile host environment. Defining the molecular basis for invasive fungal pathogens to survive in the infection environment will help understand how they cause disease and discover potential therapeutic targets.

pres54.title: From Cryptococcus neoformans in vivo gene expression to disease
pres54.abstract: Hypothesis: Highly expressed Cryptococcus neoformans genes in human cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) contribute to cryptococcal meningoencephalitis pathogenesis. Methods: We examined 324 C. neoformans genes selected from the top 500 genes with the highest median transcript abundance in human CSF. Using site-directed deletion strains from a genome-wide deletion library, we assessed their roles in virulence-associated traits and stress responses. Results: In vitro phenotypic analysis identified 94 deletion strains with increased sensitivity to cellular stressors and/or inhibitors of key cellular processes. Eighteen strains exhibited heightened sensitivity to at least five conditions. These genes included candidates involved in putative arginine biosynthesis, mannoproteins, and proteins of unknown function. Uncharacterized genes were further analyzed through construction of deletion and wild-type–restored strains; paralogous genes were evaluated using double-deletion mutants to ass ess functional redundancy. Protein localization was also determined. Several single and double-deletion mutants displayed altered disease progression in a mouse model. Conclusion: These findings demonstrate that genes highly expressed in human CSF contribute to C. neoformans pathogenesis. Our results support the use of in vivo gene expression profiles as a powerful strategy for functional inference, particularly for genes of previously unknown function.

pres55.title: Dissecting the role of Mad1 in Cryptococcus neoformans
pres55.abstract: The spindle assembly checkpoint (SAC) protects cells against chromosome segregation defects by delaying anaphase until all chromosomes are correctly bioriented. Mad1 is a key scaffolding protein involved in this checkpoint. Though found on kinetochores during mitosis, Mad1 is localised at the nuclear periphery during interphase and pull down experiments have shown that this may be due to binding with TPR, a component of the nuclear pore complex (NPC). It is possible that this increases the robustness of the SAC by enabling Mad1 to produce important SAC effectors early at the nuclear periphery during, so they are ready for the onset of mitosis. Mad1 deletion mutants (mad1Δ) therefore show a strong checkpoint defective phenotype, being unable to delay mitotic progression when treated with anti-microtubule drugs. When induced to form titans, these mutants also show a significant reduction in titan cell viability, suggesting that Mad1 is required for their survival and/or di vision. During this project, Cryptococcus neoformans mutants expressing truncated Mad1 were used to show that the first 100 residues of the N-terminal tail are necessary for nuclear periphery localisation during interphase, and therefore may contain a TPR-interacting motif. Interestingly, these mutants were checkpoint defective but had no titan cell viability defects, suggesting that Mad1’s titan cell role is independent of both its checkpoint function and its TPR interaction. I hope that this project will be able to shed some light on this additional, as yet uncharacterised, function of Mad1 and increase our understanding of the mysterious titan cell mechanisms.

pres56.title: Mechanism of antifungal persistence of Cryptococcus neoformans against Amphotericin B
pres56.abstract: Cryptococcus neoformans can survive in the host during antifungal treatment, leading to treatment failure and increased clinical burden. Unlike resistance, which involves stable genetic changes, antifungal persistence does not include any genetic changes. Yet antifungal persisters survive during antifungal therapy. Our project aims to understand the antifungal persistence phenotype of C. neoformans against Amphotericin B (AMB). We found that at a 16-fold minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of AMB, most of the stationary-phase cells die (60-90%), but a small subpopulation of persister cells survives. Surprisingly, we saw cell division even at such a high concentration of the drug. For the first time, we successfully isolated persister cells and revealed many interesting and distinct phenotypes. Persisters are smaller in size, with a larger central vacuole, fewer mitochondria and many super-sized lipid droplets. Cell wall’s chitin and beta-glucan, cell membrane’s ergos terol and polyunsaturated fatty acids are also modified in persisters to adapt to the stressful environment. To decrease the oxidative stress generated by AMB, persisters upregulate the expression of genes involved in antioxidation and downregulate the expression of genes involved in mitochondrial respiration. Persisters also have upregulated polyamines and trehalose pathways as a protective mechanism to fight antifungal stress. Persister cells have lower expression of virulence markers, which could help them stay unnoticed inside the host for a longer time, which will be explored further with host interaction studies. Overall, our study has highlighted previously unknown and uncommon mechanisms that cryptococcal persister cells use to protect themselves during antifungal treatment.

pres57.title: A modified chemical-induced protein dimerisation assay identifies key phosphatase interactions for spindle checkpoint silencing in Cryptococcus neoformans
pres57.abstract: During metaphase, incorrect or misoriented attachment of spindle microtubules (MT) to the kinetochore (KT) activates the widely conserved spindle checkpoint, whose signalling prevents cell cycle progression into anaphase. The assembly of the signalling complex, the mitotic checkpoint complex (MCC) and the interactions between the subunits have been widely studied in humans and model ascomycetes. Little is known of the checkpoint dynamics in basidiomycetes, including in human fungal pathogen Cryptococcus neoformans. In this study we have adapted the SynCheck assay previously developed in Schizosaccharomyces pombe, which utilises abscisic acid (ABA) to chemically dimerise two proteins tagged with either a PYL or ABI signalling domain, bringing key checkpoint components together allowing for analysis of checkpoint activation and silencing. To adapt the SynCheck assay for C. neoformans, we tagged the core checkpoint kinase Mps1 with the ABI signalling domain, and either Spc105 or Bub1 with the PYL domain. In the presence of ABA, Mps1 phosphorylation of either Spc105 or Bub1 was sufficient to activate checkpoint arrest, whilst mutating the newly identified key phosphorylation residues in Spc105 impaired checkpoint arrest. Mutation of the phosphatase binding sites on Spc105 and Bub1 impaired checkpoint silencing: cells remained arrested longer than wildtype cells, demonstrating the need for both PP1 and PP2A phosphatase activity in C. neoformans checkpoint silencing. We have adapted the SynCheck assay for use in C. neoformans and through a proof of principle experiment, elucidated some of the key components of phosphorylation regulation, MCC signalling, and checkpoint silencing.

pres58.title: Laccase Drives the Warburg Effect in Melanized Cryptococcus neoformans upon Glucose Depletion
pres58.abstract: Cryptococcus neoformans is a WHO critical fungal priority pathogen as a result of antifungal resistance and high mortality rates of approximately 41-61% in people living with HIV. Melanin biosynthesis from exogenous phenolic substrates is an established stress response in Cryptococcus neoformans to confer resistance to oxidative damage, antifungal drugs, and nutrient starvation. Strikingly, we have identified that melanized cells exhibit a metabolic reprogramming characterized by lactate production and accelerated budding rate with lipid anabolism upon glucose deprivation. As the rate limiting step of melanization via its diphenol oxidase function, the laccase enzyme coordinates this metabolic switch by integrating signals from oxygen and glucose. In fact, laccase1 deletion exhibits drastic oxygen consumption rates compared to WT at low glucose conditions. RNAseq analysis reveals upregulation of dioxygenases in the laccase mutant along with severe defects in lipid and heme synthesis, which together contribute to compromised oxygen sensing. In this study, we characterize moonlighting functions of the laccase, including processing of the sterol regulatory element binding protein, to expand its critical role in establishing cryptococcal infection beyond melanin synthesis. Collectively, we have determined that the laccase drives glucose consumption rate through a Warburg-like shift to outcompete the host defenses of glucose during nutrient limitation. In macrophage coinfection, this alternate metabolism results in greater macrophage killing by melanin-coated yeast than no-melanin control, an evolutionary advantage offset with increasing glucose levels. These novel findings suggest that melanin is a metabolic adaptation to survive nutrient starvation and induce alternate biosynthetic pathways to overcome host immunity.

pres59.title: A role for aquaporin (Aqp1) in the control of Cryptococcus neoformans cell morphology
pres59.abstract: Aquaporins are small, integral membrane channels that facilitate the transport of water across cellular membranes and, in the case of aquaglyceroporins, can also conduct specific neutral solutes, such as glycerol. These proteins are conserved across biological kingdoms, yet their roles in fungal virulence remain relatively understudied. In Cryptococcus neoformans, an opportunistic fungal pathogen, we examined the organism’s single aquaporin, Aqp1, and uncovered unanticipated influences on cellular morphology. Loss of Aqp1 resulted in smaller cells, whereas its presence promoted the formation of enlarged titan-like cells. This shift in size was closely linked to intracellular redox physiology. Consequently, the overexpression of the cryptococcal aquaporin increased sensitivity to oxidative stress and led to the largest titan-like cells; antioxidant supplementation suppressed this enlargement, consistent with a ROS-dependent regulatory mechanism. Additionally, Aqp1 overexpr ession produced vacuolar abnormalities in titan-like cells, suggesting that excessive water influx strained intracellular organization during rapid cell expansion. These findings position Aqp1 at a functional crossroads connecting membrane transport, oxidative balance, and size control, and they support a model in which an aquaporin contributes to the morphological plasticity that allows C. neoformans to adapt to environmental pressures.

pres60.title: Co-culture with Mycobacterium tuberculosis causes remodelling of Cryptococcus gattii morphology and cell wall architecture
pres60.abstract: Cryptococcus species complex, consisting of Cryptococcus neoformans and Cryptococcus gattii, and Mycobacterium tuberculosis (MTB), are opportunistic pathogens associated with high mortality in individuals with weakened immune systems. Evidence indicates that in areas where both pathogens are endemic, the prevalence of co-infections has increased significantly. Moreover, co-infections with Mycobacterium tuberculosis significantly increase mortality rates to 22%, compared with 18% for Cryptococcosis alone. Therefore, this study aimed to investigate how exposure to Mycobacterium tuberculosis influences the morphogenesis and cell-surface architecture of Cryptococcus gattii. We analysed morphological attributes of Cryptococcus gattii (cell body diameter, capsule thickness, and cell wall composition) during co-culture with Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Then, the results were compared with those obtained for Cryptococcus gattii in monoculture. Our results reveal that when Cryptococcus gattii is grown in a physiologically relevant human plasma-like medium, the cell body diameter and capsule thickness increase with rising MTB concentration. Notably, the proportion of titan cells also increased significantly from 6.5% (Cryptococcus gattii monoculture) to 16.6% of the total cell population in co-culture. Capsule thickness was also significantly elevated in the presence of MTB. At the highest MTB dosage, MTB induced cell wall remodelling, with increased mannan and decreased chitin levels when Cryptococcus gattii was co-cultured with MTB. In conclusion, Mycobacterium tuberculosis transforms Cryptococcus gattii into a titan-dominant, thicker-capsulated phenotype, accompanied by cell wall modifications. These changes could enhance fungal persistence in co-infected patients and underscore the urgent need for targeted diagnostic methods and therapies for co-infections.

pres61.title: Antifungal drugs induce cell wall remodelling in Cryptococcus neoformans
pres61.abstract: Background and aim The fungal cell wall is a dynamic structure essential for cellular integrity and adaptation to stress including antifungals. It is not known whether Cryptococcus modifies it cell wall in response to antifungal drugs. This study aimed to test if C. neoformans changes its cell wall composition when exposed to antifungal drugs. Methods C. neoformans H99 was exposed to fluconazole, amphotericin B, 5-fluorocytosine (5FC), or different drug combinations for 24hours. Then, its cell wall structure was examined using electron microscopy. In addition, fluorescence microscopy was used to measure cell wall components including chitin, exposed chitooligomers, chitosan, and mannan. Results Electron microscopy showed an overall thickening of the C. neoformans cell wall following exposure to antifungal drugs. Quantitative fluorescence microscopy revealed that in the presence of fluconazole, exposed chitooligomers and mannan levels increased, while chitin and chitosan levels remained unchanged. Amphotericin B exposure resulted in reduced chitin levels, with minor changes in exposed chitooligomers, chitosan, and mannan. In contrast, 5FC caused a pronounced reduction in chitosan levels, while chitin, chitooligomer exposure, and mannan content showed only modest changes. Drug combinations produced additive effects: fluconazole combined with amphotericin B partially restored chitin levels while maintaining increased chitooligomer exposure and mannan abundance; fluconazole combined with 5FC increased mannan levels alongside sustained chitosan reduction. Conclusions C. neoformans adapts to antifungal presence by remodelling its cell wall structure and composition. This cell wall remodelling can contribute to the development of antifungal resistance and immune evasion of C. neoformans.

pres62.title: Defining the Cryptococcus neoformans titanide, a pathogenic morphotype that arises from typical yeast cells in response to host-relevant conditions
pres62.abstract: Fungal morphogenesis is a major driver of disease outcome. For the opportunistic fungal pathogen Cryptococcus neoformans, morphological heterogeneity within the lung drives dissemination, immune evasion, and drug resistance. To better understand how this heterogeneity emerges, we used microfluidics to study C. neoformans under host-mimicking conditions. We observed that yeast cells are highly plastic and respond by propagating as typical Äòyeast’ (5-7 ¬µm), transitioning into titan cells (>10 ¬µm), or by generating an alternative daughter, the oval titanide (2-3 ¬µm). Titanides appear 24 hours post-induction and by 72 hours are the dominant morphotype. Despite their prevalence and the significance of morphogenesis for virulence, until now, their biology remained unstudied. Using TEM and fluorescence microscopy we demonstrate that titanides are morphologically distinct; they possess thin cell walls and capsules with reduced Pathogen Associated Molecular Pattern exposure and altered distribution. These features distinguish titanides from previously described C. neoformans Äòsmall cells’ such as in vivo seed cells (4-6 ¬µm, high cell-wall mannan content) and micro cells (round, <1 µm, thick cell walled). Despite their thin cell walls, we show that titanides display increased resistance to cell wall/membrane stressors. Additionally, titanides have unique proliferation properties and a morphological plasticity that allows them to form yeast and titan cells in response to relevant culture conditions. Importantly, we demonstrate that titanides are an efficient infectious propagule in a murine model with equivalent pathogenicity to infection with yeast. Together, these findings establish an essential characterisation and insight into a key player in C. neo formans pathogenesis.

pres63.title: Fluconazole stress and MSH1 mutation promote resistance and phenotypic shifts in Cryptococcus neoformans
pres63.abstract: Despite global treatment guidelines recommending combination therapy for cryptococcosis, fluconazole monotherapy remains common in resource-limited settings and is associated with increased risk of resistance. One mechanism for resistance observed in clinical isolates is the occurrence of hypermutator strains, exemplified by mutations in nuclear DNA Mismatch Repair genes MSH2 and MSH6. Recently, we identified a multi-drug resistant clinical isolate carrying mutations in the mitochondrial mismatch repair gene MSH1.Here, we investigated how stress adaptation and antifungal resistance are impacted by disruption of MSH1. Using CRISPR-Cas9 mediated genome editing, we generated a scarless msh1Δ and reconstituted either the wild-type and the clinical point-mutated MSH1 in a H99O background. Strains were subjected to experimental evolution in the presence of fluconazole (32ug/mL) for 1 month. We assessed phenotypic impacts before and after the experimental exposure including via g rowth assays, minimum inhibitory concentration testing, XTT metabolic activity assays, melanin production assays, titan cell induction, and cell wall staining. Whole-genome sequencing was performed to confirm the strains’ identity and genetic alterations. Notably, msh1Δ strains and clinical reconstituted mutants were initially sensitive to fluconazole, but displayed spontaneous tolerance to high doses of flucytosine and increased resistance to Amphotericin B within 1 month. This suggests a Äútwo-hitÄù model of cross-resistance, driven by genomic instability, despite intact nuclear DNA Mismatch Repair. These findings highlight the interplay between mitochondrial repair pathways, stress adaptation, and the emergence of antifungal resistance induced by fluconazole monotherapy in C. neoformans.

pres64.title: Chitin as a Hidden Driver of Cryptococcus neoformans Virulence
pres64.abstract: Meningitis caused by the fungal pathogen Cryptococcus neoformans remains a major global health problem, causing an estimated 152,000 cases and 112,000 deaths annually among people living with HIV/AIDS. The high mortality highlights limitations of current antifungal therapies and underscores the need to better understand fungal-traits that drive disease severity during infection. Cryptococcus is surrounded by a two-layered, sugar-rich structure: an outer polysaccharide capsule and an inner-cell wall. While the capsule is a well-established virulence factor, the contribution of cell wall components remains poorly defined. In this study, we screened 200 clinical C. neoformans isolates grown in physiologically relevant Human Plasma-Like Medium (HPLM) and selected 10 stable low-chitin and 10 stable high-chitin isolates representing the extremes of the chitin spectrum for in-depth analysis. Using these extreme phenotypes, we observed marked differences in fungal population struct ure and function. Low-chitin isolates were dominated by small-cell populations, exhibited higher metabolic activity, and were taken up more efficiently by macrophages. In contrast, high-chitin isolates were enriched for larger-yeast cells. Despite their smaller-cell bodies, low-chitin isolates produced proportionally larger capsules and achieved higher fungal burdens in murine lung infection models. Together, these findings indicate that cell wall chitin content is not only involved in mediating cell shape and environmental stress protection but also strongly influences Cryptococcus population structure and metabolic activity. The pronounced differences between high- and low-chitin isolates suggest that chitin acts as a dynamic regulator of key aspects of fungal biology. Further studies are underway to define the mechanism these phenotypes contribute to pathogenesis.

pres65.title: Regulation of extracellular vesicle biosynthesis by the TOR pathway in the fungal pathogen Cryptococcus neoformans
pres65.abstract: Extracellular vesicles (EVs) are small structures surrounded by a lipid bilayer that are released by cells into the extracellular environment. They are involved in many biological processes including intercellular communication, antifungal resistance and virulence. In fungi, EVs were first isolated in Cryptococcus and have since been described in other human pathogenic fungal species. They contain lipids, polysaccharides, pigments, proteins and RNAs, but the exact composition and function of these cargo molecules remains largely unknown. Unlike in mammals, the mechanism by which Cryptococcus neoformans EVs are secreted out from the cells is unknown. Recently, the structural and compositional aspects of Cryptococcus EVs have been described and their potential role as a vaccine has been suggested. However, the biosynthesis pathways that regulate EV production in fungi have yet to be described. We investigated the dynamics and genetics of Cryptococcus EV biogenesis by screening a library of kinase mutant strains for EV production. We demonstrated that EV release is regulated differently during cellular growth phases and identified mutants associated with different cellular pathways that affect EV production. Three of these kinases, PKH1, YPK1 and SCH9, which are related to sphingolipid biosynthesis showed a drastic reduction in EV production as well as in the estimated size distribution of the vesicles and their protein profiles. Their exact role in EV production is under investigation. Key words: extracellular vesicles, kinases, TOR pathway

pres66.title: Characterizing extracellular vesicle profiles in capsule mutants of the fungal pathogen Cryptococcus neoformans
pres66.abstract: Extracellular vesicles (EVs) are lipid bilayer-enclosed structures produced by cells that participate in the exchange of molecular information between cells. EVs act as carriers of proteins, nucleic acids, and lipids and are therefore involved in numerous physiological and pathological pathways. Although EVs have been widely studied in animals, plants, and even microorganisms, their characterization in fungi is limited. Most insights into fungal EVs originate from studies on Cryptococcus neoformans, an opportunistic pathogen that produces a polysaccharide capsule serving as its major virulence factor. In this study, we investigated mutants of genes involved in capsule biosynthesis for EV production. We identified both high and low EV producers, some also exhibiting differences in vesicle size and protein composition. Furthermore, we characterized their phenotypes under various drug treatments to explore potential associations with the observed differences in the EV producti on. Understanding the connection between EV biogenesis and the capsule formation may provide new insights into vesicle function and the pathways through which EVs are released into the environment. Key words: extracellular vesicles, capsule, Cryptococcus neoformans

pres67.title: Disruption of the cell wall integrity pathway drives the production of apoptotic-like vesicles in Cryptococcus neoformans
pres67.abstract: Extracellular vesicles (EVs) are lipid bilayer-bound particles released by cells into the extracellular environment and are involved in many biological processes, including intercellular communication, antifungal resistance and virulence. Unlike in mammals, the mechanism by which Cryptococcus neoformans EVs are secreted out from the cells remains unknown and our knowledge on EV production regulators is still limited. To identify regulators of EV biosynthesis, we performed a screening on a kinase mutant library for EV production in C. neoformans. We identified the Cell Wall Integrity (CWI) pathway kinases Bck1, Mkk2 and Mpk1 as negative regulators of EV production. Accordingly, we demonstrated that the three mutants, bck1Δ, mkk2Δ and mpk1Δ, produce a higher amount of EVs than the wild-type strain. Moreover, cryo-electron microscopy and nanoflow cytometry revealed that EVs produced by the CWI mutant strains are larger, and less decorated with mannoproteins than those produced by the wild-type strain. In agreement with these observations, proteomic analysis showed that the EV proteome of the mpk1Δ mutant is extensively remodeled, containing fewer GPI-anchored proteins and higher levels of ribosomal and histone proteins compared to wild-type EVs. Finally, annexin V labeling revealed increased phosphatidylserine (PS) exposure on EVs associated with CWI gene mutations. Overall, these results suggest that CWI mutant produce a subpopulation of EVs sharing characteristics with apoptotic vesicles previously described in mammals. They also indicate that C. neoformans, and fungi more generally, can produce distinct EV subtypes that may play different roles during the infection.

pres68.title: Mechanisms of RNA interference in Cryptococcus
pres68.abstract: RNA interference (RNAi) is a mechanism of genome regulation broadly conserved across eukaryotes. It is mediated by small RNAs, which are typically generated from long double-stranded RNA by Dicer enzymes, and associate with Argonaute effector proteins to direct down-regulation of target RNAs based on sequence complementarity. In Cryptococcus, RNAi plays an important role in silencing transposable elements, with loss of RNAi resulting in elevated rates of transposon-induced mutation that can contribute to adaptation and drug resistance. However, beyond the involvement of core pathway components (Dicer, Argonaute and RNA-dependent RNA polymerase), how RNAi operates in Cryptococcus remains poorly understood. We have set out to dissect the molecular mechanisms of RNAi in Cryptococcus, with a focus on C. deneoformans, which retains the full ancestral complement of core RNAi machinery including two Dicer and two Argonaute proteins. Our initial assessments of the relative contributions of these core factors revealed that while Dcr1 and Dcr2 function largely redundantly to one another in endogenous sRNA production, Ago1 is the principle Argonaute protein required to support sRNA accumulation and transposon silencing. To further understand the mechanisms of silencing, we analysed Ago1-associated proteins by IP-MS. From this we identified several novel interactors with potential roles in facilitating/modulating RNAi activity, including putative RNA helicase and methyltransferase enzymes. We are currently further characterising the functions of these factors to understand their potential roles in RNAi function and/or regulation.

pres69.title: Using MLST to explore relationships between environmental and animal associated isolates of Cryptococcus in a koala population.
pres69.abstract: Understanding of Host-Pathogen-Environment interactions (HPEIs) during early cryptococcosis pathogenesis is lacking compared to late pathogenesis. Animals like the koala are excellent sentinels to enhance these knowledge gaps. Their restricted spatial movements, intimate relationships with their environment and high cryptococcal prevalence, help overcome key barriers to studying early pathogenesis in humans. This study aims to investigate the relationship between environmental and animal isolates collected from different origins in the HPEI framework in a captive koala population with a recent cryptococcosis outbreak. Ten individually housed koalas were screened. Six C. neoformans and 101 C. gattii spp. complex isolates (n=107) were collected from positive specimens for MLST. These consisted of 52 nasal isolates from eight koalas, 12 lesion isolates from two clinical cases and 43 environmental isolates from nine enclosures. Limited diversity was observed, with only six sequence types (ST) identified. Five STs were identified from nasal cavity specimens, with over half of the koalas (6/8) having two STs in their nasal cavities. Interestingly, one disease STs and three nasal STs were not detected in the environment. Only one ST was identified across all origin types. These results suggest acquisition of Cryptococcus from the environment and early pathogenesis is multifactorial and not simply a reflection of highly abundant STs. Further investigations using WGS and/or virulence assays are needed to provide a more wholistic picture of isolate diversity during different HPEIs. This study also supports use of sentinels like the koala as natural infection models to enhance understanding of cryptococcosis pathogenesis more broadly.

pres70.title: Targeting membrane asymmetry reveals non-canonical vulnerabilities in Cryptococcus neoformans
pres70.abstract: Background and aims The fungal pathogen Cryptococcus neoformans exhibits distinctive features of cell surface organisation that contribute to stress tolerance, immune evasion, and antifungal resistance. However, how membrane lipid asymmetry and higher-order membrane organisation shape Cryptococcus cell biology remains poorly understood. We aimed to define the cellular consequences of disrupting membrane asymmetry in C. neoformans. Methods We employed butyrolactol A (butA), a membrane-active natural product, as a chemical probe to perturb phospholipid asymmetry. Genetic analysis, resistance evolution, lipid profiling, and cell biological assays were integrated with microscopy-based approaches to examine membrane organisation, cell surface integrity, and stress responses in Cryptococcus. Results Perturbation of the phospholipid flippase complex Apt1–Cdc50 by butA leads to profound reorganisation of membrane lipids and loss of membrane asymmetry. These changes compromise cell surface integrity, alter sensitivity to cell wall stress, and expose conditional vulnerabilities to antifungal challenge. Notably, resistance analyses reveal a critical requirement for glucosylceramide-enriched membrane domains, uncovering a functional link between lipid asymmetry, membrane microenvironments, and cellular fitness in C. neoformans. Conclusions Our findings demonstrate that membrane asymmetry is a central determinant of Cryptococcus cell biology and stress adaptation. By using membrane-active natural products as mechanistic probes, this work highlights how lipid organisation governs fungal cell surface function and reveals non-canonical vulnerabilities that emerge from perturbing membrane homeostasis.

pres71.title: Carbon starvation induces coincident capsule and cell wall remodeling in Cryptococcus neoformans
pres71.abstract: The pathogenesis of Cryptococcus neoformans is largely attributed to the impact of the polysaccharide capsule on the survival of the fungus in the harsh conditions of the host. These conditions include a robust immune response and nutrient limitation in different tissues. Little is known about the survival mechanisms of C. neoformans in nutrient-deprived conditions, and a key unanswered question is whether the fungus can use capsule material for nutrition during conditions of carbon limitation. We addressed this question by measuring alterations in capsule and cell wall in response to carbon limitation and found an influence on capsule porosity and density without a change in diameter. RNA-seq analysis of the response to carbon limitation identified transcripts for enzymes with potential relevance to polysaccharide changes, including carbohydrate-active enzymes. Subsequently, the impact of a selected set of enzymes was evaluated with capsule and cell wall-relevant mutants l acking Cas1 and Cas3 (O-acetylation of capsule polysaccharide), Chs1-8 (chitin synthases), Cps1 (hyaluronic acid synthase), and Kre64 (?-glucan specific glycosidase). Overall, our findings show that C. neoformans responds to carbon starvation by increasing capsular and cell wall permeability through interactions between cell wall components (α- and ?-glucans and chitin) and capsules that alter their density and porosity. The results also suggest that C. neoformans does not substantially degrade the capsule polysaccharide under the conditions of carbon limitation employed in this study.

pres72.title: Perturbing Mps1 Kinase in the Cryptococcus neoformans titan cell, implications for a key morphological transition
pres72.abstract: C. neoformans ability to undergo a striking morphological transition within the human host to form large, polyploid ÄútitanÄù cells presents a unique biomechanical challenge. How the polyploid titan genome is organised for the reductive divisions which occur from titans, and how this process is regulated via the mitotic or meiotic machinery is little understood. Additionally, titans are thought to be important for virulence in cryptococcal disease, representing a potential novel therapeutic target. Here, we investigate the requirement for components of the Spindle Assembly Checkpoint (SAC) - a key mitotic quality-control mechanism - in titan formation and division. The SAC monitors the attachment of chromosomes to spindle microtubules during meiosis and mitosis, preventing cell-cycle progression until all chromosomes are correctly aligned; errors in this process lead to aneuploidy and cell death. We hypothesise that the polyploid nature of titan cells may render them susceptible to perturbation of the mitotic machinery, with downstream consequences for viability and virulence. We find that the SAC is not required for titan formation or reductive divisions, but that a central SAC-associated kinase, Mps1, is essential for both. Perturbation of Mps1 leads to reduced titan formation, morphological and nuclear organisation defects, and severely decreased viability in the few titans that form. Furthermore, a chromosome 6 disomy is consistently observed in mps1Δ strains and is enriched among surviving mps1Δ titan daughters. These findings reveal a SAC-independent role for Mps1 in titan morphogenesis and ploidy control, and raise intriguing questions regarding the regulation of this unique morphological state.

pres73.title: Structure-function analysis of the RNAi machinery in Cryptococcus
pres73.abstract: RNAi is a conserved mechanism of gene regulation in eukaryotes. Central to the pathway are the Argonaute proteins which, using small RNAs as guides, act to recognise and silence specific targets. In Cryptococcus, the primary targets of the RNAi machinery are the transposable elements. Loss of the RNAi mechanism in Cryptococcus has been associated with increased antifungal resistance and pathogenesis. Beyond a role in transposon regulation, however, the mechanisms and wider functions of RNAi in Cryptococcus remain poorly understood. We aim to characterise the proteins involved in Cryptococcus RNAi, with a particular focus on the essential complexes such as the Argonaute-containing RNA-induced silencing complex (RISC): the molecular machinery responsible for mediating silencing. Initial sequence alignments and structural modelling of the Cryptococcus Argonaute proteins in AlphaFold3 have revealed an insert rich in RGG motifs, a feature uncommon in orthologs that could mediate novel interactions and/or regulation. Additionally, Argonaute immunoprecipitation followed by mass spectrometry has identified several RNAi-associated proteins whose roles in Cryptococcus remain undetermined. To characterise the functional complexes, we are purifying Argonaute proteins directly from Cryptococcus, and isolating Argonaute-containing complexes through size exclusion chromatography for analysis with mass spectrometry and cryo-EM. By investigating the components and assembly principles of the RNAi protein complexes, and probing the mechanics of RNAi-mediated silencing through structure-guided separation-of-function mutations in cells, this work will provide valuable insights into the relationship between RNAi and infectivity in Cryptococcus.

pres74.title: Expanding insight into the Cryptococcus neoformans proteome and morphotypes through proteomic and covariation analyses
pres74.abstract: As we develop molecular toolkits to study Cryptococcus neoformans, an area that is lacking is a detailed annotation of its proteome. With 60% of C. neoformans proteins still uncharacterised, this is an area sorely in need of further attention. High-throughput studies have aided this endeavour through co-expression analyses and phenotypic screens; however, to date, no high-throughput studies have incorporated proteomics. Here I have established a workflow for proteomics covariation analysis, a method which can be used to identify functional connections between proteins by measuring how their abundances fluctuate together over different conditions. We aim to complement the exciting findings from previous high throughput screens, by using this proteomics workflow. In the process, we hope to discover basidiomycete specific pathways which will aid in the identification of future drug targets. I am also developing methods to enrich for clinically relevant morphotypes, titans and titanides, which remain uncharacterised in their transcriptome and proteome. Through comparisons with their typical yeast morphotype, we hope to identify morphotype specific markers and pathways which would aid further study. This work will contribute towards a growing understanding of how a master of stress adaption, C. neoformans, promotes successful infections and improve our appreciation of a world of basidiomycete specific protein co-regulation networks that we are only just beginning to uncover.

pres75.title: Regulation of Cryptococcus Argonaute by arginine methylation
pres75.abstract: Cryptococcus fungi are opportunistic pathogens that can cause life-threatening infection in humans. An important factor in Cryptococcus virulence is its genetic variability, often derived from transposable element (TE)-mediated mutations. TE repression is commonly achieved via RNA interference (RNAi), a conserved gene regulatory pathway mediated by Argonaute (Ago) proteins and guided by small RNA (sRNA). In plants and mammals, some Argonaute family proteins can be regulated through arginine methylation catalysed by the protein arginine methyltransferase 5 (Prmt5), and subsequent binding by Tudor staphylococcal nuclease (TSN). Notably, Cryptococcus Agos carry the signature RGG-rich motif targeted by Prmt5 in other organisms, suggesting they may also be subject to methylation-dependent regulation. Consistent with this, our immunoprecipitation/mass-spectrometry analyses in Cryptococcus deneoformans identified that the TSN homolog coimmunoprecipitates with Agos, and found methy lation of the Agos RGG-rich motifs. Furthermore, deletion of the Ago1 RGG motif results in increased expression of the LINE1 retrotransposon, and decreased LINE1 sRNA accumulation . I further aim to dissect (i) the role of the RGG motif in Argonaute localisation and function and (ii) how methylation of the RGG motif contributes to its function. This study will shed light onto the post-translational regulation of Cryptococcus Argonautes, and their impact on TE repression.

pres76.title: Investigating the role of Cyclins in the Cryptococcus neoformans cell cycle
pres76.abstract: Many mechanisms underpinning the cell-cycle of Cryptococcus neoformans remain poorly understood; for example, the role that cyclins and cyclin dependent kinases play in its regulation. While these proteins have been identified and have known homologues in other fungi, their functions have not been characterised in Cryptococcus. My project, therefore, focuses on investigating the cyclins Cln1, Clb2, and Clb3 and Cdk1 (a cyclin dependent kinase) to which cell-cycle checkpoints that they regulate and the mechanisms underpinning this regulation. In other fungi, homologues of these factors have been shown to be involved in both the G1/S checkpoint and the G2/M checkpoint However, the study of Cdk1 and Clb2 are complicated by the fact that they are essential. I will use an auxin degron system which allows for the conditional degradation of these proteins from cells. This, combined with studying cellular DNA content via flowcytometry, allows us to see which checkpoints these cyclins are involved in by determining what percentage of the population is 1C or 2C following degradation. Furthermore, in the related fungi Ustilago maydis, the homologue for Clb3 is a significant virulence factor due to its role in ploidy changes and morphogenesis. Since Titan cell formation, a key cryptococcal pathogenic process, remains mechanistically uncharacterised but is known to involve changes in ploidy and morphology, I will investigate if the removal of Clb3 impairs this. Titan cell formation has been linked to cyclins previously, with Cln1 knockouts upregulating it. Therefore, I will uncover the function of cyclins in these important mechanisms.

pres77.title: Unravelling kinetochore assembly principles of the critical fungal pathogen, C.neoformans
pres77.abstract: Cryptococcus neoformans is a fungal pathogen known to cause meningitis in humans, particularly dangerous for immunocompromised individuals. An estimated 220,000 annual cases of Cryptococcal meningitis occur worldwide. Increasing resistance of the pathogen to antifungal drugs, due to the high genetic diversity of C. neoformans, is a growing concern. During human infections, many C. neoformans cells (~5µm diameter haploid yeast form) undergo a striking morphological transition. They increase size to form thick-walled 10-100µm diameter cells, called polyploid titan cells. Remarkably, through an unknown reductive division mechanism polyploid titan cells bud off viable haploid progeny. However, not much is known about how this division is achieved. Thus, knowledge of the chromosome segregation machinery is crucial to uncover therapeutic vulnerabilities of this pathogen. The kinetochore is a multi-subunit protein complex that is key for the segregation of the genetic material during cell division. Interestingly, a study by Sridhar et al., 2021 revealed that the kinetochore composition of C. neoformans is significantly divergent compared to canonical yeast and human systems, with a loss of the whole Constitutive Centromere Associated Network (CCAN) except for CENP-C and its replacement by a single novel kinetochore protein called Bridgin. Here we aim to understand the molecular mechanisms of accurate chromosome segregation in C. neoformans by recombinantly reconstituting the components of the C. neoformans kinetochore and structurally characterising them using X-ray crystallography and CryoEM. Our analysis shows striking differences in the organisation of even seemingly conserved components of kinetochore and evidences of Aurora-B kinase mediated regulation of kinetochore assembly.

pres78.title: Cryptococcus spore germination can occur in the absence of oxygen
pres78.abstract: Because invasive cryptococcosis is caused by fulminant yeast growth, Cryptococcus spores must germinate into yeast to cause disease in mammalian hosts. Our lab used a high-throughput screen of small molecules to identify novel germination inhibitors. We discovered one class of compounds that presumably targets complex II of the electron transport chain (ETC) in a fungal-specific manner. Although yeast (obligate aerobes) cannot grow in the presence of ETC inhibitors or in anaerobic environments, we discovered that spores can germinate in the absence of detectable oxygen. This calls into question the need for ETC function and the mechanism of complex II inhibitors during germination. These paradoxical findings reveal a substantial knowledge gap in how respiration and metabolism operate during the energy-intensive process of germination. To evaluate the role of the ETC in germination, we tested inhibitors of ETC complexes and conducted transcriptomic analyses on spores germina ting hypoxically. Our data indicate that ETC function is important for hypoxic germination, including a role for the cyanide-independent alternative oxidase (AOX1). Future experiments will determine the unique and potentially targetable mechanisms of cellular respiration in germinating Cryptococcus spores, including evaluating potential roles of non-oxygen terminal electron acceptor pathways.

pres79.title: Titan Cell Production Increases In Vivo Diversity and can Enhance Antifungal Drug Resistance.
pres79.abstract: Titan cells are large polyploid cells that play a role in virulence of C. neoformans and make up about 30% of the cell population in vivo. Previous in vitro studies showed that titan cells have an increased probability of randomly generating progeny with increased resistance to the antifungal drug fluconazole. Since titan cells are a minority of the in vivo cell population, we then asked is titan cell production biologically relevant for in vivo development of antifungal drug resistance? To address this question, we utilized the mouse inhalational model of cryptococcosis and infected mice with the laboratory reference strain KN99α that generated titan cells, and a congenic gpr4Δ gpr5Δ deletion strain with minimal titan cell production. Mice were either left untreated or treated with 10 mg/kg of fluconazole. At 14 days post infection, lungs were homogenized and serially plated in the presence of increasing concentrations of fluconazole. We then enumerated colony forming u nits (CFU) and colony size to quantify levels of fluconazole resistance (IC50), heteroresistance, and tolerance. No difference in IC50 or tolerance was observed with and without titan cell formation. However, higher rates of heteroresistance were observed with titan cell production that was independent of in vivo fluconazole exposure. These data suggest that in vivo titan cell formation is biologically relevant through the generation of increased population diversity that can influence antifungal resistance.

pres80.title: The glycosyltransferase Goe1 (Glucan Organizing Enzyme 1) contributes to cell wall integrity and echinocandin resistance
pres80.abstract: The lingering mystery of why Cryptococcus neoformans resists echinocandins, which inhibit Fks1-mediated synthesis of cell wall ?-1,3-glucan, underscores how much of this organism’s biology remains poorly understood. It also highlights the importance of identifying additional enzymes that contribute to cell wall integrity. We recently discovered the putative glycosyltransferase Glucan Organizing Enzyme 1 (Goe1). Cells lacking Goe1 (goe1Δ) have defects in two cell wall glucans: ç?-1,4- and ?-1,3-. Intriguingly, upon treatment with echinocandins, goe1Δ struggles to grow. To understand this, I am interrogating Goe1’s enzymatic activity and how it influences Fks1. Surprisingly, I found that Goe1 resides in the Golgi. This suggests it does not work directly on cell wall glucans, although it could potentially impact Fks1 activity through several mechanisms. So far, I have found through RT-qPCR that FKS1 expression is not perturbed in goe1Δ, while yeast two-hybrid studies suggest that Goe1 interacts with an intracellular domain of Fks1. This suggests that protein interactions may be involved, possibly relevant for Fks1 trafficking. Finally, I showed by imaging that Fks1 is mislocalized in goe1Δ. One model to explain these disparate observations is that Goe1 acts in lipid glycosylation. Preliminary analysis of glycolipids extracted from wild-type and goe1Δ cells by thin layer chromatography and mass spectrometry suggests that Goe1 may contribute to the synthesis of glycoinositol phosphoceramide (GIPC) lipids, which are particularly elaborate in C. neoformans. Together, my data implicate Goe1 in GIPC synthesis and suggest a novel model for the role of cryptococcal GIPCs in Fks1 function and echinocandin resistance.

pres81.title: Defining Mechanisms of Cryptococcal Sterol Transport
pres81.abstract: The critical membrane lipid ergosterol must distribute between its site of synthesis and key membranes. Specialised lipid transport proteins coordinate the movement of sterols and other lipids at membrane contact sites, sub-30 nm spaces between adjacent membranes. These proteins represent an unexplored aspect of cryptococcal ergosterol biology that may reveal pharmacologically exploitable processes. Accordingly, we sought novel or understudied proteins that influence cryptococcal sterol homeostasis. To do this we screened for spontaneous mutants that suppress the growth defects of cells lacking the sterol transporter Ysp2. Whole genome sequencing of suppressors revealed numerous nucleotide polymorphisms in coding regions directly relevant to sterol biosynthesis, regulation, and transport; this strongly validated the utility of our screen for studying sterol homeostasis. Excitingly, one unstudied gene encodes a protein with a predicted phospholipid ligand-binding domain, a f eature linked to lipid transporters. We hypothesised that this protein affects sterol transport indirectly, possibly through a mechanism involving a phospholipid ligand. Subsequent characterization revealed roles of the gene in lipid homeostasis, cell morphology, stress tolerance, and virulence, while microscopy suggests the encoded protein localises at ER-plasma membrane contact sites. Further, in vitro studies of the exogenously expressed protein suggest a non-sterol lipid ligand. These results support our working model that this novel protein associates with membrane microdomains enriched with its lipid ligand and influences sterol transport through a non-sterol transport mechanism. Current studies aim to further define its role in sterol transport and potentially inhibit its activity. Overall, this work will illuminate the fundamental process of sterol transport.

pres82.title: Neuropathological differences driven by cryptococcal species in a murine model of infection
pres82.abstract: Cryptococcal meningitis (CM) is a fatal brain infection associated with debilitating neurological sequelae. CM research has historically focused on understanding the neuropathology of Cryptococcus neoformans-related infections, neglecting C. gattii -related CM. Recent rises in C. gattii-related CM, coupled with the fact that it predominantly affects immunocompetent individuals, highlights a dire need for experimental models for studying its neuropathogenesis. This study aimed to optimize a model of C. gattii infection and compare its neuropathogenesis to C. neoformans. We intravenously infected 8–10-week-old C57BL/6 mice with 5x104 colony forming units of C. gattii and C. neoformans. We monitored disease progression by measuring fungal burden, body mass gain and survival rates. Histological analysis was performed on 1, 3, 6 days post-infection (dpi) and experimental endpoint assessing neuropathological damage. Brain and serum cytokines were measured using multiplex assays. We observed marked differences in the neuropathogenesis of C. neoformans– and C. gattii–associated CM. C. neoformans infection progressed rapidly, mice succumbed within a week post-infection, compared to nearly one month for C. gattii. Despite longer incubation periods observed during C. gattii infection, end-stage disease in both models was characterized by comparable fungal burdens. Large cryptococcal lesions were evident as early as 6 d.p.i in C. neoformans-infected mice, while C. gattii was associated with smaller lesions. Cytokine analysis further revealed significant species-specific differences in inflammatory profiles across d.p.i. These findings highlight distinct temporal and pathological features of C. neoformans and C. gattii infection and support the utility of this model for investigating C. gattii–induced CM.

pres83.title: IFN-? counteracts type II immunity to promote protection during pulmonary cryptococcosis
pres83.abstract: Cryptococcus neoformans is a leading cause of opportunistic mycosis and remains a major driver of HIV-associated mortality. Protection in immunocompetent hosts is linked to Th1/ IFN-? responses, yet mechanisms of control remain unclear, particularly because STAT6-dependent type II immunity (IL-4/IL-13) correlates with non-protective outcomes in the lung. Using a chronic, granuloma-inducing pulmonary cryptococcosis model, we unexpectedly found that IFN-? signaling was not required for protection when type II signaling was removed. Specifically, combined loss of STAT6 and IFN-?R1 preserved host survival and limited fungal burden, suggesting that the protective function of IFN-? is context-dependent and may primarily reflect antagonism of type II–driven susceptibility rather than an obligate role in fungal clearance. To define hematopoietic-intrinsic requirements for IFN-?, we generated competitive mixed bone marrow chimeras. In this setting, IFN-?-deficient cells were selectively underrepresented within the monocyte-derived Ly6C-high macrophage compartment, whereas this defect was rescued when STAT6 was concurrently absent. These data implicate Ly6C-high macrophages as a key cellular node where IFN-? counterbalances STAT6-dependent type II signals to promote control of pulmonary cryptococcosis. Together, our findings support a model in which IFN-? promotes antifungal protection primarily by counteracting type II/STAT6-mediated suppression of Ly6Chigh macrophages linked to fungal control.

pres84.title: IL-5R? deletion controls cryptococcal meningoencephalitis development
pres84.abstract: Main: Cryptococcal meningoencephalitis (CM) is the result of CNS entrance of Cryptococcus neoformans (CN). Current treatment options are limited and result in long-lasting side-effects post-infection. Better understanding of the host immune response is necessary to develop immunomodulatory treatments. Results: To uncover immunological drivers of CN extrapulmonary dissemination and CNS entrance, we used an intranasal mouse infection model, advanced in vivo microscopic and mesoscopic imaging, proteomics, and transcriptomics to build a 3D spatiotemporal map. We found that pulmonary infection induced eosinophil expansion in the early stage of disease. Degranulating eosinophils were recruited in airways and alveoli, affecting the integrity and morphology of the airway epithelium. Deletion of the eosinophil lineage protein IL-5Rα resulted in lower CNS fungal burden. Mice lacking IL-5Rα had significantly lower expression of proteins associated with eosinophil degranulation, activation, antimicrobial killing, and airway epithelium distress, indicating a detrimental role of eosinophil activation during pulmonary cryptococcosis. Interestingly, IL-5Rα deficiency resulted in an increased IFN-? response during lung infection prior to brain infection, but resulted in lower IFN ? levels once brain i nfection was established. This suggests an ambiguous role of IL-5Rα regulated IFN-? in a time dependent manner during CM development. Future perspectives: We uncover IL-5Rα as novel potential therapeutic target for cryptococcosis. To further develop IL-5Rα targeting as immunomodulatory treatment strategy, we are unravelling the underlying molecular mechanism of IL-5Rα expressing cells through scRNA-seq of bronchoalveolar lavage fluid, blood, and leptomeninges, with data analysis and validation of results with functional in vivo and in vitro experiments currently underway.

pres85.title: The Role of CD4+ T Cells in Hypervirulence of the Clinical Isolate UgCl422 of Cryptococcus neoformans
pres85.abstract: Cryptococcus neoformans is an environmentally acquired fungal pathogen and the leading cause of cryptococcal meningitis, a life-threatening disease that primarily affects immunocompromised individuals. Disease severity is shaped by both host genetic background and fungal virulence traits. Distinct clinical isolates can drive divergent disease outcomes depending on host immune context, including hypervirulent infections characterized by excessive inflammation. The clinical isolate UgCl422 induces hypervirulent disease in A/J mice, marked by elevated IFN-? production and a mixed Type 1/Type 17 immune response, while causing typical disease in C57BL/6 mice. Although IFN-? is generally protective during cryptococcosis, excessive levels correlate with worsened disease outcome, suggesting immune dysregulation as a driver of pathology. The cellular source of IFN-? during UgCl422 infection remains unclear. Based on clinical observations that HIV-infected patients with depleted C D4+ T cells survived cryptococcal meningitis, we hypothesized that CD4+ T cells contribute to excessive IFN-? production during UgCl422 infection. To test this, A/J mice were intranasally infected with UgCl422 or the reference strain KN99α, followed by selective CD4+ T-cell depletion. Lung fungal burden, cytokine profiles, and survival were assessed, and CD4+ depletion was confirmed by flow cytometry. CD4+ T-cell depletion abolished the IFN-? spike associated with UgCl422 infection and altered disease progression, demonstrating that CD4+ T cells are required for pathogenic IFN-? responses. These findings reveal a critical role for CD4+ T cells in driving hypervirulence and provide insight into immune-mediated pathology during cryptococcal infection.

pres86.title: GM-CSF regulates monocyte and granulocyte response during Cryptococcus gattii infection
pres86.abstract: Autoantibodies against the pleiotropic cytokine GM-CSF have been linked to invasive infections by the fungal pathogen Cryptococcus gattii in apparently immunocompetent patients. Using a murine model of respiratory infection, this study investigated the potential role of GM-CSF (or CSF2) in the host immune response to C. gattii.
