Initially, we found that GM-CSF secretion is induced in the lungs of wild type (WT) C57BL/6J mice when given a fatal inoculum of C. gattii. In infected Csf2-/- mice, there is significantly decreased lung fungal burden, reduced inflammatory lung damage, and improved survival compared to WT. Conversely, intranasal administration of recombinant GM-CSF to WT mice increases lung fungal burden. We determined that GM-CSF promotes monocyte differentiation into alveolar and interstitial macrophages during C. gattii infection and that ablation of these cells in infected CCR2-DTR+ mice improves host outcomes, similar to Csf2-/- mice. Both alveolar and interstitial macrophages in WT mice skew towards M2 polarization, and in vitro, WT BMDMs exhibit poor anti-fungal activity against C. gattii. Additionally, we found that infected WT mice have increased eosinophil over neutrophil influx into the lung, in contrast to Csf2-/- and CCR2-DTR+ mice. Ablation of neutrophils in infected Mrp8cretg iDTR+ mic e increased lung fungal burden, indicating that the relative absence of neutrophils in infected WT lungs may also impair fungal control. Our results highlight that GM-CSF plays a detrimental role in host responses to C. gattii by regulating the presence of monocyte-derived macrophages and granulocytes in the lungs and subverting their effector functions to benefit the fungus.

pres87.title: Human Brain Organoids: A New Model to Study Cryptococcus neoformans Neurotropism
pres87.abstract: With the rise in immunocompromised individuals and patients with immune-related comorbidities such as COVID-19, the rate of fungal infections is growing. This increase, along with the current plateau in antifungal drug development, has made understanding the pathogenesis and dissemination of these organisms more pertinent than ever. The mouse model of fungal infection, while informative on a basic scientific level, has severe limitations in terms of translation to the human disease. Here we present data supporting the implementation of the human cerebral organoid model, which is generated from human embryonic stem cells and accurately recapitulates relevant brain cell types and structures, to study fungal infection and dissemination to the central nervous system (CNS). This approach provides direct insight into the relevant pathogenesis of specific fungal organisms in human tissues where in vivo models are impossible. With this model system we assessed the specific brain tr opisms and cellular effects of fungal pathogens known to cross the blood–brain barrier (BBB), such as Cryptococcus neoformans. We determined the effects of this fungal pathogen on the overall gross morphology, cellular architecture, and cytokine release from these model organoids. Furthermore, we demonstrated that C. neoformans penetrates and invades the organoid tissue and remains present throughout the course of infection. These results demonstrate the utility of this new model to the field and highlight the potential for this system to elucidate fungal pathogenesis to develop new therapeutic strategies to prevent and treat the disseminated stages of fungal diseases such as cryptococcal meningitis.

pres88.title: When Immunity Turns Pathogenic: Monocyte-Derived iNOS Protects Against Lethal Immunopathology in Cryptococcal Meningitis
pres88.abstract: Inducible nitric oxide synthase (iNOS) is a pivotal regulator of host defense, yet its role in balancing antimicrobial activity against tissue damage during Cryptococcal meningitis (CM) remains undefined. Here, we demonstrate that iNOS functions as an essential "brake" on lethal CNS inflammation rather than a primary antifungal effector. Using a murine model of CM, we found that Nos2-deficient mice infected with Cryptococcus neoformans (H99) exhibited fulminant neurological decline, rapid cachexia, and early mortality compared to wild-type controls. Crucially, this accelerated lethality was uncoupled from fungal burden, driven instead by extensive CNS immune infiltration and blood-brain barrier disruption. Using CCR2creNos2flox mice, we identified inflammatory monocytes as the obligatory source of protective iNOS. Mechanistically, the loss of nitric oxide-mediated feedback inhibition locked myeloid cells in a hyper-inflammatory state, driving uncontrolled CCL2, IFN?, and I L-1α/? production. This cytokine storm provoked pathological neutrophil recruitment. Strikingly, neutrophil depletion rescued Nos2-deficient mice from early mortality. These results mirror clinical observations in human CM cohorts, where reduced iNOS expression correlates with poor neurological outcomes despite antifungal therapy. Collectively, our data establishes iNOS as a vital suppressor of neurotoxicity, suggesting that host-directed immunometabolic therapies targeting the iNOS-NO axis may prevent lethal immunopathology in CM.

pres89.title: Neuroimmune Responses at Brain Borders During Cryptococcal Infection
pres89.abstract: Background and aims: We previously showed that microglia respond poorly to brain-infiltrating Cryptococcus (52D strain) and remain largely inactive until CD4+ T cells enter the brain and secrete IFN? (PMID: 41330370). Thus, CD4+ T-cell infiltration precedes microglial activation. This suggests that other immune sentinels initiate leukocyte entry into the brain. We hypothesized that border-associated macrophages (BAMs) in brain border tissues detect Cryptococcus early and initiate leukocyte recruitment. Methods: We examined the kinetics of BAM responses in meningeal and other brain border tissues following Cryptococcus infection. We assessed whether BAM activation is associated with local inflammatory responses and whether these responses could promote leukocyte entry into the brain, potentially by altering blood–brain barrier (BBB) integrity. Results: BAMs responded rapidly to Cryptococcus infection, preceding detectable microglial activation. This response was accompanied by localized inflammatory changes in brain border tissues, suggesting early immune activation at these sites. Conclusions: Cryptococcus stealthily infiltrates the brain by exploiting the delayed responsiveness of microglia. In contrast, BAMs act as early sentinels that detect infection and appear to orchestrate the initial inflammatory events that enable leukocyte infiltration into the brain, highlighting brain border tissues as important sites in early antifungal immune responses.

pres90.title: To CT or Not CT?' That is the question, but LP is the answer
pres90.abstract: Background Lumbar puncture (LP) is required for diagnosis in those suspected of having meningitis, but carries risks. The utility of pre-LP brain imaging to identify space-occupying lesions and prevent adverse events is debated. Methods We prospectively enrolled Ugandan adults with HIV-associated meningitis from August 2013 to November 2024. All participants received a diagnostic LP upon hospital admission without prior head CT. Death within 24 hours post-LP served as a surrogate marker for assessing post-LP complications. Risk factors, including baseline laboratory results, focal neurological deficits, Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score, brain imaging findings, and meningitis etiology, were assessed. Results Of 3,552 participants, 83 (2.3%) died within 24 hours. GCS≤8 (16% (13/83) vs survivors, 4% (138/3469; p<0.001), higher median baseline CSF opening pressure (392 mmHÇO [IQR: 173–542] vs survivors 200 mmHÇO [IQR: 125-300], p<0.001), and focal neurologic deficits 20% (16/80) vs survivors 10% (360/3,444; p=0.009) were associated with death within 24 hours post-LP. Focal neurologic deficits were associated with increased odds of 24-hour mortality (unadjusted OR=2.08, 95%CI 1.15–3.53; adjusted OR=3.10, 95%CI, 1.21–7.29). Of those with CNS imaging, 221 had a relative contraindication to LP on imaging, of whom 0.9% (2/221) died within 24 hours of LP versus 0.8% (2/259) mortality among those whose imaging showed no contraindication (p=>0.99) Conclusion In persons with HIV-associated meningitis, GCS<8 and focal deficits on clinical examination were associated with death within 24 hours; brain imaging findings contraindicating LP weren't. Few brain imaging studies were done in those who died within 24 hours.

pres91.title: Differential activation and exhaustion of circulating T-cells and monocytes at baseline may determine risk of developing cryptococcal meningitis among individuals with HIV-associated asymptomatic cryptococcal antigenemia
pres91.abstract: Introduction: The role of host immune factors in the progression of asymptomatic cryptococcal antigenemia (CrAg) to cryptococcal meningitis outcomes are unclear. This study assessed immunological predictors of progression to meningitis despite fluconazole preemptive therapy. Methods: Twenty-one Ugandans adults living with HIV with asymptomatic CrAg and titers >=1:160 received fluconazole preemptive therapy. Six developed meningitis while 15 did not, surviving to 6-months without meningitis. Baseline peripheral blood mononuclear cells were analyzed via spectro-flow cytometry. Plasma fluconazole was measured by mass spectrometry at week 4. Results There were no significant differences in age, CrAg titers, CD4 counts, fluconazole concentrations (median 36 mcg/mL with meningitis vs 22 mcg/mL without meningitis), (p>0.05). Individuals who developed meningitis had lower %CD4+ T-cells, higher %CD8+ T-cells, and lower CD4/CD8 ratio (0.12 versus 0.20, p=0.020) than those without men ingitis. The %CD69 and CD163 activation marker expression on classical monocytes was lower in individuals who developed meningitis than those without (P=0.018; P=0.008, respectively). The %CCR4 expression on CD4+ T-cells, reflecting Th2 chemokine receptor, was higher in individuals with meningitis than those without. Individuals who developed meningitis had higher %PD-1 expression (immune cell exhaustion marker) on CD4+ T-cells , CD8+ T-cells, and on classical monocytes (P<0.05 for each). The CXCR3+/CCR4+ (Th1/Th2) ratio on CD4+ T-cells was lower among individuals with meningitis than those who without (0.635 versus 1.043, p=0.007), reflecting a greater Th2>Th1 skew. Conclusions: Lower CD4/CD8, Th1/Th2 ratios, divergent activation, and increased T-cell/monocyte exhaustion may increase the risk of developing meningitis among individuals with HIV-associated asymptomatic cryptococcal antigenemia

pres92.title: Differential recognition of C. neoformans by male and female human macrophages elicits contrasting immune responses
pres92.abstract: Cryptococcus neoformans (Cn) is a pathogenic yeast and the cause of cryptococcosis, a life-threatening fungal disease. Strikingly, ~70% of patients that develop cryptococcosis are male. Here, we investigated decreased Cn recognition by male macrophages and whether that affected the subsequent innate immune response. Human PBMCs were isolated from healthy male and female donors, differentiated into macrophages, and tested for immune response differences. Cn incubated with female serum showed increased binding of the opsonin serum amyloid P, possibly explaining why female macrophages show increased Cn phagocytosis. After infection, cytokine analysis showed increased secretion of proinflammatory cytokines at 24 h in male macrophages that were substantially decreased by 48 h. In contrast, female macrophages secreted fewer proinflammatory cytokines at both 24 and 48 h but had increased secretion of CCL5 (24 and 48 h) and IL-18 (48 h). These data suggested the male immune respons e was changing 24 h post-infection; thus, we tested macrophage activation state. Male macrophages were alternatively activated at 48 h, with increased secretion of arginase 1 whereas female macrophages were classically activated, with increased secretion of nitric oxide at 24 and 48 h. Cn genes related to capsule production were increased in Cn grown in male serum at 48 h. These data suggest that female macrophages better recognize Cn and become classically activated whereas male macrophages initiate an inflammatory response that is markedly reduced after 24 h, possibly due to testosterone-induced GXM secretion. Thus, antifungal therapeutics targeting testosterone-induced GXM release may improve outcomes in male patients with cryptococcosis.

pres93.title: Role of H2-K1 and Metabolism in Defining Innate Immunity to Cryptococcus neoformans
pres93.abstract: Cryptococcus neoformans, the etiological agent of cryptococcosis, can survive and replicate within host immune cells, contributing to the development of cryptococcal meningitis. Within the lung, C. neoformans interacts with innate immune cells, including macrophages and dendritic cells (DCs). Our previous work showed that Ly6C+ monocyte-like macrophages restrict fungal proliferation, whereas CD11b+ DCs promote fungal growth. Transcriptomic analysis revealed differential expression of the MHC class I gene H2-K1, Calreticulin (Calr), and multiple metabolic genes, leading us to hypothesize that antigen presentation and metabolic pathways regulate the intracellular fate of C. neoformans. To test this hypothesis, we performed siRNA-mediated knockdown (KD) of H2-K1 in J774 macrophages and bone marrow–derived DCs (BMDCs). H2-K1 KD significantly reduced antifungal activity in both J774 cells and BMDCs. Additionally, H2-K1 KD resulted in reduced Calr expression suggesting H2-k1 gene has downstream effects in the pathway. We also observed a significant decrease in reactive oxygen species (ROS) production in H2-K1 KD cells. Notably, H2-K1 knockdown did not significantly alter glycolytic pathway, suggesting that H2-K1–mediated ROS production and metabolic reprogramming function through independent mechanisms. Further investigation of the pentose phosphate pathway (PPP) revealed that PPP inhibition significantly reduced cryptococcal CFUs while increasing ROS levels. Ongoing studies are examining whether the reduced ROS production observed in H2-K1 silenced cells is PPP dependent. These findings indicate that the H2-K1 gene and metabolic reprogramming are critical for effe ctive antifungal responses. Our data provide a foundation for future studies aimed at targeting host immune pathways to enhance antifungal immunity.

pres94.title: The Mannose Receptor (MR) on CD169+ myeloid cells modulates the generation or maintenance of anti-fungal Tbet+ stem-like progenitors
pres94.abstract: The mannose receptor (MR) is a C-type lectin receptor broadly expressed on myeloid cells and often considered a marker of immunosuppressive ÄúM2-likeÄù macrophages. However, recent data have identified immunostimulatory roles for MR, highlighting its complexity and context-dependent functions. Hence, we sought to investigate the precise functional role(s) of MR in modulating T cell fate decisions using the highly mannosylated, CD4 T cell-dependent fungal pathogen Cryptococcus neoformans as a model. At 10 days post intranasal infection with KN99alpha C. neoformans, MR-deficient mice exhibited decreased Tbet+ Th1 cells in the TCF1high stem-like progenitor compartment in both the lungs and lung-draining lymph nodes (ldLN), while terminal effector Th1 cells and other Th subsets were unaffected. Using mixed bone marrow chimeras to selectively deplete MR from specific myeloid populations, we found this defect occurred only when MR was deleted from CD169+ cells, but not from cDC1s or cDC2s. Furthermore, loss of MHCII within the CD169+ compartment similarly impaired Tbet+ progenitor generation. Notably, MR+ CD169+ cells in the ldLN expressed IL12p40 and formed direct contact with CD4 T cells, identifying these cells as a previously unrecognized bona fide antigen-presenting cell population that utilizes MR to selectively license Tbet expression on stem-like progenitor cells. Finally, to determine functional relevance, we infected mice with the gcs1delta C. neoformans strain which establishes a chronic infection in which protection is mediated by Th1 immunity. We found that MR-deficient mice had decreased survival compared to controls, suggesting that MR-dependent Th1 progenitor maintenance is required for effective long-term antifungal immunity.

pres95.title: Cryptococcal Meningitis Cerebrospinal Fluid Cellular Immune Response: From Sterile Cerebrospinal Fluid to Low and High Fungal Burden Disease
pres95.abstract: Introduction Cryptococcal meningitis is a major cause of serious meningitis in people with advanced HIV. While the disease is linked to inflammation, little is known about how the fungal burden relates to the immune response in the central nervous system-associated neuroinflammation. Methods We studied 211 consenting adults presenting with HIV-related cryptococcal meningitis. We characterized baseline CSF cellular immune responses using spectral flow cytometry. We grouped participants into three unique visualized groups using unsupervised UMAP and PCA CSF quantitative fungal culture clusters: sterile (n=21), with low fungal burden (≤15,000 CFU/mL, n=77), and high fungal burden (>15,000 CFU/mL, n=113) groups separated at approximately the median CFUs. Results Higher CSF fungal burden was inversely associated with lower peripheral CD4+ counts, CSF white cell counts by microscopy, and CSF protein (all; P≤0.035). Subsequently, CSF fungal burden was inversely associated with CSF total CD3+ leukocytes, CD4+ T cells (r= -0.28), natural killer cells (r-0.13), neutrophils (r=-0.17), and dendritic cells (r=-16) as defined by flow cytometry (all Pearson r; P≤0.013). Fungal burden was positively associated with CD4+ T cells expressing Th1-polarized CXCR3+ chemokine receptor (median 24% high culture, 13% low, and 15% sterile groups; P=0.048), and immune checkpoint PD-1+ expression on CD8+ T cells (66% high culture, 61% low culture, and 53% sterile; P=0.009). Conclusion Higher CSF quantitative fungal culture burdens were associated with reduced absolute CD4+ T cells, NK cells, neutrophils, and dendritic cells, yet elevated PD-1 checkpoint expression among CD8+ T cells, and elevations in CD4+ T cells expressing Th1-polarized CXCR3+ inflammation-activated chemokine receptor.

pres96.title: Mechanistic target of rapamycin (MTOR) signaling pathway loss-of-function variants in previously healthy patients with non-HIV cryptococcosis.
pres96.abstract: Cryptococcosis primarily affects individuals with advanced immunosuppression, including those with HIV/AIDS, solid organ transplants, or prolonged corticosteroid exposure; however, little is known about whether host genetic factors might predispose certain individuals to develop disease independent of known risk factors. Identification of genetic defects in human patients is an important Äúnatural experimentÄù that can reveal important human traits required for host disease susceptibility and resistance. In the present studies, genetic analysis of a large cohort of previously healthy (PH) patients who developed infections with Cryptococcus were investigated to determine underlying immune deficiencies leading to susceptibility. In a cohort of 138 PH patients with cryptococcosis, whole exome sequencing was performed, identifying rare alleles predicted to have deleterious functional consequences (CADD >30). ToppGene pathway analysis revealed MTOR signaling pathway defects as enriched in this patient population, with 50% carrying predicted deleterious variants, including 5 in MTOR. Functional analysis showed that these MTOR variants were associated with T cell dysfunction. Mouse models of MTOR deficiency– chemically (rapamycin) or genetically (CD4-Cre MTOR-floxed), exhibited increased susceptibility to cryptococcosis: reduced survival, increased fungal burdens, and diminished lymphocyte recruitment to sites of infection. Moreover, adoptively transferred MTOR-deficient CD4 T cells failed to migrate effectively during cryptococcosis. These findings are the first to identify loss-of-function MTOR variants in humans and highlight the crucial role of MTOR-related signaling in anti-cryptococcal adaptive responses. Ongoing experiments, utilizing MTOR-flox mice and human cells, aim to further elucidate MTOR's role in immunity and the unique susceptibility to this important fungal pathogen.

pres97.title: HIV-negative Colombian patients with Cryptococcus gattii and C. neoformans infection present autoantibodies neutralizing GM-CSF
pres97.abstract: Background and aims: Cryptococcosis is a life-threatening disease caused by Cryptococcus neoformans or Cryptococcus gattii. While autoantibodies (auto-Abs) neutralizing granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor (GM-CSF) in otherwise healthy adults with cryptococcal meningitis have been described since 2013, these findings have been scarcely reported in Latin America. The aim of this study was to search for neutralizing auto-Abs in sera collected from Colombian patients with non-HIV-associated cryptococcosis, from a retrospective national cohort from 1997 to 2016. Methods: Clinical and laboratory records were reviewed from the National Surveillance on cryptococcosis, and the presence of neutralizing anti-GM-CSF auto-Abs in sera from 30 HIV-negative adults with cryptococcosis was assessed by flow cytometry. Out of the patients, 13 had infection caused by C. gattii and 17 by C. neoformans. Results: Auto-Abs neutralizing GM-CSF were detected in the sera of 10 out of 13 ( 77%) patients with C. gattii infection and one out of 17 (6%) patients infected with C. neoformans. Conclusions: This study emphasizes that, in HIV-negative patients, the presence of auto-Abs that neutralize GM-CSF is an important risk factor for the development of cryptococcosis, particularly caused by C. gattii, which, in turn, shows the differences in the epidemiology of cryptococcosis caused by the two species. Notably, the disease-causing factors of almost two-thirds of the studied patients remain unknown. Additional inborn errors of immunity may explain some of these cases.

pres98.title: IFNg therapy remodels mammalian copper transporter ATP7A to support myeloid cell antifungal immunity
pres98.abstract: IFNg therapy has been shown to exert therapeutic benefits for cryptococcal meningitis patients, but the specific mechanisms of protection are not clear. We recently found that C. neoformans copper starvation within the brain is niche-specific where yeast residing intracellularly within microglia were protected from copper starvation. Notably, we observed that IFNg treatment of microglia resulted in copper starvation of intracellular fungi. We therefore tested the hypothesis that IFNg may exert protective antifungal immunity via the manipulation of intracellular copper pools in macrophages. We find that IFNg-treated macrophages have improved fungal killing capacity that was dependent on fungal copper homeostasis pathways. IFNg treatment did not affect total copper concentration in macrophages but caused significant remodelling of the mammalian copper exporter ATP7A, which co-localised near intracellular fungi following phagocytosis. When ATP7A was inhibited, intracellular fu ngal survival increased. In vivo, we found ATP7A expression correlated with fungicidal capacity of brain myeloid cells. Using microglia-specific IFN? receptor knockout mice (Sall1CreERIfngr1flox), we investigated how microglial defence against C. neoformans brain infection was affected by fungal copper homeostasis in the context of IFNg therapy. Our ongoing work has found that therapeutic benefits of IFN? treatment is dependent on microglia expression of IFN?R, which we are now exploring whether this is linked with myeloid polarisation status and fungal killing capacity. Taken together, these results highlight the dynamic interplay between copper homeostasis and host immune defence in the brain, providing new insights into protective mechanisms of IFNg therapy within the central nervous system during cryptococcal meningitis.

pres99.title: Characterization of monocyte-derived microglia-like cells as an in vitro model of Cryptococcus-microglia interactions.
pres99.abstract: Background and aims: Cryptococcus neoformans is a neurotropic fungal pathogen that causes lethal meningitis in immunocompromised individuals. Within the central nervous system, C. neoformans can use microglia, the resident macrophages of the brain, as a reservoir of infection and subvert their immune functions. However, microglia–Cryptococcus interactions remain poorly characterised, partially due to limitations of murine models and immortalised microglial cell lines. Monocyte-derived microglia-like cells (MDMLs) provide a tractable primary human alternative. Here, we aimed to characterise MDML responses to C. neoformans and assess their suitability as an in vitro model of cryptococcal meningitis. Methods: Human peripheral blood mononuclear cells were isolated by density gradient centrifugation, monocytes enriched by attachment, then differentiated into MDMLs using IL-34 and GM-CSF over 14 days. Differentiation was confirmed by immunocytochemistry. MDMLs were infected with serum-opsonised C. neoformans (H99E), and phagocytosis a were assessed by immunocytochemistry. Results: MDML differentiation was confirmed by increased expression of the microglial markers Iba1 and TMEM119 and morphological changes consistent with microglial identity, compared to donor-matched monocytes. In a pilot experiment, MDMLs efficiently phagocytosed C. neoformans, with a proportion of phagocytic cells comparable to iPSC-derived microglia, but with significantly higher fungal uptake per cell. Conclusions: Future experiments will robustly describe immunological phenotypes of MDMLs to C. neoformans via immunocytochemistry, ELISAs, and microbial proliferation assays. These will inform the potential of MDMLs as a robust and scalable human in vitro model for studying microglial–fungal interactions, enabling mechanistic dissection of cryptococcal neuroimmune evasion.

pres100.title: Central role of regulatory T cells in protecting brain from inflammatory damage in cryptococcal meningoencephalitis
pres100.abstract: Unchecked T-cell inflammation in the brain contributes to the progression of cryptococcal meningoencephalitis (CM), including neuronal damage and apoptosis. We examined the function of regulatory T-cells (Tregs) during CM in maintaining immune balance and their precise role/mechanisms in preventing pathological neuroinflammation. We found that Tregs are present in the CSF of CM patients and accumulate in the mouse brain over the course of disease progression, albeit with some delay compared to the effector T-cells. Treg depletion resulted in exacerbated neurological symptoms and increased mouse mortality to a 100%, despite a reduction in fungal burden. This was due to increased Th1-inflammation, neuronal damage and apoptosis. We found that Treg recruitment into the brain largely depended on CCR8-CCL1. Tregs were the major source of anti-inflammatory IL- 10, of which deletion phenocopied Treg depletion in our CM model. Tregs, in CM brain, were also an important source of Amp hiregulin (Areg), a trophic, neuroprotective factor involved in tissue repair. Interestingly, Treg-restricted knockdown of Areg suppressed IFN-? production by Teffs, but ultimately did not change the symptoms, pathology, or mouse survival. Finally, Treg-expanding immunotherapy with low-dose IL-2 vastly improved the CM-mouse neurological score, preserved survival, and reduced inflammation, neuronal damage, and apoptosis. In conclusion, CCR8-CCL1-recruited Tregs are critical for maintaining immune balance in the brain during CM. Harnessing these cells could be exploited therapeutically as a strategy to mitigate CM immunopathology and fine-tune the protective immune responses in the brain for optimal clinical outcomes.

pres101.title: Development and validation of an experimental evolution model for Cryptococcus neoformans during extreme temperature events.
pres101.abstract: Cryptococcosis, an invasive fungal infection typically of the brain, is primarily caused by Cryptococcus neoformans, a yeast found ubiquitously in the environment. Pathogenicity of C. neoformans is thought to be a secondary effect of adapting to the environment. With climate change expected to alter the environment in various ways, there are concerns that this may drive increased virulence in fungi. Extreme temperature events have been suggested as a potential driver for virulence in fungi due to the necessity of all human fungal pathogens to tolerate mammalian endothermy. Additionally, pathways associated with virulence and antimicrobial resistance are upregulated in response to heat stress in C. neoformans. Therefore, this study has developed and validated a model for the experimental evolution of C. neoformans during extreme temperature events to determine the effects on virulence-associated traits and thermotolerance. Using a heat shock gradient, 42 degrees centigrade w as established as the highest temperature C. neoformans survived for the purpose of experimental evolution over 7 generations. Phenotypic changes associated with cryptococcal virulence were measured, and an in vitro murine macrophage model of infection was used. We successfully isolated populations with increased thermotolerance over generations, however generational viability remained low after each treatment suggesting that there was no fixation of genetic changes. Within thermotolerant populations, there was phenotypic variation in colony morphotype, with some populations showing morphotype changes associated with enhanced virulence. The model demonstrates a potential method to experimentally evolve C. neoformans under controlled conditions, providing a framework for long-term pathogen evolution studies.

pres102.title: Determining Sex Differences in Spore-Mediated Cryptococcosis Using a Mouse Model
pres102.abstract: Human cryptococcosis exhibits a well-established sex bias, with males experiencing higher disease incidence and poorer clinical outcomes than females. The underlying bases for these differences (i.e., biological, cultural, social, etc.) are poorly understood. Although extensive research has been conducted on the virulence mechanisms of Cryptococcus, the effects of sex and sex hormone differences on infection outcomes remain largely unexplored. To evaluate a potential role for biological sex in cryptococcal disease, we infected both male and female mice (as determined by anogenital distance at weaning) with Cryptococcus and evaluated disease progression and outcomes. Male and female C57/BL6 and BALB/c mice were infected intranasally with either spores or yeast (B3501 and B3502 strains) and assessed for days of survival after infection and organ fungal burdens at end point. In both mouse backgrounds, male mice succumbed to disease faster than female mice (C57/BL6, p = 0.0359 and BALB/c, p < 0.0001). Fungal burdens in the brain, lung, kidney, and spleen did not differ between sexes at end point. From these data, we hypothesize that dissemination from the lung occurs earlier in males, leading to lethal fungal organ burdens earlier than in females. Future experiments will 1) determine timelines of lung dissemination in males vs. females, 2) identify any differences in lung immune cell interactions with spores between males and females, and 3) determine the roles of sex hormones in controlling time-to-disease. This study highlights a potential need for sex-specific therapeutic considerations when treating cryptococcosis.

pres103.title: Paradoxical microglial inactivation during cryptococcal infection of the brain
pres103.abstract: Cryptococcal meningitis (CM) is a fatal fungal infection of the brain and remains a leading cause of HIV-related mortality worldwide. Brain injury in CM has traditionally been attributed to excessive inflammation; however, the cellular mechanisms underlying cryptococcal neuropathogenesis remain poorly defined. Particularly unknown is how microglia, the resident immune cells of the brain, respond to brain invasion by Cryptococcus neoformans (C. neoformans), the causative agent for CM. Here, we investigated microglial-fungal interactions using complementary rodent and human brain-based experimental systems. Human and rodent organotypic brain slices (OBSs) were infected with a GFP/mCherry-expressing H99 strain of C. neoformans, and host -pathogen interactions were examined using immunofluorescent labelling of microglia (IBA1). Microglial activation was measured using nuclear localisation of the transcription factor, nuclear factor for interleukin-6 (NF-IL6): a robust marker for inflammatory signalling. Immune responses in C. neoformans-treated slices were compared to untreated controls and to slices treated with the canonical immunogen- lipopolysaccharide (LPS). Inflammatory responses were further validated using single-nucleus RNA sequencing and multiplex cytokine profiling. Microglia readily internalised C. neoformans; however, fungal uptake was not accompanied by classical microglial activation. C. neoformans -treated slices showed minimal NF-IL6 activation, negligible pro-inflammatory cytokine release, and no induction of key inflammatory gene programs, comparable to untreated controls and markedly attenuated relative to LPS-stimulated slices. Our findings reveal a paradoxical microglial inactivation as a previously unrecognised feature of cryptococcal neuropathogenesis and suggest that immune silencing within the brain may contribute to fungal persistence and disease progression.

pres104.title: Cryptococcal aggregation in the perivascular compartment drives glymphatic disruption during murine cryptococcal meningitis
pres104.abstract: Cryptococcal meningitis (CM) is an invasive fungal infection of the brain associated with severe neurological complications including hydrocephalus and elevated intracranial pressure (ICP). Dilated perivascular spaces (PVS) have long been recognised as a radiological hallmark of CM but mechanisms underlying this pathology are poorly defined. Here, we hypothesise that neuropathology in CM may arise from glymphatic disruption and investigate cryptococcal distribution and its effect on glymphatic flow. Twenty-four C57BL/6 mice were intravenously injected with 5x10^4 Cryptococcus neoformans (FD651), Cryptococcus gattii (R265), or saline. To measure the distribution of fungal cells relative to the vascular network, optically cleared whole brains were imaged using light-sheet microscopy, followed by quantitative distance mapping to determine fungal proximity to blood vessels. Glymphatic influx was evaluated by intracisternal infusion of 10 uL of a fluorescent tracer (Alexa 555-ov albumin, 45 kDa) at 2 uL/min. Tracer penetration was determined by measuring the mean fluorescence intensity and thresholded area fraction across coronal sections collected from bregma +2 to -2. Three-dimensional spatial analysis revealed preferential aggregation of cryptococcal cells within PVS. Fungal aggregation was associated with disruption of glymphatic influx, as evidenced by reduced tracer penetration in infected mice compared to healthy controls. Furthermore, infected mice exhibited increased resistance to tracer infusion, reflected by the prolonged infusion times. Our findings provide the first experimental evidence of glymphatic disruption during CM, potentially arising from occlusion of the PVS by aggregated cryptococcal cells. This mechanism offers a plausible biological framework linking fungal neuroinvasion to the hydrocephalus and elevated ICP that characterises CM.

pres105.title: Time-Dependent Antibody Signatures of Cryptococcal Disease After Kidney Transplant
pres105.abstract: Background and aims: Cryptococcal disease (CD) remains a high-mortality opportunistic infection after kidney transplant (KT), typically presenting during maintenance immunosuppression (IS) when immune reconstitution is heterogeneous. No validated immune markers risk-stratify KT recipients for CD. We evaluated whether total/antigen-specific antibody profiles differ by CD status and time post-KT as a surrogate for cumulative IS exposure. Methods: Plasma from three KT cohorts (n=58) was analysed: no-CD (n=29), pre-CD (n=10; prior to CD diagnosis), and CD (n=19; after diagnosis); stratified early (<1 year) or late (>1 year) post-KT. Total IgM, IgA, IgG1, IgG2, and cytokines quantified by Luminex; antigen-specific IgM/IgG to capsular glucuronoxylomannan (GXM) and laminarin (?-glucan proxy) by ELISA. Results: Median age was 58 years; 75% male. CD occurred 2.2 years post-KT; sampling ~33 days later. Compared with no-CD, CD had hypogammaglobulinemia, ~5-fold lower IgM (median 928μg/ml vs 4,441), ~4-fold lower IgG2 (482 vs 1,940); late CD remained depressed (IgM 775 vs 3,588; IgG2 651 vs 1,711). Within CD, antigen-responses declined from early to late (GXM-IgM 21.6Üí14.4; laminarin-IgM 161Üí51; laminarin-IgG 304Üí163), contrasting with stable/increasing levels in no-CD/pre-CD. Despite low total immunoglobulins, early CD had comparable/higher GXM-IgM and laminarin-IgM than early no-CD (21.6 vs 16.3; 161 vs 49.8) while GXM-IgG was lower (45.9 vs 121). IgM, laminarin/GXM-IgM correlated with IFN?, GM-CSF, and IL-17A in CD. Conclusions: CD after KT is associated with decreased IgM/IgG2 and time-dependent antigen-specific antibody trajectories during the expected period of CD4 reconstitution, suggesting distinct immune states driving disease, supporting marker-guided IS management. Small strata, heterogeneous sampling limit inference.

pres106.title: In vivo assessment of host-pathogen interaction in the crossing the blood-brain barrier in murine model of experimental cerebral cryptococcosis – correlating cellular with pathophysiological events
pres106.abstract: Cryptococcus neoformans (CN) causes severe infections that can spread from the lungs to the central nervous system (CNS). The mechanisms by which CN crosses the blood–brain barrier (paracytosis, transcytosis, or Trojan Horse mechanism) remain unclear. Clarifying early CNS entry and disease progression, a multimodal imaging approach combining Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) and Two-Photon Intravital Microscopy (2P-IVM) was used to monitor CN dissemination longitudinally. Thirty Balb/C mice with an implanted cranial window were infected intravenously with 50.000 CN. After infection, mice were imaged for 8h, 24h, 48h using 2P-IVM. Lesions were confirmed by MRI on 4/7days. Influence on lesion formation was examined in depth (silicone oil vs. PBS, presence vs. absence of meninges, and impact of window placement). In mice infected with CNKN99αE2-Crimson, cryptococci were observed after 1h with cluster after 5h with capillary bed occlusion. Mice infected with CNH99-EGFP showed similar results. Titan cells were confirmed at 24h and 48h with daughter cell production. After 7 days, lesions were observed in MRI in the cortex beneath the cranial window, allowing for imaging these lesions at cellular level with 2P-IVM, where indications of capillary vessel ruptures were observed. No differences were observed in lesion formation based on window placement (RW/LW) or compound used (PBS/silicone oil). Removing meninges didn’t affect autofluorescence. We developed a multimodal 2P-IVM/MRI platform to track cerebral cryptococcosis across disease stages. Longitudinal cellular imaging revealed spread from onset to lesion formation, with indications of vessel rupture and transcytosis but no Trojan horse mechanism, highlighting the need for further studies.

pres107.title: An Uncharacterized Kinase Regulates Macrophage Adaptation of Cryptococcus neoformans
pres107.abstract: The ability to survive and replicate in host cells is a critical virulence trait of Cryptococcus neoformans which can impact the trajectory of an infection. However, the genes required for fungal survival in host cells like macrophages and the mechanisms through which this trait evolved are largely unknown. To gain insight into how C. neoformans adapts to grow inside of macrophages, we established a serial passaging platform and identified several fungal populations with robust increases in macrophage intracellular growth. Here, we focus on characterizing one isolate from these experiments which carries a single missense mutation in the serine/threonine kinase, SKS1, that is necessary and sufficient for macrophage adaptation in this strain. Through competition experiments, we show that strains carrying SKS1 mutations have fitness advantages in the intracellular macrophage environment but not extracellularly. Introduction of the SKS1 mutation into closely related C. neoforma ns strains results in similarly increased survival in macrophages, suggesting a conserved role of this mutation across genetic backgrounds. Additional phenotypic characterization of SKS1 mutants show that this gene regulates additional virulence-related traits important for macrophage survival, including the ability to form titan cells and produce melanin. Our ongoing work focuses on identifying targets of Sks1 phosphorylation, characterizing host responses to infection by Sks1 mutants, and deciphering the role of Sks1 in regulating growth in macrophages from alternative host species. Collectively, this work is revealing the genetic basis of host adaptation and will provide critical insight into how new virulence traits can emerge in this important fungal pathogen.

pres108.title: Immune Mechanism of Programmed Cell Death of Macrophage in Prognosis of Non-HIV Cryptococcal Meningitis
pres108.abstract: Background and aims: Excessive host immune responses may contribute to poor prognosis in non-HIV cryptococcal meningitis (CM). Programmed cell death (PCD), including pyroptosis, apoptosis, and necroptosis, plays a pivotal role in host-pathogen interactions; however, its involvement in non-HIV CM is poorly understood. This study aimed to characterize PCD profiles in non-HIV CM and to explore the macrophage PCD-related immune mechanisms associated with disease prognosis. Methods: PCD-related gene expressions were assessed in monocyte-derived macrophages (MDMs) and correlated with baseline cerebrospinal fluid cytokines, fungal burden and disease severity. An in vitro RAW264.7 macrophage model infected with opsonized Cryptococcus neoformans H99 was established to investigate fungal load-dependent cytotoxicity and to explore the phenotype and the potential signaling pathways of PCD using RNA-sequencing, quantitative PCR, western blotting and targeted pathway inhibition. Results: Patients with higher fungal loads showed a tendency toward elevated expression of pyroptosis and necroptosis markers in MDMs. Notably, the necroptosis marker RIPK3 was significantly elevated in severe cases. In vitro, opsonized Cryptococcus was phagocytized by RAW264.7 cells, leading to activation of pyroptosis and necroptosis, concomitant suppression of apoptotic executioners, and upregulation of thioredoxin-interacting protein (TXNIP) in a time or dose-dependent manner at both transcription and translation levels. RNA-sequencing also revealed marked transcriptional reprogramming characterized by early activation of inflammasome-related genes (NLRP3 and IL-1?). Pharmacological inhibition experiments indicated that macrophage pyroptosis was supposed to be mediated through a TLR4-TXNIP-NLRP3 inflammasome axis. Conclusions: High fungal burden in CM triggers inflammatory PCD dominated by TXNIP-mediated, NLRP3-dependent pyroptosis.

pres109.title: Phenotypic landscape of Cryptococcus neoformans drives mechanistic interrogation of virulence factors
pres109.abstract: Cryptococcus neoformans is a pathogenic basidiomycete yeast that diverged from well-studied fungi in the phylum Ascomycota at least 450 million years ago. Defining functions for basidiomycete-specific genes is essential for deciphering mechanisms of cryptococcal pathogenesis, yet doing so at the genomic scale remains challenging. We performed massively parallel fitness screens of 4328 single-gene C. neoformans deletion strains in 141 unique in vitro growth environments and in a mouse infection model. In vivo screens revealed 574 fungal genes required for fitness during murine pulmonary infection. Hierarchical clustering of mutant fitness profiles produced a phenotypic atlas of this organism, reconstructing core molecular pathways and assigning validated cellular roles to unstudied genes. Integrative analysis of these datasets revealed a novel virulence gene, RGH1 (Required for Growth in Host-like conditions), uniquely required for fitness during tissue culture and in vivo i nfection. Concordantly, RGH1 transcription is massively induced upon shifting to host-like growth conditions. We pursued the function of this factor though a genome-scale CRISPR/Cas9 suppressor screen and affinity purification-mass spectrometry experiments, uncovering a putative role in cell wall alpha-glucan biogenesis. Together, our results suggest a model in which Rgh1 is actively deployed upon host entry to drive adaptive cell wall remodeling that is critical for tolerating infection-relevant stressors. This work highlights the power of high-throughput genetics in linking unbiased discovery with mechanistic studies, providing an experimental framework for interrogating uncharacterized fungal virulence determinants.

pres110.title: Elucidating Dissemination of the Fungal Pathogen Cryptococcus neoformans Through Genetic Barcoding
pres110.abstract: Cryptococcus neoformans causes 194,000 cases of meningoencephalitis annually with very high mortality (~80%). Invasive cryptococcosis begins with inhalation of infectious organisms followed by dissemination throughout the host, especially to the central nervous system. The mechanisms underlying dissemination are essential for progression of disease yet remain poorly described. In a murine model of inhaled cryptococcosis I demonstrated that C. neoformans disseminates reliably to organs including the lungs, brain, liver, spleen, kidney, and blood. I then infected mice with a pool of five C. neoformans strains with varying drug resistance markers inserted at a genomic Äòsafe haven’ locus. The proportions of each strain remained stable in the lungs but became highly variable after dissemination to the brains of these animals, implying a bottleneck in fungal transit. To further interrogate the kinetics of dissemination I created a library of 125 barcoded, otherwise isogenic s trains in a wild-type background. This larger library eliminated the heterogeneity introduced by drug resistance markers and improved the resolution of our study. The library was pooled for intranasal infection followed by Sanger sequencing to determine barcode abundance in each organ at multiple timepoints during infection. A significant bottleneck was again revealed between the lung and brain in late-stage infection, and was found to vary by individual animal. In ongoing work I am performing an intravenous infection with the library to compare bottlenecks created by this alternative infectious route. Delineating cryptococcal dissemination will enable understanding of the clinical course and may create new avenues for therapeutic approaches.

pres111.title: Neuroimmunometabolic dysfunction in cryptococcal meningitis
pres111.abstract: Cryptococcal meningitis (CM) is associated with severe neurological injury and a high mortality despite antifungal therapy. A hallmark of cryptococcal infection is the formation of cryptococcomas - densely packed fungal lesions embedded within a viscous, polysaccharide-rich matrix that markedly restricts diffusion of metabolites and immune mediators within the brain. The contribution of these metabolically restricted microniches to neuropathology and brain injury have not been previously described. We hypothesize that neurological injury in CM may arise from disruption of metabolic homeostasis leading to bioenergetic failure. We performed bulk metabolomic analysis in mouse brains, six-days post infection with Cryptococcus neoformans. We then used single-nucleus RNA sequencing of organotypic hippocampal brain slices infected with C. neoformans to characterise metabolic transcriptional changes across host neural cell types. Our data shows significant cell-type-specific metabolic reprogramming during cryptococcal infection. Neuronal and glial populations showed a coordinated upregulation of glycolytic pathways and hypoxia responsive genes, accompanied by suppression of mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation, consistent with functional oxygen limitation and restricted glucose availability. Gene Ontology analysis supported these findings, demonstrating enrichment of hypoxic and glycolytic biological pathways. These findings identify metabolic dysregulation as a central driver of cryptococcal brain injury, highlighting bioenergetic failure as a previously underappreciated therapeutic target in cryptococcal me ningitis beyond antifungal clearance.

pres112.title: From the environment to the host, Cryptococcus neoformans and the building of a better model
pres112.abstract: Hypothesis: Cryptococcus neoformans clinical and environmental isolates can differ in their ability to cause disease, but the evolutionary factors underlying these differences remain poorly understood, in part due to the complexity of both the pathogen and the host. Methods: To address this knowledge gap, we evaluated infections caused by clinical and environmental isolates using an oropharyngeal cryptococcosis mouse model and multiple unique isolate collections to enable more detailed analyses of disease production. Results: Consistent with prior studies, we observed that consistently environmental isolates generally fail to cause severe disease comparable to that caused by aggressive clinical isolates. Unlike the environmental isolates, clinical isolates display a more varied virulence potential. Thus, we have assembled a library of 30 nonaggressive and 30 aggressive isolates comprised of both environmental and clinical strains of opposite mating types, different clades, and sources. This collection supports whole-genome sequence analysis (WGS), genome-wide association studies (GWAS), and phenotypic characterizations to identify factors that influence disease outcomes in a robust mouse model. In addition, using a new mouse model for the interrogation of host model responses, we compared granuloma formation between nonaggressive and aggressive yeast isolates and found that aggressive isolates induced robust host granuloma formation within 21 days of infection, whereas nonaggressive isolates did not. Conclusion: This work highlights the importance of the disease potential of individual yeast isolates for understanding the evolution of virulence through genetic, genomic, and host-pathogen studies and provides additional evidence to the observation that environmental isolates tend to be less aggressive.

pres113.title: The novel carbohydrate modifying enzyme Cel1 is required for Cryptococcus neoformans cell wall remodeling in response to host environmental stress.
pres113.abstract: The cell wall provides support and integrity to the fungal cell. As the interface with the external environment, it is also an important structure for stress adaptation. Accordingly, rapid changes within the carbohydrate composition and architecture occur in response to environmental stresses. Lytic polysaccharide monooxygenases (LPMOs) are copper-enzymes, secreted by microbes to assist in the first steps of remodeling and degradation of complex carbohydrates. While recent studies have established an important role for LPMOs in plant pathogenic microbes, studies about the role of LPMOs in human pathogenic microbes are scarce and limited to bacteria. The human fungal pathogen Cryptococcus neoformans (Cn) encodes for 4 potential LPMOs. Initial phenotypic analysis of loss-of-function mutant strains identified the CEL1 gene to be required for growth at mammalian host temperature and pH. In line with this finding, the cel1àÜ mutant strain is avirulent in a murine model of infection. In contrast to other known LPMOs which are secreted into the extracellular environment and thereby targeting exogenous carbohydrates, CnCel1 is retained within the cryptococcal cell wall, suggesting a role in promoting intrinsic fungal cell wall remodeling. To further elucidate CnCel1 function we performed a combination of solid-state NMR, carbohydrate and microscopic analysis. These analyses suggest a novel role for the Cel1 enzyme in modulating cell wall chitosan distribution and deposition, a key cell wall carbohydrate required for stress adaptation during infection. Taken together, our recent work has demonstrated a novel role for an LPMO-family enzyme for fungal cell wall remodeling and stress adaptation during pathogenesis.

pres114.title: Cryptococcus modulates host immune tolerance through STING-dependent inhibition of PFKFB3
pres114.abstract: Background and aims: Immune tolerance is a critical determinant in the pathogenesis of non-HIV-associated cryptococcal meningitis (CM). While stimulator of interferon genes (STING) is a known negative regulator of antifungal immunity, its mechanistic role in mediating immune tolerance remains unclear. This study aims to elucidate how STING interacts with glycolysis-related pathways to mediate immunometabolic suppression in non-HIV CM. Methods: Peripheral blood monocytes were collected from non-HIV CM patients, pulmonary cryptococcosis (PC) patients, and healthy controls (HC). Multi-platform analyses were conducted, including cytokine profiling, STING activation assessment, and glycolytic flux quantification. In vitro immune-tolerant models and murine cryptococcal infection models were established, utilising STING genetic knockout and glycolytic rescue experiments. Molecular docking and Co-IP were employed to validate interactions between STING and 6-phosphofructo-2-kinase/fructose-2,6-biphosphatase 3 (PFKFB3). Results: CM monocytes displayed significantly impaired TNF-α/IL-6 secretion and reduced glycolytic intermediates, alongside elevated STING expression, relative to PC and HC cohorts. Notably, persistent glucuronoxylomannan (GXM) exposure recapitulated this tolerogenic phenotype in vitro. Pharmacological antagonism or genetic ablation of STING effectively rescued cytokine production. In vivo, STING-/- mice demonstrated significantly improved survival and reduced fungal burden. Mechanistically, molecular docking and Co-IP verified a direct high-affinity interaction between STING and PFKFB3. Consequently, STING deficiency reversed GXM-mediated PFKFB3 suppression. Conclusions: Cryptococcal infection upregulates STING, which directly binds and inhibits PFKFB3, suppressing glycolysis and inducing immune tolerance. Targeting the STING-PFKFB3 axis restores immunometabolic function and enhances antifungal responses. These findings establish STING as a master regulator of immunometabolic reprogramming and provide a novel therapeutic strategy.

pres115.title: Assessment of cerebral cryptococcosis caused by different molecular sub-types in rodent models using correlations between in vivo imaging and histology
pres115.abstract: Molecular subtypes within the Cryptococcus species complexes manifest differently within the CNS, although the exact correlations between clinical and cellular manifestation are poorly understood. It was our aim to further characterize them in vivo5,6 as to deepen our understanding of the neuropathogenic aspects of cryptococcosis. Female BALB/c mice were intravenously infected (50.000 cells/100 ¬µL; n = 4) with an isolate representing one of the eight major molecular subtypes of C. neoformans (CN) (VN I-IV) and C. gattii (CG) (VG I-IV) as described by Meyer et al (Electrophoresis. 1999 Jun;20(8):1790–9). Mice were scanned weekly until the humane endpoints using a 9.4 T MRI scanner (Biospec 94/20, Bruker). Structural, diffusion weighted (ADC) and T2 relaxometry images were acquired and semi-quantified for assessment of lesion and meningitis formation, based on Vanherp et al (2020). Post-mortem histopathological analysis included a wide range of immunohistochemical markers to assess host-pathogen interactions. The CN/CG complex consists out of differently behaving subtypes deviating in manifestation/morbidity. We identified the capsular size to be the main virulence factor in the dissemination towards the CNS, as concluded from ADC and T2 relaxometry. Post-mortem histopathology confirmed these results, along with indications of CN/CG provoking an autoimmune-like host-reaction, linked to an immunosuppressed CNS phenotype. Lastly, we identified the choroid plexus as a main entrance point for cerebral dissemination. By implementing longitudinal, multiparametric MR acquisitions we gained new insights in the behavior and formation of cryptococcoma. Combined with histopathology, the in vivo imaging-based characterization of cryptococcosis allows for future investigation for an improved prognosis/treatment.

pres116.title: Longitudinal in vivo bioluminescence imaging reveals a protective role for MARCO deficiency in cryptococcal clearance and fluconazole efficacy
pres116.abstract: Background: Cryptococcus neoformans is an opportunistic fungal pathogen that can disseminate from the lungs to the CNS, causing life-threatening meningoencephalitis. The role of cryptococcal vomocytosis from pulmonary macrophages in dissemination remains unclear. Macrophage Receptor with Collagenous structure (MARCO) was recently identified as a potent negative regulator of cryptococcal vomocytosis in vitro, with its absence strongly enhancing expulsion rates. Here, we investigated the in vivo role of MARCO in cryptococcal infection and fluconazole responsiveness. Methods: Wild-type and MARCO-KO C57BL/6 mice were orotracheally infected with a low (50 cells) or higher (5000 cells) inoculum of luciferase-producing KN99α C. neoformans. Fluconazole 80 mg/kg/day was started 1.5 days post-infection and switched to 40 mg/kg/day after 8 days. To non-invasively track real-time fungal burden over 4.5 weeks, we developed a novel bioluminescence imaging (BLI) method using 500 mg/kg D- luciferin. Results: MARCO deficiency enhanced fluconazole’s antifungal efficacy, significantly reducing brain dissemination and fungal burden compared to wild type controls. MARCO-KO mice also cleared low inoculum pulmonary infection more effectively, delaying or preventing CNS spread, but showed no protection at higher fungal inocula unless combined with fluconazole. These effects are potentially driven by increased extracellular fungal exposure via enhanced vomocytosis in MARCO-KO mice, improving antifungal access. Conclusion: Our findings identify MARCO as a host factor influencing early cryptococcal clearance and antifungal potentiation and highlight longitudinal BLI as a powerful tool for studying infection and treatment dynamics in vivo. Combining host-directed MARCO targeting with antifungal therapy may offer a novel strategy to prevent cryptococcal dissemination, warranting further investigation.

pres117.title: Long-term evaluation of cryptococcal dissemination from pulmonary infection to late-stage neurological complications in an optimized rat model with orotracheal inoculation
pres117.abstract: Currently, mouse models that use intravenous inoculation is the gold standard for studying cryptococcal dissemination due of its high throughput and reproducible workflow. To more closely mimic the natural route of infection, we aimed to optimize the implementation of a rat model after intratracheal (IT) infection. Rather than an artificial bloodborne dissemination, this IT model starts from a primary pulmonary infection, as described in clinical cases. Furthermore, this rat model provides many advantages related to immunological aspects and follow up by in vivo imaging. Female Fisher 334 rats were orotracheally infected (1.000.000 cells/200 ¬µL; n = 6) with either C. gattii (R265) or C. neoformans (KN99a) as previously described by Meyer et al. Pulmonary infection was followed-up weekly by micro-CT (SkyScan 1278). To assess cerebral lesion formation and development of meningitis, anatomical (T2w) and diffusion weighted MR imaging (ADC) was used (9.4T MRI scanner, Biospec 94/20, Bruker). Weekly CT acquisitions revealed a clear increase in pulmonary fungal burden from week 5 p.i. onwards, and evidence of neurological involvement in the form of cerebral lesions was found on MRI acquisitions from week 7 p.i. These results indicate that the OT rat model is ideal to monitor cryptococcal dissemination and disease progression. Rats kept stable until week 10 p.i. despite incremental increase in fungal dissemination, proving that infection can be sustained without immunosuppression, contrary to previous literature reports. This model may allow us to monitor disease development and dissemination longitudinally without the restrictions of the current standard mouse model.

pres118.title: Investigating novel lectibodies against the capsule of human fungal pathogen Cryptococcus neoformans
pres118.abstract: The capsule of Cryptococcus neoformans remains the most prominent virulence factor responsible for evasion of the host immune system. Unfortunately, no preventive or curative treatments have been successful in targeting the capsule. Novel antibody-lectin hybrid molecules, termed lectibodies, have been shown to mediate immune clearance of cancer cells and virus-infected cells thanks to the recognition of aberrant glycosylation patterns presented by the cells. We therefore hypothesize that lectibodies targeting the fungal capsule could represent a new therapeutic avenue to alleviate patient suffering from cryptococcal infections. We conducted a screen of 66 purified human recombinant lectibodies against encapsulated C. neoformans cells and identified four candidate lectibodies binding to the outer capsule in a calcium dependent manner. Specificity testing of the four hits revealed a preferential recognition of C. neoformans serotype A. However, no impact on cryptococcal growt h or capsule elaboration was observed in culture. The ability to facilitate phagocytosis by host bone marrow derived macrophages was investigated in a tissue culture system and we found that phagocytosis of fungal cells was significantly increased upon opsonization with the lectibodies. These results were consistently observed with human THP-1 monocyte derived macrophages, as well as BV-2 murine microglial cells. These results suggest that novel lectibodies may play a critical role in the host recognition of C. neoformans during infection, paving the way for exploiting these recognitions to develop innovative immunotherapy options.

pres119.title: Hir1, a subunit of the HIR complex, is required for iron regulation, cell wall integrity and virulence in Cryptococcus neoformans
pres119.abstract: Iron is important for proliferation of C. neoformans, and acts both as a nutrient and as a signal to regulate the expression of the main virulence factors. We recently discovered a previously unidentified role of a transcription corepressor, Hir1, in iron acquisition and regulation, and in the virulence in C. neoformans. A hir1 mutant was originally identified by screening an Agrobacterium tumefaciens - mediated insertion library. In Saccharomyces cerevisiae and other fungi, Hir1 is a subunit of the HIR complex that is involved in regulation of histone gene transcription. Deletion of HIR1 in C. neoformans results in reduced capsule formation in low iron capsule-inducing media, and increased sensitivity to elevated temperatures. Furthermore, the hir1 deletion mutants exhibited reduced growth in iron-limiting media, poor growth on heme as the sole iron source, and increased sensitivity to the iron - chelating agent curcumin. Hir1 is localized in the nuclei of cells that grow in low or high iron conditions. Interestingly, preliminary results from a pull-down assay revealed that Hir1 is one of the possible interacting partners of the iron master regulator, Cir1, indicating that Hir1 may regulate iron acquisition by interaction with Cir1. Deletion of HIR1 also causes increased sensitivity to the azole drug, fluconazole, and compromised cell wall integrity. Lastly, a pilot virulence assay in a mouse inhalation model demonstrated that Hir1 is required for full virulence. In summary, our preliminary investigation identified a new role for Hir1 in iron acquisition, cell wall integrity and virulence in C. neoformans.

pres120.title: Unbiased protein correlation profile analyses unravel putative virulence-associated protein complexes in Cryptococcus neoformans
pres120.abstract: Protein-protein interactions (PPIs) are crucial for understanding critical biological processes in pathogenic species, such as Cryptococcus neoformans. Current methods used to characterize such interactions either restrict analysis to a single protein of interest or may overestimate interactions due to complex global proteomics data. Therefore, co-fractionation methods offer an alternative for globally assessing PPIs in a simplified environment, providing a comprehensive understanding of the mechanisms driving cryptococcosis. As a result, I am designing a novel platform for investigating PPIs in C. neoformans that can help identify crucial players in virulence. To achieve this goal, I have developed a co-fractionation method based on size exclusion chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry to enhance the detection of true interactors in secreted and cellular protein samples (i.e., secretome and proteome, respectively). PPI predictions were then inferred via the STRING n etwork database to highlight known and novel physical protein complexes within functional clusters. As a result, 216 and 1699 unique proteins were identified, spread across 24 secretome and proteome fractions, respectively. Notably, predicted secretome networks include vesicle-associated proteins that bind stress-response proteins, indicating a role in extracellular defense. Furthermore, CNAG_00287 was inferred to play a role in thermal stress, as it was found to bind heat-shock proteins (CNAG_06208 and CNAG_03944) in both the secretome and proteome networks. Further in silico and in vitro validations will be carried out to characterize this novel putative heat-shock protein fully. Overall, this project delivers a new method capable of comprehensively detecting interacting proteins essential for C. neoformans pathogenicity.

pres121.title: Invasive cryptococcosis with Klebsiella pneumoniae co-infection associated with refractory disease and high mortality: A case series
pres121.abstract: Cryptococcus neoformans is the leading cause of fungal meningitis worldwide. Standard induction therapy combines amphotericin B with a second antifungal agent; however, treatment failure occurs due to drug resistance, inadequate dosing, monotherapy, or host immune dysfunction, leading to relapsing or refractory disease with high mortality. We report four cases of invasive cryptococcosis with concurrent Klebsiella pneumoniae bloodstream infection. All patients developed refractory disseminated cryptococcal disease despite initial combination antifungal therapy. Two of four patients (50%) died despite aggressive antifungal and antibiotic treatment. C. neoformans and K. pneumoniae isolates were recovered from three cases. We performed comprehensive antifungal and antimicrobial susceptibility testing and whole genome sequencing to characterize resistance patterns and genetic signatures. Clinical presentations, phenotypic resistance profiles, and genomic analysis revealed antifungal resistance signatures in all characterized strains. The concurrent bacterial-fungal co-infection was associated with poor outcomes despite appropriate combination therapy. This case series highlights the clinical challenge of cryptococcal-Klebsiella co-infection and suggests that concurrent bloodstream infections may contribute to antifungal treatment failure and mortality. Further investigation is needed to determine whether bacterial co-infection influences cryptococcal virulence or treatment response.

pres122.title: Genetic and pathogenic diversity of Cryptocococcus gattii
pres122.abstract: Cryptococcosis is a severe fungal disease caused by Cryptococcus neoformans and its sister species C. gattii. Advances in whole-genome sequencing and population genomics have revealed six genetically distinct C. gattii lineages (VGI–VGVI). C. gattii displays massive phenotypic diversity. We performed genomic analysis on 46 C. gattii strains, including 25 clinical isolates, 15 environmental isolates, 3 veterinary isolates and 3 of unknown origin gathered from 13 countries across six continents. Phylogenetic analysis of the 46 isolates revealed a clear separation into the 6 distinct lineages. We evaluated virulence of these C. gattii isolates using the insect model Galleria mellonella. Only 6 out of the 46 isolates caused death in all larvae after 10 days post infection, whereas 5 caused less than 50% killing after 10 days post infection. Among the six lineages, VGI and VGIII were the least virulent. We also observed substantial intra-lineage variations, which likely reflec ts intra-lineage genetic diversity. Surprisingly, we observed that in the insect model, VGV isolates showed on average ~80% mortality, which may be due to the lack of adaptive immunity. Survival of larvae did not differ significantly between infections with environmental and clinical isolates (12.5% and 15%, respectively). Given that C.gattii is commonly inhaled by the host from the environmental sources, it is unsurprising that no notable differences were found between environmental and clinical isolates.These findings underscore the substantial genetic and phenotypic diversity within the C. gattii complex and highlight the value of integrating genomic and infection-model data to elucidate the evolutionary and pathogenic mechanisms.

pres123.title: Anticryptococcal Mechanisms of Action by Dendritic Cell Lysosome Proteins
pres123.abstract: Cryptococcus neoformans is an opportunistic fungal pathogen that enters the lungs and leads to meningitis in immunocompromised individuals. The pathogen initially encounters innate immune cells, such as dendritic cells (DCs) in the lungs, and these cells can kill C. neoformans. Previous studies in our lab showed that proteins from the DC lysosome have potent antifungal activity against C. neoformans, including NOSTRIN, matrix metalloproteinase 25 (MMP25), and myeloperoxidase (MPO). We hypothesized that these compounds kill C. neoformans by affecting signaling pathways, leading to fungal cell wall and membrane damage. To test this, we used microscopy following treatment to visually identify structural changes following treatment, and we used cryptococcal phosphatase and kinase mutant libraries to identify mutants and signaling pathways involved in resistance or susceptibility to treatment. Microscopy of Cryptococcus treated with NOSTRIN, MMP25, and MPO showed release of intr acellular contents and misshapen fungal cells. This phenotype was rescued by the addition of sorbitol for osmotic stability, indicating osmotic lysis of Cryptococcus due to lysosomal proteins. Mutant library analyses following NOSTRIN and MPO treatment showed increased resistance in mutants with genes involved in inositol phosphate metabolism, while MMP25 treatment showed increased resistance in mutants with genes involved in purine and pyrimidine metabolism and genes involved in secondary metabolite biosynthesis. These studies indicate that there are some shared mechanisms and some unique mechanisms used by DC lysosome proteins for antifungal activity. Further studies will determine the importance of the identified signaling pathways in antifungal activity of DC lysosome proteins.

pres124.title: Characterization of extracellular vesicles diversity in Cryptococcus neoformans: A new avenue for identifying disease markers and understanding host interaction.
pres124.abstract: Background and aims Extracellular vesicles (EVs) are fundamental mediators of intercellular communication and pathogenic processes across diverse organisms, including fungi such as Cryptococcus species, an opportunistic human pathogen. Although current structural and biogenetic models of fungal EVs have advanced the field, they often overlook the intrinsic heterogeneity of EV populations, which may underpin distinct biological functions. While EV subpopulations have been extensively characterized in mammalian systems, their diversity and immunomodulatory potential in fungi remain poorly understood. This study aimed to characterize EV heterogeneity in Cryptococcus neoformans and explore the functional relevance of distinct EV subpopulations. Methods Cryptococcus-derived EVs were isolated using an integrative strategy combining size-, density-, and weight-based separation techniques, enabling the resolution of discrete EV subpopulations. Proteomic profiling was performed to compare protein cargo across EV fractions and identify subpopulation-specific signatures. Results We identified multiple EV subpopulations with distinct biophysical properties and protein compositions. Notably, a previously uncharacterized small EV population was discovered, exhibiting a unique proteomic profile. Comparative proteomic analyses revealed that each EV subpopulation harbors distinct protein cargos, including candidates associated with immune modulation and fungal virulence. Conclusions This work reveals previously unrecognized layers of EV diversity in Cryptococcus neoformans. Ongoing functional studies using in vitro host–pathogen interaction assays are investigating how individual EV subpopulations differentially influence host immune responses, including cytokine production and immune cell activation. Together, these findings lay the groundwork for elucidating EV-mediated mechanisms of host interaction and support the potential development of EV-based biomarkers and therapeutic strategies targeting fungal pathogenic processes.

pres125.title: Using human airway and alveolar organoid-derived monolayers to quantify parameters of early cryptococcal infections
pres125.abstract: Background and aims Cryptococci are environmental fungi that cause localized or disseminated infections typically after uptake via the respiratory tract. Studies on pathogenicity are based on animal models, cancer cell lines or immune cells. However, to understand pathomechanisms novel models are required. Methods We cocultured cryptococcal reference strains (C. gattii VGII/R265 and C. neoformans VNI/H99) with organoid-derived monolayers (ODMs) originating from adult stem cells of proximal (= Airway) and distal (= Alveoli) human respiratory tissue. Barrier function of ODMs was probed by measuring the transepithelial electrical resistance. Internalization of fungi into respiratory cells was visualized by immunofluorescence stainings. Fungal viability was assessed by cell counting and cultivation. Ink stainings were used to quantify fungal cell morphology. Results While barrier function of airway ODMs was maintained, cryptococci induced a barrier breakdown in alveolar ODMs. Immunofluorescence stainings revealed that cryptococcal adhesion and internalization events to and into in airway ODMs were rare, while alveolar ODMs showed an increased susceptibility towards cryptococcal internalization. Both cryptococcal strains were able to proliferate on ODMs. Analysis of fungal morphology revealed strain specific fungal adaptation with giant cells in C. gattii and pseudohyphal growth for C. neoformans while cocultured with alveolar, but not with airway ODMs. Conclusions Studying interactions of cryptococci with primary human cells of the respiratory tissue is feasible with Cryptococcus suggesting different fungal-host interactions of C. gattii and C. neoformans and specific tissue reaction in proximal versus distal tissues. Future studies will be used to characterize immunologic response and analysis of fungal subpopulations.

pres126.title: Investigating interactions between macrophage ridges and Cryptococcus neoformans
pres126.abstract: Cryptococcus neoformans (Cn) is an opportunistic pathogen causative of serious infection of the central nervous system and the primary causative agent of cryptococcal meningitis (CM), a lethal infection predominantly affecting people living with HIV. We hypothesise that increasing phagocytosis of Cn by macrophages is a potential adjunctive therapy. We and others have extensively characterised the parasitism of macrophages by Cn, including in-macrophage growth. However, phagocytosis of Cn by macrophages is essential for surviving infection in both protective and susceptible animal models of cryptococcosis. This in vitro/vivo disparity was explained by us directly measuring macrophage parasitism in vivo in zebrafish demonstrating reduced overall fungal burden despite in-macrophage growth, even in a susceptible model. In agreement with this, human susceptibility to CM results from loss of in macrophages that can phagocytose cryptococci. In CM, the levels of macrophage-targeting in phagocytosis are a positive predictor of survival (independently of anti-fungal treatment). Thus, experimental and clinical data support our hypothesis that phagocytosis is key to surviving life-threatening infection and may be a therapeutic target for CM. Therefore, we have sought new ways to increase the phagocytosis of Cn and have discovered sub-micron actin-based ridges on the macrophage surface which are used to bind and phagocytose cryptococci. However, on exposure to Cn, macrophage ridge formation is attenuated, we aim to elucidate the mechanism by which Cn modulates this interaction.

pres127.title: Determination of hypothetical protein’s role in Cryptococcal virulence
pres127.abstract: Cryptococcus neoformans is an opportunistic fungal pathogen causing severe disease in immune compromised hosts. The increasing rates of disease prevalence and rising threat of antifungal resistance highlights the need to identify determinants of fungal infection and factors modulating host-pathogen interactions. Macrophages as the first line of defense against C. neoformans were used to uncover proteins important for their critical interaction. Herein, mass spectrometry-based proteomic profiling of co-cultured C. neoformans and bone marrow-derived macrophages identified multiple infection-associated candidates, including the uncharacterized protein, CNAG_03492. Based on reduced capsule size and thermotolerance, CNAG_03492Δ is hypothesized to influence cryptococcal virulence. To define this role, CNAG_03492Δ is being evaluated for changes in macrophage uptake and intracellular persistence using bone marrow-derived macrophages. In addition, in vivo studies using CNAG_03492Ôø? Ôø? and complemented strains in BALB/c mice will be used to assess how CNAG_03492Δ affects fungal dissemination, organ-specific burden, and host survival. These findings will be complemented with in silico prediction modeling of target structure and interactions, as well as functional assays exploring global proteome disruption upon deletion of CNAG_03492. Together, these findings will explore the positioning of CNAG_03492 as a target for therapeutic development against cryptococcal disease.

pres128.title: Towards spatial and temporal proteome mapping of fungal infection dynamics to reveal novel druggable targets
pres128.abstract: The incidence and impact of fungal diseases continue to rise worldwide, accompanied by increasing severity, mortality, and therapeutic failure.
