Successful fungal invasion relies on highly regulated virulence mechanisms, while the host mounts dynamic and tissue-specific immune responses to contain infection. Dissecting this complex host–pathogen interplay is essential for advancing fungal disease management and improving global health outcomes. In this study, we apply state-of-the-art mass spectrometry–based proteomics to interrogate proteome remodeling during active infection with Cryptococcus neoformans, achieving unprecedented depth with spatiotemporal resolution. By prioritizing three host organs central to disease initiation, dissemination, and immune response: the lungs, brain, and spleen, we reveal distinct, tissue-specific proteomic trajectories across the course of infection. In the lungs, we observe early and sustained activation of host immune pathways, couple d with the identification and functional characterization of a promising antifungal target and the discovery of a competitive inhibitor to disrupt its activity. In the brain, proteome remodeling closely mirrors disease progression, revealing a previously unrecognized mechanistic role for haptoglobin in modulating fungal cells, alongside evidence of adaptive fungal survival under hypoxic conditions. In the spleen, we uncover dynamic and evolving patterns of immune system activation in response to cryptococcal dissemination. Together, this work provides the most comprehensive, integrated proteomic view of cryptococcal infection to date, capturing disease dynamics across temporal and spatial scales. These findings uncover unrecognized host–pathogen mechanisms and establish a powerful framework for identifying new therapeutic targets and intervention strategies against invasive fungal disease.

pres129.title: Unique recognition, phagocytosis, and intracellular survival of Cryptococcus neoformans in human microglia
pres129.abstract: In immunocompromised individuals, Cryptococcus neoformans escapes from the lungs and disseminates to the central nervous system (CNS), where it causes an estimated 147,000 deaths annually. In the brain, C. neoformans interacts with microglia, the tissue-resident macrophages of the CNS. Previous studies showed that microglia are ineffective at controlling this fungal infection through mechanisms that remain unclear. Thus, I aim to understand the recognition and intracellular survival of C. neoformans by immortalized human microglia. I show that microglia have limited phagocytic activity that is specific to C. neoformans and independent of various cryptococcal factors. I also show that microglia respond to C. neoformans differently than other tissue-resident macrophages, especially relating to recognition, phagocytosis, and phagosome maturation. I have screened 2,800 cryptococcal mutants and identified novel genes, most of which are currently uncharacterized, that may be resp onsible for the yeast’s ability to specifically evade phagocytosis by microglia. Interestingly, we find that factors such as cell size and chitin/chitosan exposure seem to be instrumental in yeast phagocytosis by microglia. Additionally, I show that microglia are ineffective at killing phagocytosed C. neoformans, and that this could be due to the ability of this yeast to disrupt phagosome maturation and induce phagosome membrane damage in these cells, which is partially dependent on fungal opsonization. These findings provide us with fundamental knowledge regarding cryptococcal pathogenesis in the CNS, specifically insight into how C. neoformans is recognized by microglia under different conditions, and how the yeast could be surviving and replicating within the microglial phagosome after engulfment.

pres130.title: Rab20 conveys immune signaling to the cryptococcal-containing phagosome during Cryptococcus infection of macrophages.
pres130.abstract: Cryptococcus neoformans causes ~200,000 deaths yearly, mostly in immunocompromised individuals. It is acquired through inhalation and lung macrophages are the main line of defense. The interactions with these cells will determine the outcome of the infection, influencing if the lung macrophages will serve as safe haven for replication and promote extrapulmonary dissemination, both of which are associated with mortality. The mechanisms C. neoformans use to mediate this intracellular parasitism and how it is influenced by the immune response are not fully understood. Therefore, understanding these interactions and elucidating the properties of the cryptococcal-containing phagosome (CCP) are critical steps needed to improve the management of this disease. We have identified three distinct populations of CCPs based on acidification patterns: those that remain acidic, those that lose acidity, and those that never acidify. Unlike phagosomes containing S. cerevisiae, CCPs exhibit significant phagosomal membrane damage and maturation delay. Moreover, a subpopulation of CCPs display both early endosome (PI3P) and lysosome (LAMP1) markers, suggesting that C. neoformans can alter its phagosome and provides a potential mechanism for intracellular survival that may be driving cryptococcal pathogenesis. Notably, when macrophages are stimulated with interferon-?, acquisition of lysosomal markers occurs more rapidly and more CCPs acidify. Host Rab20 has been shown to be interferon-?-inducible and appears to be critical for this promotion of phago-lysosomal fusion as knockdown of Rab20 delays maturation of phagosomes containing non-pathogenic cargo. Therefore, Rab20 may be linking host immune status and CCP properties during infection, playing a critical role in cryptococcal infection outcome.

pres131.title: Investigating the Role of Phosphatidylinositol 3-phosphate in the Cryptococcal Containing Phagosome
pres131.abstract: Cryptococcus neoformans’ ability to survive within the phagosomes of immune cells is one of the main drivers of infection. These cryptococcal-containing phagosomes (CCPs) within macrophages allow for a permissible environment for the fungi. Although studies have begun to characterize the fungal factors contributing to the CCP, there is a major knowledge gap in the specific host factors that influence its formation. We have found that a significant population of CCPs are labeled with both early and late endosomal markers. One of these, the host lipid phosphatidylinositol 3- phosphate (PI3P), is required for the proper maturation of phagosomes. However, reports from other microbial pathogens suggest that prolonging its association to the pathogen-containing phagosome inhibits its maturation. The purpose of this study is to determine the effect of the presence of PI3P on the CCP’s properties. THP-1 cells are infected with KN99 C. neoformans and BY4741 S. cerevisiae for up to 8 hours. Cells are then stained with antibodies against EEA1, an early endosomal marker, or LAMP1, a late lysosomal marker. Up to 30% of all CCPs exhibit a hybrid state (PI3P and LAMP1) solely in cells infected with KN99 compared to BY4741 infected cells. While the presence of PI3P is unsurprising, its extended presence could be causing a delay in the fusion of the maturing phagosome with lysosomes which would be required to acidify. This, in turn, could promote the establishment of a permissive niche. We plan to further investigate this phenomenon using chemical inhibition or knock-down of the PI3P kinase.

pres132.title: Simulating actin-driven phagocytosis of Cryptococcus neoformans
pres132.abstract: The actin cytoskeleton is a central component of almost all animal and plant cells and plays a critical role in a wide range of biological processes. In humans, these include normal organ development, cancer, and immune defence. Within the immune system, actin dynamics are essential for phagocytosis, where coordinated cytoskeletal reorganisation drives the formation and progression of the phagocytic cup. This process is particularly important for the clearance of fungal pathogens such as Cryptococcus neoformans, which enters the body via the lungs and can cause life-threatening cryptococcal meningitis in immunocompromised patients. Notably, C. neoformans exhibits a range of sizes and morphologies that challenge effective phagocytic uptake. The overall function of the actin cytoskeleton arises from the collective behaviour of actin filaments and associated regulatory proteins. Among these, the Arp2/3 complex plays a key role by nucleating branched actin networks that are thought to be crucial for efficient target engulfment. We present a computational modelling framework that describes the kinetics and dynamics of the actin cytoskeleton using Monte Carlo–Brownian dynamics simulations. The model combines stochastic Monte Carlo approaches with Brownian dynamics to enable large-scale network remodelling to emerge from small-scale interactions. This work demonstrates how stochastic models of the actin cytoskeleton can be used to investigate immune responses to fungal infection, providing mechanistic insight into host–pathogen interactions during cryptococcal phagocytosis. This complements experimental studies by supporting hypothesis generation and potentially reducing reliance on costly and technically challenging in vitro and in vivo models.

pres133.title: Determining the kinetics and consequences of germination in Cryptococcus spore-mediated disease
pres133.abstract: Spores and yeast can both cause fatal cryptococcosis in mouse models of disease; however, spores from some backgrounds cause disease when their yeast parents cannot. Spores also differ from yeast in that they are readily engulfed by phagocytes and then disseminate from the lung via an apparent Trojan Horse mechanism. How differences in initial spore-phagocyte interactions influence disease outcomes are poorly understood. We hypothesize that lung escape is directly influenced by rate of germination and resulting changes in frequency of phagocytosis. To determine the timeline of germination in vivo, we infected mice intranasally with spores and evaluated germination progression over 24 hours. We discovered that germination in the mouse lung progressed at a rate similar to germination in vitro. However, in contrast to in vitro germination in which >99% of spores germinate, a sub-population of spores (~25%) failed to germinate in the mouse. To help determine the effects of host conditions on germination, we developed a lung homogenate (LH) medium and determined that it mimicked the in vivo germination phenotypes. To identify mechanisms of germination initiation we carried out RNA-seq of spores germinating in LH, which revealed specific upregulation of transcripts associated with stress tolerance and virulence. These data suggest that host-like conditions elicit a rapid and protective transcriptional response that facilitates survival and potentially pathogenicity. These findings lay the foundation for future experiments to determine how interactions with lung phagocytes change over time and how germination responses impact dissemination and disease outcomes.

pres134.title: Innate Immune Responses Differ in Latent, Hypervirulent, and Typical Clinical Isolates of Cryptococcus neoformans
pres134.abstract: Cryptococcus neoformans is an environmental fungal pathogen that primarily causes disease in immunocompromised people, including HIV/AIDS patients and those undergoing organ transplants or chemotherapy. It is known that infections caused by different clinical isolates of C. neoformans can lead to different disease manifestations; we classify these manifestations based on disease presentation severity in the inhalational mouse model of cryptococcosis as either typical, hypervirulent, or latent. Immunological responses between these three categories vary —typical strains induce a detrimental type 2/Th2 mediated-response, latent strains have a beneficial type 1/Th1 mediated-response, and hypervirulent strains cause a detrimental overreactive type 1/type 17 response. It is unknown whether these differences in immune polarization are occurring as a result of early innate or adaptive immunity. To test the hypothesis that differential immune responses correlate with early innate responses, we conducted in vitro phagocytosis assays using bone marrow-derived macrophages and dendritic cells isolated from A/J mice and typical, latent, and hypervirulent C. neoformans isolates. Our results show a lower phagocytosis rate in the hypervirulent UgCl 422 and a higher phagocytosis index in the latent UgCl 223 compared to the typical reference strain KN99α. These data suggest that early innate phagocytosis rates correlate with immune polarization and subsequent disease outcomes. This early innate response may influence antigen presentation and subsequent immune polarization during the C. neoformans infection.

pres135.title: Titan cell formation is essential for C. neoformans latency
pres135.abstract: Virtually everyone is exposed to Cryptococcus in childhood by inhalation of fungal cells, with an estimated 95% of adults harboring a persistent subclinical Cryptococcus infection in their lungs. This latent infection reactivates to cause disseminated disease in immunocompromised people yet little is known about how this latency arises. Our lab developed a mouse model of latent C. neoformans infection to define both host and pathogen factors that influence latency. Titan cells are an important virulence factor produced during C. neoformans pulmonary infection. Previous studies showed that deletion of the G-protein coupled receptors gpr4àÜ and gpr5àÜ significantly reduced titan cell formation from 30% to <5% and resulted in attenuated disease in the KN99 acute infection model. To determine the role of titan cells during latent infection, we generated a gpr4àÜ gpr5àÜ mutant in the latent isolate UgCl223. During latent infection, the pulmonary UgCl223 cell population consisted of 70% titan cells whereas the gpr4àÜgpr5àÜ mutant had <20% titan cells at 14 days post infection (dpi). Surprisingly, no titan cells were detected in the gpr4àÜgpr5àÜ mutant by 30 dpi while the wildtype strain retained its high titan cell production. Fungal burden was significantly lower in the gpr4àÜgpr5àÜ mutant at 30 dpi, with clearance of the gpr4àÜgpr5àÜ mutant by 90 dpi. These results indicate titan cell formation is critical for essential for persistence of latent infections and could serve as an effective target for new treatment strategies.

pres136.title: Cryptococcus neoformans serotype A virulence and pathogenicity are capsular glucuronoxylomannan (GXM) motif composition dependent
pres136.abstract: The Cryptococcus neoformans extracellular polysaccharide (EPS) is essential for virulence and host immunomodulation. Here, we analyzed the host pathogen interaction of four C. neoformans strains with different polysaccharide structures: two multi-motif (H99 and KN99α) and two single-motif (Mu-1 and ATCC-24064 (24064)). We compared a variety of virulence and pathogenic factors, like capsule and cell body size, melanization, urease, and laccase production. Mice were also infected by the intranasal (IN) or intravenous (IV) route. Survival, fungal load, cell lung population, cytokines, and histology were analyzed. No differences in cell size or capsule size were found between the four strains. However, polysaccharide sizes were highly variable, and no significant differences were observed for all other virulence factors. Polysaccharides from single and multi-motif strains elicited different effects on Nitrite and reactive oxygen species production in macrophage-like cells and dendritic-like cells in vitro. There were differences in lung neutrophil infiltration between the analyzed strains, and a higher presence of activated macrophages and CD4+ lymphocytes for the Mu-1 or 24064 strains. Cytokines differed from the IN and IV infections. Lung histology showed increased inflammatory cell presence after infection with Mu-1 or 24064 strains, with no cryptococcal cells observed in the lung parenchyma or alveoli, whereas the opposite pattern was seen after challenges with H99 or KN99α. These results suggest that variation in polysaccharide composition and structure can translate into differences in virulence and pathogenicity.

pres137.title: Identification of drug targets for Cryptococcus neoformans drug discovery
pres137.abstract: Cryptococcus neoformans (Cn) is a pathogen with global distribution and is a primary cause of mortality and morbidity among immunocompromised individuals. Limited accessibility, toxicity, and resistance to available antifungal classes are key challenges. Consequently, there is an urgent need to develop novel therapeutic options targeting previously unexploited pathways. This study was therefore conducted to identify a potential drug target against Cn and related pathogenic fungi. Published essentiality data were used as a starting point for target identification. FungiDB and NCBI databases were used to identify fungal orthologs and human homologs, respectively. The identified targets were further characterised using multiple databases, including BRENDA, UniProt, PDB, AlphaFold, and FungiDB, to evaluate which targets have published 3D structures, are likely to be expressed and purified, are tractable, and are enzymes. fPocket, PyMOL, ChimeraX, JALVIEW and UniProt were used to assess the druggability of each target. All targets with druggable substrate-binding pockets were evaluated for assayability using InterPro, SciFinder, UniProt and literature. Cn has 1,465 essential targets, of which 113 lack human homologs and are also essential in Candida albicans. Seventy-nine of these targets are deemed tractable, of which 40 are enzymes, and nine exhibit druggable pockets with well-aligned binding and active sites. Five targets are identified as readily assayable, with Thioredoxin reductase (TrxR) demonstrating the most favourable attributes. The majority of the potential drug targets identified in this project are novel and have promising profiles, worthy of further exploration for antifungal drug discovery.

pres138.title: Dormant Cryptococcus neoformans yeasts persist in a concealed state in a murine model of infection
pres138.abstract: Several microorganisms, including Cryptococcus neoformans, can enter a dormant state characterized by reduced metabolic activity, transcription, and translation, enabling survival under unfavorable conditions with the potential to resume growth when environmental cues become permissive. The present study aimed to identify host immune factors that prevent or promote reactivation of dormant C. neoformans. We established an in vitro protocol to induce dormancy and generate viable but non-culturable (VBNC) yeasts and developed a murine model of latent infection using these cells. Wild-type (WT) mice infected with VBNC yeasts exhibited prolonged survival, and no fungal growth was detected in pulmonary tissue by colony-forming unit assays. However, following immunosuppression, high fungal burdens were recovered from the lungs and brains, confirming the presence of viable latent infection that did not cause mortality. In contrast, TLR-4-, TLR9-deficient mice and bone marrow-derived macrophages from these animals showed enhanced reactivation of dormant fungi compared to WT controls, even in the absence of immunosuppression. Moreover, at 110 days post-infection with VBNC cells, TLR9-deficient mice displayed reduced survival, indicating a role for TLR9 in maintaining fungal dormancy. Flow cytometric analyses further demonstrated an increased frequency of monocytederived dendritic cells in TLR9-deficient animals infected with dormant yeasts, implicating this population in the regulation of latency. Collectively, these findings indicate that the murine immune response, particularly TLR9-dependent pathways, contributes to maintaining C. neoformans dormancy and restricting fungal reactivation, thereby limiting infection-associated pathology.

pres139.title: Wasp venom-derived peptides exhibit antifungal activity against Cryptococcus neoformans
pres139.abstract: Antimicrobial peptides (AMPs) constitute a diverse class of multifunctional bioactive molecules that exhibit broad-spectrum antimicrobial, antineoplastic, and immunomodulatory activities and have emerged as attractive candidates for the development of next-generation antifungal agents to overcome antifungal resistance, host toxicity, and the limited efficacy of currently available therapies. In this study, we evaluated the antifungal activity of the mastoparan peptides Polybia-MPII, isolated from the venom of Pseudopolybia vespiceps testacea, and Agelaia-MPI, derived from Parachartergus fraternus venom, against the human pathogenic yeast Cryptococcus neoformans. Both Polybia-MPII and Agelaia-MPI displayed potent in vitro fungicidal activity, which was associated with increased fungal plasma membrane permeability and significant alterations in cellular biophysical properties, including cell size and surface roughness. Combination assays revealed additive interactions between the two peptides and between each peptide and fluconazole. Notably, disruption of established C. neoformans biofilms was observed only at high peptide concentrations. In the Galleria mellonella infection model, treatment with either peptide significantly increased larval survival, exceeding 80% in comparison with the untreated control group. Collectively, these results demonstrate that Agelaia-MPI and Polybia-MPII exhibit relevant antifungal and in vivo protective activities, supporting their further development as lead compounds for novel antifungal strategies and highlighting wasp venom–derived mastoparan peptides as promising components of therapeutic formulations for the treatment of cryptococcosis.

pres140.title: Multi-omics analysis of extracellular vesicles secreted by Cryptococcus neoformans and their role in modulating the chronic inflammatory process
pres140.abstract:
Cryptococcus neoformans is a major opportunistic fungal pathogen and a leading cause of life-threatening meningoencephalitis in immunocompromised individuals, particularly following primary pulmonary infection. Protective immunity against cryptococcosis is predominantly mediated by CD4+ T cell–driven Th1 and Th17 responses, orchestrated by
dendritic cells (DCs), which promote classical (M1) macrophage activation and effective fungal clearance. The efficiency of this process critically depends on the pulmonary microenvironment, the expression of co-stimulatory molecules, and the quality of antigen processing and presentation by DCs. Extracellular vesicles (EVs) released by fungi have emerged as important mediators of host–pathogen communication, acting as carriers of complex bioactive cargo that modulate immune cell activation, cytokine production, and inflammasome signaling, thereby influencing disease outcome. Although environmental and nutritional conditions are known to affect fungal physiology, how nutrient availability reshapes the molecular composition of C. neoformans EVs and their immunological functions remains poorly defined. In this project, we apply integrated multi-omics approaches, including proteomics, lipidomics, and metabolomics, to systematically characterize EVs produced by C. neoformans cultured under distinct nutrient availability conditions (100%, 50%, and 10%). We further assess how nutritionally driven alterations in EV cargo modulate DC maturation and activation in murine models of pulmonary infection and how these changes translate into differential local immune responses in the lung. To enhance translational relevance, we extend our analyses to primary human DCs obtained from HIV-infected individuals, with and without cryptococcal co-infection. Preliminary LC–MS/MS-based metabolomic and lipidomic profiling revealed that EVs derived from nutrient-rich and intermediate growth conditions are markedly enriched in bioactive lipid species, including phosphatidylethanolamines, phosphatidylcholines, ergosterol, and sphingoid bases. This distinct lipid signature suggests that EVs generated under favorable nutritional environments have potential immunomodulatory and immunosuppressive capacities, which may contribute to the establishment of a permissive niche for fungal persistence within the host. Together, our findings support a model in which nutritional cues dynamically reprogram the molecular composition and immunological impact of C. neoformans EVs, with direct implications for host defense and disease progression.

pres141.title: Affordable PEGylated liposomal amphotericin B enables effective and safe oral treatment of experimental murine cryptococcosis
pres141.abstract: Cryptococcosis is a life-threatening fungal infection that predominantly affects immunocompromised individuals and remains a major global health challenge. Amphotericin B (AmB) is a central pillar of therapy; however, its clinical use is limited by nephrotoxicity, high cost, and the need for intravenous administration. In this study, we developed an affordable PEGylated liposomal AmB formulation (LAmB) using low-cost lipids and a simple, scalable ethanol injection method, and evaluated its oral efficacy and safety in a murine model of Cryptococcus neoformans H99 infection. The formulation produced uniform nanoparticles with a mean diameter of approximately 110 nm and an encapsulation efficiency exceeding 95%. Oral administration of LAmB significantly improved survival, achieving complete protection of infected animals, and markedly reduced fungal burden in the lungs and brain when compared with untreated mice and animals receiving conventional AmB deoxycholate. Histopatholo gical analysis demonstrated substantial preservation of pulmonary architecture in LAmB-treated mice, accompanied by a pronounced reduction in pulmonary inflammation. Consistently, LAmB treatment significantly decreased CXCL10 and IL-6 levels in lung tissue and bronchoalveolar lavage fluid. Toxicological evaluation revealed only mild renal alterations and no evidence of hepatotoxicity, consistent with the known safety profile of AmB formulations. Overall, these findings demonstrate that orally administered PEGylated liposomal AmB combines potent antifungal efficacy with reduced inflammation and acceptable safety. Its oral bioavailability and low-cost composition support its potential as a promising and accessible therapeutic strategy for treating cryptococcosis, particularly in resource-limited settings.

pres142.title: Ebselen leads Cryptococcus gattii yeasts to death by disturbing redox homeostasis and shows antifungal efficacy in a murine model of cryptococcosis
pres142.abstract: Cryptococcus gattii is a World Health Organization priority fungal pathogen responsible for pulmonary and cerebral cryptococcosis in immunocompetent individuals, with limited therapeutic options. This study evaluated the antifungal activity of the organoselenium compound ebselen (EbSe) against C. gattii using in vitro assays and a murine model of cryptococcosis. Minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) were determined by broth microdilution for strain R265, clinical isolates (n=8), and distinct morphotypes, including enlarged capsule and titan cells, ranging from 0.5–2 µg/mL. Time-kill assays demonstrated fast fungicidal activity within the first hours of exposure. EbSe reduced capsule size, increased capsular permeability, and inhibited melanization in L-DOPA–supplemented medium. Cell death protocols revealed DNA fragmentation, chromatin condensation, mitochondrial membrane depolarization, and membrane permeability after treatment. EbSe exposure increased reactive oxy gen species and lipid peroxidation, while reducing intracellular glutathione and total thiol levels. An increase in superoxide dismutase activity was also observed, suggesting a compensatory response to oxidative stress. Together, these findings indicate that EbSe disrupts fungal redox homeostasis, leading to oxidative damage and cell death. In a murine model, BALB/c mice were infected intranasally with 5x105 UFC/mice of C. gattii R265 and treated daily with Amphotericin (AMB, 1 mg/kg), Fluconazole (FLC, 100 mg/kg), EbSe (10 mg/kg) or combinations (AMB and EbSe; FLC and EbSe) for 6 or 13 days. EbSe treatment alone or in combination was associated with reduced fungal burden, particularly in the brain, highlighting its pharmacological activity during infection and supporting further investigation of its antifungal effects.

pres143.title: Uncovering Genetic Drivers of Biofilm Formation in Cryptococcus neoformans to Enable Antibiofilm Drug Discovery
pres143.abstract: Fungal pathogens form biofilms—structured communities of cells embedded in a self-produced extracellular polymeric matrix—that enhance survival under environmental stress and contribute to persistent infections. In the human fungal pathogen, Cryptococcus neoformans, biofilm formation is associated with reduced antifungal susceptibility, often requiring higher drug doses or surgical intervention. For cryptococcal biofilms, key structural components shared with the polysaccharide capsule (a major virulence factor of C. neoformans) influence biofilm formation. While capsule-deficient strains frequently exhibit impaired biofilm formation, the molecular determinants driving this process remain incompletely understood. This study aims to identify proteins involved in cryptococcal biofilm formation and discover novel antibiofilm molecules targeting them. Using a forward genetics approach, we screened a C. neoformans var. grubii KN99 deletion library of 4,030 mutants (MadhaniÔø? Ôø?s Library) with a high-throughput biofilm quantification assay. Nineteen deletion strains exhibiting greater than 50% reduction in biofilm formation were prioritized for further analysis. These mutants were evaluated using additional phenotypic assays, including capsule production, thermotolerance, stress response, and virulence in the Galleria mellonella infection model. In parallel, proteins encoded by prioritized genes are being evaluated as therapeutic targets using a target-based drug discovery strategy. AlphaFold-predicted protein structures are employed for virtual high-throughput screening (vHTS) to identify small molecules with high predicted affinity for biofilm-associated targets. Computational screening is complemented by state-of-the-art mass spectrometry–based identification and characterization of bioactive compounds. Collectively, this work uncovers previously unrecognized molecular determinants of cryptococcal biofilm formation while discovering novel antibiofilm molecules using integrated computational and experimental drug discovery strategies.

pres144.title: Developing new antifungals in an Academic - Industry partnership.
pres144.abstract: Cryptococcal meningoencephalitis (CM) has a major health impact, mainly in sub-Saharan Africa, with limited treatment options available. Current treatments suffer from multiple limitations, including long duration, toxicity, parenteral administration, resistance and poor availability. There is therefore an urgent need to develop new, better treatments. The Drug Discovery Unit at the University of Dundee, the Centre for Medical Mycology at the University of Exeter and GSK global health have joined forces to tackle this challenge. In a 5-year Wellcome-funded programme we seek to deliver new pre-clinical candidates for CM that act through novel mechanisms. In this presentation we will cover the rationale behind our partnership and describe the overall approach of the programme. A key focus in the initial stages of the programme has been the development of cell-based Cryptococcus neoformans assays that are suitable for drug discovery, in terms of throughput, and, more important ly, in terms of predicting in vivo and patient outcomes. We have developed high-throughput screening assays that represent the in vivo morphotype heterogeneity of Cryptococcus as well as assays that assess fungicidal activity, including assays that are able to detect complete cidality (i.e. all cells killed). We have used these assays to carry out a high-throughput screening campaign and have identified new compounds with activity against Cryptococcus neoformans. In conclusion, our partnership has brought together all the skills and expertise required to successfully deliver much needed new preclinical candidates for cryptococcal infections, and has put in place key tools to achieve this.

pres145.title: The role of thermotolerance and nutrient availability on resistance to fluconazole in clinical isolates of Cryptococcus neoformans.
pres145.abstract: Cryptococcus neoformans is the major causative agent of cryptococcosis and fungal meningoencephalitis across the globe and accounts for approximately 15% of all AIDS related deaths. One of the most applied antifungal medications for maintenance therapy in cryptococcosis is fluconazole (FLC). Unfortunately, development of resistance to FLC occurs frequently due to its fungistatic vs. fungicidal effect. The effects of environmental conditions on resistance to the drug fluconazole have been modeled quantitatively using Candida albicans, another common fungal pathogen. However, minimal research has been done to quantify and model the effects of environmental conditions on FLC drug resistance in a variety of C. neoformans clinical isolates. This study uses quantitative techniques that were previously utilized for C. albicans to characterize how environmental factors, including temperature and nutrient availability, impact FLC resistance in C. neoformans. Disk diffusion assays, i mage analysis software, diskImageR, and heat gradient assays were used to assess key differences in resistance to fluconazole between clinical isolates. Our results showed that resistance to fluconazole and responses to temperature and nutrient availability were strain specific and elucidates genetic factors as being a key influence on those responses. Our results also show distinct differences in thermotolerance and resistance to FLC between C. albicans and C. neoformans. Overall, this research displays the complex nature of antifungal drug responses in C. neoformans. Our results suggest that there could be clinical importance in distinguishing C. neoformans strains when treating cryptococcosis and could provide insights to help improve anticryptococcal therapy.

pres146.title: Use of Oteseconazole for Consolidation Therapy and Secondary Prophylaxis in Fluconazole Non-Susceptible Cryptococcal Meningitis.
pres146.abstract: Background: Fluconazole non-susceptible Cryptococcus neoformans (MIC 16 ¬µg/mL) represents a significant therapeutic challenge in culture-positive relapse due to the absence of alternative oral consolidation therapies. Oteseconazole, a tetrazole CYP51 inhibitor with potent in vitro activity against Cryptococcus (MIC≤ à0.125 ¬µg/mL) has a prolonged half-life of à138 days. Oteseconazole offers a promising option for fluconazole non-susceptible cryptococcosis. Methods: In Kampala, Uganda, we enrolled a compassionate-care cohort for individuals with culture-confirmed cryptococcal-relapse due to fluconazole non-susceptible isolates (MIC  16 ¬µg/mL). After re-induction with liposomal amphotericin-based therapy, participants received oteseconazole 600 mg twice daily for 10 days as loading dose and then weekly 600 mg for 4 weeks. We assessed clinical course, CSF sterility, and recurrence. Results: Two individuals with culture-positive cryptococcal-relapse due to fluconazole non-susceptible Cryptococcus initiated oteseconazole therapy. Case 1 presented on antiretroviral therapy with CD4 count of 294 cells/¬µL and a third episode of cryptococcal-relapse. CSF culture grew 6,500 CFU/mL at relapse with a fluconazole MIC=256 ¬µg/mL. Case 2 presented on antiretroviral therapy with CD4 count of 124 cells/¬µL. CSF fungal culture grew 195,000 CFU/mL at relapse with a fluconazole MIC=32 ¬µg/mL. Both participants achieved clinical stabilization and CSF sterility. To date, >2 months of follow-up, no further episodes of meningitis, disease recurrence, or drug-related toxicity have been observed during follow-up. Conclusions: Early experience suggests that oteseconazole may provide effective consolidation therapy and secondary prophylaxis in cryptococcal meningitis. These preliminary observations support further systematic evaluation of oteseconazole as a novel oral agent for cryptococcosis. Enrollment is ongoing.

pres147.title: Experience of research nurses with oral Encochleated amphotericin B for treatment of Cryptococcal Meningitis in a resource-limited setting
pres147.abstract: Background and aims Cryptococcal meningitis accounts for upto 19% of AIDS-related deaths. First-line therapy uses either seven-day induction with intravenous amphotericin B deoxycholate and oral flucytosine or single-dose liposomal amphotericin with oral flucytosine and fluconazole. Besides the commonly used intravenous formulations of amphotericin B, an oral nanocrystal amphotericin B (MAT2203) formulation is being evaluated for efficacy to treat invasive fungal infections. This new experimental oral formulation has not been used before. Herein, we aimed to describe our experiences with using oral cAmB in a resource-limited setting from the perspective of research nurses. Methods This was part of a sequential Phase I and then Phase II multi-site randomized controlled trial that aimed to investigate the efficacy of oral nanocrystal amphotericin B formulation among persons with cryptococcal meningitis. Results We screened 240 participants in the two trials and enrolled 168 of which 81 (48%) were women. We found oral amphotericin a better alternative to intravenous amphotericin B deoxycholate due to less toxicity, mostly limited to gastrointestinal-related toxicities only. We clinically observed no drug reactions like rigors, phlebitis, and less vomiting among patients on oral amphotericin as compared to those on intravenous amphotericin B deoxycholate. Subjectively, meningitis symptoms of patients on oral amphotericin seemed to overall clinically improve more rapidly compared to those receiving intravenous amphotericin B deoxycholate. Few adverse events were observed. Conclusions In conclusion, oral amphotericin B was generally safe and well tolerated. However, it requires some training for the nurse, patient and caretakers for better administration, adherence and treatment outcomes.

pres148.title: In vivo targeting of Hsp90 in murine models using positron emission tomography, magnetic resonance imaging and computed tomography.
pres148.abstract: Introduction: Heat shock proteins (Hsp) contribute to one of Cryptococcus neoformans main virulence factors, its ability to grow at elevated temperature. Targeting Hsp90 has emerged as a potential approach to combat cryptococcosis. Using positron emission tomography (PET), tracers to target Hsp90 serves both diagnostic but also therapeutic purposes. Methods: Binding specificity of [11C]HSP990 to C.neoformans was assessed through pre-incubation with HSP990. In vivo PET/MRI and PET/CT studies of [11C]HSP990 were conducted in Balb/C mice following intratracheal (10^5 H99) or intracranially injection (10^4 H99). PBS and LPS injections acted as negative and positive controls. PET studies were performed under baseline conditions and after pre-treatment with HSP990 (5 mg/kg, ip) to evaluate in vivo Hsp90 binding specificity. Post-mortem in vitro Hsp90-specific binding of [°°C] HSP990 was evaluated using autoradiography. Pathogenesis and lesion formation of cerebral cryptococcosis was confirmed by MRI, bioluminescence and CT imaging. Results: [°°C]HSP990 binding to cryptococci was higher at 37°C compared to 30°C and RT. Tracer binding was confirmed to be Hsp90-specific, as significant reductions were observed after blocking with Hsp90 inhibitors. In vivo PET imaging of infected mouse brains demonstrated Hsp90-specific [°°C] HSP990 uptake, with isointensity between lesions and healthy brain. In contrast, in vivo PET imaging of infected mouse lungs showed significantly higher Hsp90-specific tracer retention in infected lungs compared to healthy tissue and compared to LPS-positive and PBS-negative controls. Conclusions: [°°C]HSP990 is a suitable tracer for in vivo diagnosis and treatment follow-up of cryptococcosis with excellent contrast in particular in infected lungs and good BBB penetration.

pres149.title: A novel CpG-adjuvanted heat-killed Cryptococcus vaccine induces superior immune protection in an intramuscular model
pres149.abstract: Vaccines have been transformative in controlling infectious diseases. However, despite the rising global burden of invasive mycoses, no fungal vaccine is available for clinical use. Hence, there is a great unmet need to develop vaccines against fungal infections, including cryptococcosis. We previously developed a whole-cell vaccine candidate based on heat-killed C. neoformans fbp1Δ mutant cells (HK-fbp1), which provided complete protection against cryptococcosis following intranasal immunization by inducing durable pulmonary immunity. In this study, we developed a highly effective intramuscular and subcutaneous vaccine (HK-fbp1/ODN) by incorporating a CpG adjuvant into HK-fbp1. The adjuvanted formulation conferred robust protection against virulent C. neoformans challenge, achieving near-sterilizing immunity in multiple mouse strains. Immunological analysis revealed that intramuscular HK-fbp1/ODN vaccination promoted early recruitment of monocytes, monocyte-derived dendri tic cells, and neutrophils to the lung, accompanied by strong CD4+ T cell activation. Vaccine protection was associated with a spatially organized T cell response, characterized by proliferative priming in muscle-draining lymph nodes and potent Th1/Th17 effector cytokine production in lung-draining lymph nodes. IFN-? was essential for vaccine-mediated protection, whereas other interferons were dispensable. Notably, HK-fbp1/ODN vaccination remained protective in mice lacking CD4+ T cells or B cells, underscoring its potential utility in immunocompromised hosts. Collectively, this study established a CpG-adjuvanted whole-cell cryptococcal vaccine that elicits coordinated innate and T cell-mediated immunity and is compatible with standard parenteral delivery, providing a strong foundation for the development of clinically relevant antifungal vaccines.

pres150.title: Activity of Ibrexafungerp Against Clinical and Environmental Isolates of Cryptococcus neoformans and Cryptococcus gattii
pres150.abstract: Background and aims: Cryptococcosis remains a life-threatening fungal infection, particularly among immunocompromised individuals, with Cryptococcus neoformans and Cryptococcus gattii causing most cases. Despite advances in antifungal therapy, mortality rates remain high. Drug toxicity, limited access to first-line antifungals in resource-limited settings, and the emergence of resistance worsen management and underscore the urgent need for novel therapeutic options. Ibrexafungerp (IBX), a first-in-class oral triterpenoid inhibitor of ?-(1,3)-D-glucan synthase, has demonstrated potent antifungal activity. Therefore, we aimed to evaluate the in vitro activity of IBX against clinical and environmental cryptococcal isolates, including isolates with reduced susceptibility to fluconazole. Methods: Fifteen clinical and 5 environmental isolates were studied, 10 of C. neoformans (serotypes A, D, and A/D) and 10 of C. gattii (serotypes B and C). From these, three C. neoformans and f our C. gattii have reduced susceptibility to fluconazole (16 ¬µg/mL). Antifungal susceptibility testing was performed by broth microdilution according to the CLSI, with IBX ranging from 0.015 to 8 ¬µg/mL. Results: Against both cryptococcal species, the MIC90 for IBX at the 50% endpoint was 2 ¬µg/mL. At the 100% endpoint, the geometric MIC was 3.4 µg/mL and 3.2 µg/mL for C. neoformans and C. gattii, respectively. Conclusions: IBX may overcome the intrinsic echinocandin resistance of Cryptococcus species, as well as the reduced susceptibility to fluconazole of some isolates, independently of the serotype and the source. Our findings contribute to the growing evidence supporting novel antifungal strategies against cryptococcosis, addressing a critical unmet need in the management of this clinically important mycosis.

pres151.title: Repurposing Benzimidazole to Inhibit a Dissemination-Prone Morphotype of Cryptococcus neoformans
pres151.abstract: Mortality from Cryptococcus neoformans primarily results from fungal dissemination from the lungs to the brain. We previously demonstrated that a small morphotype (≤10 µm total diameter) termed ÄúseedÄù cells, forms in the lungs and appears in the brain early in the dissemination process. These cells exhibit an enhanced capacity for dissemination and the establishment of new infections in extrapulmonary tissues. Here, we report 24 small-molecule hits from a drug repurposing screen that significantly inhibited seed cell formation and propagation (p ≤ 0.005). The strongest inhibitors —albendazole, fenbendazole, and mebendazole— are benzimidazole anthelmintics. In C. neoformans, this appears to disrupt normal cell development, resulting in enlarged cells and defective division. Benzimidazole treatment reduced seed cell formation from ~40% to ~5% and decreased established populations to ~4% within 24 hours. These findings suggest that benzimidazoles may inhibit the dissemination of C. neoformans and represent a promising class of compounds for treatment. While seed cells are confirmed, mechanisms of their formation and self-propagation capacity remain unclear. We hypothesize seed cells are the daughters of a ÄúnormalÄù mother cell under seed-cell-inducing conditions. To address this, live-cell imaging will visualize seed cell production and their ability to produce identical morphotypes. During dissemination from the lung, seed cells encounter various tissues, blood, and cerebrospinal fluid. Using media mimicking these conditions, we will measure seed cell production to determine how these microenvironments influence the preservation of this morphotype. Elucidating mechanisms of seed cell formation and propagation provides a novel strategy to halt progression of cryptococcal meningitis.

pres152.title: Enzymatic Inactivation of Fluconazole Defines a Novel Resistance Pathway in Cryptococcus spp.
pres152.abstract: Recently, an increasing body of evidence has demonstrated that agrochemicals play a significant role in the development of fungal resistance to clinical antifungal agents. Our studies revealed that the adaptation of Cryptococcus neoformans and Cryptococcus gattii to azole fungicides, strobilurins, and ?-tubulin inhibitors alters fluconazole susceptibility and modulates host–pathogen interactions. These findings emphasize the importance of developing a comprehensive understanding of additional resistance mechanisms. Resistance in fungi is commonly driven by the overexpression of efflux pumps, gene mutations, and chromosomal alterations that reduce treatment efficacy. Adopting an integrative approach combining molecular biology, biochemistry, and genomics within a One Health framework, we investigated whether Cryptococcus species are able to enzymatically inactivate fluconazole, a key therapeutic agent for treating cryptococcosis. Resistome and gene expression analyses rev ealed an increased number of genes coding for acetyltransferases, which were overexpressed in response to agrochemical exposure. Strains overexpressing these enzymes convert fluconazole into O-acetyl-fluconazole, a derivative that cannot bind to 14-α-demethylase and therefore lacks antifungal activity. Remarkably, Cryptococcus spp. strains recently isolated from the cerebrospinal fluid of cryptococcosis patients were also found to acetylate fluconazole, confirming the clinical relevance of this mechanism. Furthermore, chemical inhibition of acetyltransferases reduced fluconazole acetylation and restored its antifungal activity, suggesting that these enzymes could be targeted in the development of new drugs. Collectively, our findings provide the first evidence that Cryptococcus spp. can chemically modify and inactivate fluconazole through enzymatic acetylation.

pres153.title: Decoding Antifungal Resistance Using Proteome-Wide Approaches
pres153.abstract: Fungal diseases pose a significant and growing threat to global health, particularly as the effectiveness of existing antifungal therapies continues to decline. The widespread emergence of azole resistance driven by prolonged clinical use, environmental fungicide exposure, and fungal adaptation has severely compromised treatment outcomes and underscores the urgent need for new therapeutic strategies. Quantitative proteomics offers a powerful approach to uncover molecular mechanisms underlying antifungal resistance and to identify candidate pathways and targets for intervention. Here, we present an established proteomics-driven framework to define protein-level signatures associated with fluconazole resistance in the opportunistic human fungal pathogen Cryptococcus neoformans. Using this approach, we previously demonstrated that targeting ATP-dependent stress and protein quality-control pathways can restore antifungal susceptibility in resistant strains, with efficacy valida ted in both cellular and in vivo infection models. Mechanistic analyses further revealed links between these pathways and key metabolic processes, including heme biosynthesis and ergosterol production, that influence azole responsiveness. Building on this foundation, we are now applying this validated strategy to investigate additional candidate proteins and pathways with the potential to reverse antifungal resistance, the discovery of compounds that overcome resistance. We are also developing computational and experimental chemoproteomics workflows to define mechanisms of acton for the compounds and assess on- and off-target effects for the design of optimized therapeutics. Collectively, this work highlights the utility of quantitative proteomics as a discovery platform for identifying and prioritizing novel therapeutic targets, and establishes a scalable framework for developing strategies to counteract antifungal drug resistance in clinically relevant fungal.

pres154.title: Targeting the electron transport chain as a strategy for novel antifungal drugs against Cryptococcus neoformans
pres154.abstract: Cryptococcus neoformans remains a major cause of fungal meningitis, particularly in immunocompromised patients, highlighting the urgent need for new antifungal therapies with activity in host-relevant environments. Here, we demonstrate that our drug exhibits potent anticryptococcal activity through disruption of fungal bioenergetics and virulence-associated phenotypes. Treatment with the compound significantly reduced cellular respiration, ATP levels, and viability of C. neoformans. Importantly, its antifungal activity was retained in both nutrient-rich YPD medium and artificial cerebrospinal fluid (aCSF), supporting its potential utility for the treatment of cryptococcal meningitis. In addition to impairing growth and survival, the drug inhibited key pathogenic traits of C. neoformans. Under capsule-inducing conditions, it prevented capsule development, while under titan cell-inducing conditions in vitro, it significantly impaired titan cell formation. These findings suggest that the compound targets not only fungal survival but also mechanisms linked to immune evasion and disease progression. In vivo, the drug showed no detectable toxicity in the Galleria mellonella model and significantly reduced mortality in infected larvae, indicating therapeutic efficacy. Furthermore, the compound was non-toxic to red blood cells and remained stable in blood, supporting a favorable preliminary safety and stability profile. Collectively, these results identify our drug as a promising antifungal candidate with activity against C. neoformans in both standard and host-relevant conditions.

pres155.title: Trehalose phosphate synthase 1 (tps1) deficient Cryptococcus neoformans alters host murine adaptive immune response
pres155.abstract: The trehalose biosynthetic enzyme, trehalose-6-phosphate synthase (TPS1) is a virulence factor and a promising therapeutic target. Previously we established that the loss of TPS1 renders C. neoformans vulnerable to resident and innate pulmonary defenses. Unlike the lethal WT (H99), the TPS1-deleted (tps1åÓ) strain is rapidly cleared from the infected lungs in a CD4+ T cell independent manner. While CD4+ T cells were dispensable for tps1åÓ-clearance, we investigated if tps1åÓ could trigger a vaccine-like modulation of the adaptive immune response, further enhancing antifungal protection. Mice were exposed to 2 live-doses of tps1åÓ intranasally 14 days(D) apart, then challenged with the C. neoformans H99 (D0) after a 14D recovery. tps1åÓ-exposure substantially improved 70-D-survival (from 0-70%) and reduced brain and spleen dissemination, compared to the SHAM-exposed, infected mice. tps1åÓ-exposure generated an enhanced early (D7) pulmonary CD4+ T cell response and a protecti vely polarized T cell signature was observed. This increased frequency of protectively (Th1/Th17)-polarized T cells was sustained at D21. Depletion of CD4+ T cells during the WT challenge significantly ablated this protection, supporting CD4+ T cells as the mechanism of adaptive immune protection in mice exposed to Trehalose-deficient cryptococcus. As such, in addition to serving as a promising drug target, TPS1 inhibition can both enhance the innate response and adaptive anticryptococcal defenses in a vaccine-like manner, allowing for multi-factorial benefits of targeting TPS1 for therapeutic purposes.

pres156.title: Pharmacokinetics of low-dose flucytosine in patients with HIV-associated cryptococcal meningitis
pres156.abstract: Background/Aims HIV-associated cryptococcal meningitis has a high case-fatality rate. A phase 2 trial (FLOOR) in Uganda demonstrated suboptimal efficacy of low-dose flucytosine (5-FC; 60 mg/kg/day in three divided doses for 10 days) based on cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) fungal clearance. Here, we characterize FLOOR plasma and CSF pharmacokinetics (PK) of 5-FC and examine associations between CSF exposure and clinical outcomes. Methods Plasma was intensively sampled 1, 2, 3-5 or 5-7, and 8 hours post-dose on treatment day 5. CSF was sparsely sampled on days 1, 3, 5-7, and 10-14. Plasma and CSF 5-FC concentrations were quantified using liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry. Initial plasma PK parameters were estimated via non-compartmental analysis (NCA) using Phoenix Winnonlin 8.5.2.4. Fisher’s Exact Test using R 4.5.2 compared week 18 CSF sterility and rehospitalization and/or death between participants above/below the MIC90 threshold. Results Forty-eight participants (22 male, 26 female) contributed sparse sampling; eleven contributed intensive sampling. Median (IQR) steady-state plasma concentrations were Cmin 12.9 mg/L (9.66 - 30.2), Cmax 28.3 mg/L (14.6 - 36.1), and AUCtau 151.42 mg°h/L (91.18 - 250.63). Seven participants had at least one CSF concentration below MIC90 and were less likely to achieve CSF sterility and more likely to experience rehospitalization or death; differences were not statistically significant (50% vs. 61% and 57% vs. 43%, respectively; p>0.6). Conclusions Transient CSF concentrations below MIC90 may be linked to poorer fungal clearance. NCA-derived PK estimates will be tested in a population PK model under development to define population exposures and their relationships to clinical outcomes.

pres157.title: Repurposing Acetylsalicylic Acid for Photodynamic Treatment of Cryptococcal Infections
pres157.abstract: Photodynamic treatment (PDT) is often successful when used against aerobic microbes, given their natural susceptibility to oxidative damage. To this end, the current study aimed to explore the photodynamic action of acetylsalicylic acid (ASA; aspirin, which is commonly used to treat non-infectious ailments), when administered to respiring cryptococcal cells. The treatment of cryptococcal cells, i.e., exposure to 0.5 or 1 mM of ASA in the presence of ultraviolet light (UVL) for 10 min, resulted in a significant (p < 0.05) reduction in the growth of tested cells when compared to non-treated (non-Rx) cells, i.e., no ASA and no UVL. The treated cells were also characterised by diseased mitochondria, which is crucial for the survival of respiring cells, as observed by a significant (p < 0.05) loss of mitochondrial membrane potential (Δ®M) and significant (p < 0.05) accumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) when compared to non-Rx cells. Moreover, the photolytic products of acetylsalicylic acid altered the ultrastructural appearance of treated cells as well as limited the expression levels of the capsular-associated gene, CAP64, when compared to non-Rx cells. The results of the study highlight the potential use of ASA as a photosensitiser that is effective for controlling the growth of cryptococcal cells. Potentially, this treatment can also be used as an adjuvant, to complement and support the usage of current anti-microbial agents.

pres158.title: Quantifying Tolerance and Heteroresistance in Cryptococcus neoformans Using ColonyPy
pres158.abstract: Clinical outcomes in cryptococcal meningitis patients infected with fluconazole-susceptible Cryptococcus neoformans vary widely, including treatment failure, relapse, prolonged therapy, and high mortality. These discrepancies suggest that conventional susceptibility testing may overlook subpopulations that survive above the Minimum Inhibitory Concentration (MIC). Tolerance, defined as slow-growing cells that persist above the MIC, and heteroresistance, in which ~1% of cells grow at concentrations exceeding the MIC. Both are frequently observed with fluconazole (FLC), a fungistatic drug central to cryptococcal meningitis therapy, and are distinct from classical resistance. While tolerance is common in Candida albicans, C. neoformans exhibits both tolerance and heteroresistance. To measure these phenotypes simultaneously, we developed ColonyPy, a Python-based image analysis pipeline that quantifies colony number and size within the zone of inhibition (ZOI) in disk diffusion a ssays and above the MIC in agar dilution plates. Using ColonyPy, we assessed tolerance and heteroresistance in 40 C. neoformans clinical isolates exposed to 25 ¬µg FLC disks. Plates grown on Sabouraud dextrose agar (SDA), yeast peptone dextrose agar (YPD), and M?ºller Hinton agar (MHA) with 2% glucose were photographed at 48, 72, 96, and 120 hours at 37°C, and colonies within the 80% inhibition zone (ZOI80) were analyzed. Our results show that (1) clinical isolates vary widely in tolerance and heteroresistance; (2) these phenotypes differ significantly across media; (3) YPD supports the highest levels of both;and (4) measurements stabilize after 96 hours. Together, these findings demonstrate that tolerance and heteroresistance are distinct, stable phenotypes influenced by nutrient conditions, that our assay can differentiate.

pres159.title: Therapeutic potential and synergistic effects of antifungal and off target drugs against clinical isolates of Cryptococcus laurentii
pres159.abstract:
Cryptococcus laurentii is an emerging opportunistic yeast pathogen associated with unpredictable antifungal susceptibility and biofilm-mediated resistance. Aims:This study investigated the therapeutic potential and synergistic interactions of standard antifungals (fluconazole, amphotericin B) and off-target drugs against clinical isolates of C. laurentii. Methods: Three clinical isolates recovered from blood (n=2) and cerebrospinal fluid (n=1) were confirmed by phenotypic characteristics and PCR amplification of the 18S rRNA gene (~480 bp). Biofilm formation was screened using Congo red agar and quantified via crystal violet assay. Susceptibility testing employed CLSI-guided disc/well diffusion and broth microdilution methods for antifungals and 14 off-target drugs. Minimum biofilm inhibitory concentrations (MBICs) were determined, and synergistic effects were evaluated using checkerboard assays (fractional inhibitory concentration index, FICI) in planktonic cel
 ls and biofilms, supplemented by coverslip biofilm reduction assay. Results: Isolates showed moderate biofilm production (mean OD??? = 0.32 ± 0.008), fluconazole susceptibility (mean MIC = 0.77 µg/mL), and amphotericin B resistance (mean MIC = 4.48 µg/mL). Diclofenac sodium exhibited the strongest off-target activity (mean MIC = 1.32 µg/mL). Checkerboard assays revealed synergy between fluconazole + amphotericin B (FICI = 0.5) and fluconazole + diclofenac sodium (FICI = 0.32) in planktonic cells, with additive effects against biofilms (FICI = 0.9). The fluconazole-diclofenac combination significantly disrupted biofilm matrix. Diclofenac sodium enhances fluconazole efficacy and disrupts biofilms, supporting drug repurposing as an adjuvant strategy for managing biofilm-associated C. laurentii infections. Conclusion: So off-target drugs can be a suitabable alternatives t

pres194.title: ECF Joint Social and Poster Session
